topics.title: Measurement of Pressure
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL408 - FLUID FLOW & PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Pressure

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Pressure

**Pressure (P)** is defined as the normal force exerted per unit area on the surface of a fluid (liquid or gas) by another fluid, body, or external load. In SI units, pressure is expressed in **Pascals (Pa = N/m²)**, with the standard atmospheric pressure defined as **101.325 kPa**.

$$P = \dfrac{F}{A}$$

where $F$ is the perpendicular (normal) force in **Newtons (N)** and $A$ is the area in **square meters (m²)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** Pressure is the intensity of the internal compressive force per unit area acting on a fluid element, transmitted equally in all directions (Pascal's Law). In the context of the **Fluid Flow & Particle Technology Lab (PCBBL408)**, pressure measurement forms the **fundamental prerequisite** for almost all subsequent experiments (Bernoulli's apparatus, orifice/venturi meters, fluidized beds, etc.).

## 1.2 Classification of Pressure

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, the experiment on pressure measurement requires the student to distinguish between the following pressure types:

| Type | Definition | Reference Datum |
|------|------------|-----------------|
| **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$)** | Total pressure measured from absolute zero (perfect vacuum) | $0$ Pa (vacuum) |
| **Atmospheric Pressure ($P_{atm}$)** | Pressure exerted by the weight of the earth's atmosphere | Sea level $\approx$ **101.325 kPa** |
| **Gauge Pressure ($P_g$)** | Pressure measured relative to atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ |
| **Vacuum / Negative Gauge Pressure** | Pressure below atmospheric | $P_{atm}$ |
| **Differential Pressure ($\Delta P$)** | Difference between two system pressures | Variable |

The fundamental relationship is:

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vacuum}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of pressure like the **air inside a bicycle tyre**. If you pump more air, the *gauge* reads higher (say 60 psi), but the *absolute* pressure inside the tyre is what the gauge shows *plus* the atmospheric pressure pushing from outside. The atmospheric pressure is essentially the "background" that is always there.

## 1.3 Working Units in KTU Lab Practice

KTU board problems often use the following conversions (memorize!):

$$1\ \text{atm} = 1.01325\ \text{bar} = 101.325\ \text{kPa} = 760\ \text{mmHg} = 10.33\ \text{m of H}_2\text{O}$$

$$1\ \text{N/m}^2 = 1\ \text{Pa};\ \ 1\ \text{bar} = 10^5\ \text{Pa}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** $1\ \text{atm} \approx 1\ \text{bar}$ (error $<$ 1.3%) – this approximation is **acceptable** in KTU numerical problems unless otherwise stated.

## 1.4 Concept of the Hydrostatic Head

A column of static fluid exerts pressure at its base simply due to its own weight. This is the **governing principle** behind every manometer used in the lab.

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

where $\rho$ = density of fluid (kg/m³), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², and $h$ = height of fluid column (m).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** When you dive into a swimming pool, you feel more pressure on your ears as you go deeper. The deeper you go, the taller the column of water *above* you, and the heavier the weight pushing down — that weight per unit area is the hydrostatic pressure.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Pressure Measuring Devices

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module Experiment 2) categorizes pressure measurement devices into two functional classes:

**A. Manometers (Fluid-Column Devices)** — used for low-to-moderate pressures with high accuracy:
1. **U-Tube Manometer** (simple piezometer type)
2. **Inverted U-Tube Manometer** (for heavy liquids)
3. **Inclined Single-Limb Manometer** (for low pressures, high sensitivity)
4. **Differential U-Tube Manometer**
5. **Micromanometer (well-type)**

**B. Mechanical / Elastic-Element Gauges** — used for direct local reading:
1. **Bourdon Tube Gauge** (most common)
2. **Diaphragm Gauge**
3. **Bellows Gauge**
4. **Dead-Weight Pressure Gauge Tester** (primary standard for calibration)

## 2.2 Operating Principle of a U-Tube Manometer

A U-tube is filled with a manometric fluid (typically mercury, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600\ \text{kg/m}^3$, or water/CCl₄). Two system points (A and B) at unknown pressure are connected to the two limbs. The manometer reading $R$ is the difference in heights of the manometric fluid in the two limbs.

**Assumptions (KTU Board):**
- Manometer tubes are of uniform cross-section.
- Fluid in connecting tubes is the same as the line fluid (or specified).
- Manometric fluid is immiscible with the line fluid.
- Temperature is constant (densities are constant).
- No capillary depression effects.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** for KTU board problems on this experiment. Every expression here has appeared in past KTU university examinations.

| # | Instrument / Case | Governing Equation | Output |
|---|-------------------|--------------------|--------|
| 1 | Simple piezometer (open to atmosphere) | $P_A = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Gauge pressure at A |
| 2 | U-tube manometer (A above manometric fluid in limb 1) | $P_A + \rho_1 g h_1 - \rho_m g R = P_B$ | $P_A - P_B$ |
| 3 | Inverted U-tube (light fluid in manometer) | $P_A - \rho_1 g h_1 + \rho_m g R = P_B$ | $P_A - P_B$ |
| 4 | Inclined manometer (angle $\theta$) | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g L \sin\theta$ | Differential $\Delta P$ |
| 5 | Differential manometer with two fluids | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g \cdot h$ | $\Delta P$ |
| 6 | Bourdon gauge (C-shaped tube) | $P = \dfrac{E \cdot e^2 \cdot \Delta\theta}{1 - \nu^2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R_t^3}$ | Deflection angle |
| 7 | Dead-Weight Tester | $P = \dfrac{M \cdot g}{A_p}$ | Calibration reference |
| 8 | Hydrostatic relation | $P = \rho g h$ | Static fluid |
| 9 | Absolute / Gauge conversion | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Type conversion |
| 10 | Multi-fluid manometer | $P_A - P_B = \sum_{i} \rho_i g h_i - \rho_m g R$ | Complex cases |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation conventions used above:**
> - $\rho_m$ = density of manometric fluid (kg/m³)
> - $\rho_f$ or $\rho_1$ = density of flowing/line fluid (kg/m³)
> - $R$ = manometer reading (m), $L$ = inclined length (m)
> - $h$ = vertical height (m)
> - $M$ = dead weight mass (kg)
> - $A_p$ = plunger area (m²)

## 2.4 Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauge — Working Principle

The **Bourdon tube** is a curved, flattened, C-shaped (or helical, or spiral) metal tube sealed at one end. The open end is connected to the system whose pressure is to be measured. When system pressure enters the tube, it tends to **straighten** the curved tube. This small straightening motion is mechanically amplified by a **link–lever–gear sector–pinion** mechanism, which rotates the pointer on a calibrated dial.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world usage:** Bourdon gauges are the standard pressure indicators on every LPG cylinder regulator, every steam boiler, and every compressed air line in industry. They are rugged, cheap, and have a typical accuracy of $\pm 1\%$ of full-scale range.

**Components of a Bourdon gauge:**
1. **Bourdon tube** (the sensing element — Phos bronze, beryllium copper, or stainless steel)
2. **Socket / connection** (connects to system)
3. **Link mechanism** (amplifies displacement)
4. **Sector and pinion gear** (converts linear to rotary motion)
5. **Pointer and dial** (calibrated scale)
6. **Hair spring** (eliminates backlash)

## 2.5 Dead-Weight Pressure Gauge Tester (Primary Standard)

This is the **fundamental reference instrument** for calibrating Bourdon gauges in the lab. The principle is the direct application of force per unit area:

$$P = \dfrac{W_{total}}{A_{piston}} = \dfrac{M \cdot g}{A_p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** A dead-weight tester is the only pressure instrument whose accuracy is *derived directly from base SI units* (mass, length, time). Hence it is called a **primary standard**. All other gauges (Bourdon, diaphragm, transducers) are *secondary* instruments that must be calibrated against a primary standard.

## 2.6 Inclined Manometer — Sensitivity Analysis

For a standard U-tube, a small pressure difference of $1\ \text{mm H}_2\text{O}$ gives only $1\ \text{mm}$ of reading. By inclining one limb at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal, the *vertical* deflection is $L \sin\theta$ for an *inclined length* $L$, giving a **magnification factor** of:

$$\text{Magnification} = \dfrac{1}{\sin\theta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Inclined manometers are preferred for *low pressure* measurements (draft, small $\Delta P$ in ducts). A $30°$ inclination gives $\sim 2\times$ sensitivity, while a $10°$ inclination gives nearly $6\times$ sensitivity. However, very shallow angles cause meniscus instability.

## 2.7 Engineering Significance

Pressure measurement is the **backbone of all process industries**:
- Chemical reactors require precise pressure control for safety and yield.
- Hydraulic and pneumatic systems depend on accurate pressure readouts.
- Flow meters (orifice, venturi) inherently use *differential* pressure measurement.
- Steam boiler operation is governed by pressure (and temperature) limits.
- Particle technology equipment (cyclones, bag filters) uses $\Delta P$ as the controlling parameter.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Simple U-Tube Manometer Equation

**Problem Setup:** Two pressure taps A and B in a horizontal pipe carry the same line fluid of density $\rho_f$. A U-tube containing manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$ ($\rho_m > \rho_f$) connects them. The manometric fluid in limb A is depressed by an amount $x$ below the original level; consequently, it rises by an amount $x$ in limb B (by volume conservation, since the tube is uniform). The total reading is $R = 2x$.

**Goal:** Derive the relationship between $P_A$, $P_B$, and the manometer reading $R$.

**Step 1 — Identify reference levels.**

We choose the lower meniscus (left limb) as the reference isobaric level. Let point 1 be at the left meniscus, and point 2 be at the right meniscus, at the same elevation.

**Step 2 — Apply the hydrostatic equation from A to point 1.**

Going downward from A through the line fluid adds pressure; reaching the left meniscus (point 1) we have:

$$P_1 = P_A + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1$$

where $h_1$ is the vertical distance from tap A down to the left meniscus.

**Step 3 — Cross the manometric fluid from point 1 to point 2.**

Points 1 and 2 are at the same elevation. The fluid between them is the manometric fluid (density $\rho_m$). Since the manometric fluid rises in the right limb by $R$ above the left meniscus, point 2 is at elevation $+R$ above point 1. Therefore, traversing *up* from 1 to 2 *subtracts* hydrostatic head:

$$P_2 = P_1 - \rho_m \cdot g \cdot R$$

**Step 4 — Apply hydrostatic equation from point 2 up to B.**

Going *upward* through the line fluid (density $\rho_f$) by a vertical distance $h_2$:

$$P_B = P_2 - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_2$$

**Step 5 — Combine the equations.**

Substitute $P_2$ from Step 3 into Step 4:

$$P_B = P_1 - \rho_m \cdot g \cdot R - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_2$$

Substitute $P_1$ from Step 2:

$$P_B = P_A + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1 - \rho_m \cdot g \cdot R - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_2$$

Rearrange:

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot R - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h_1 - h_2)$$

**Step 6 — Final simplified form.**

The quantity $(h_1 - h_2)$ is the elevation difference between the two pressure taps. If taps A and B are at the **same elevation** (a common KTU assumption for horizontal pipes), then $h_1 = h_2$ and the second term vanishes. The classical U-tube equation is recovered:

$$\boxed{\,P_A - P_B = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot R\,}$$

**Step 7 — Special Case (taps not at same elevation).**

If the elevation difference is $\Delta z = z_A - z_B$ and the line fluid is at rest, hydrostatics across the line fluid gives $\rho_f g \Delta z$. Then:

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot R + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \Delta z$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** In the KTU lab, the Bernoulli's apparatus always has taps at the *same elevation* on a horizontal section, so the simpler form applies. In inclined or vertical sections, the elevation correction is mandatory — forgetting it is the **#1 reason students lose marks**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inclined Single-Limb Manometer

**Setup:** A well-type reservoir of large cross-section $A_w$ is connected to a tube of smaller cross-section $A_t$ inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. The line fluid (density $\rho_f$) is on top, and the manometric fluid (density $\rho_m$) is in the inclined tube.

**Step 1 — Apply the manometric equation between the two system points.**

The general form gives:

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h$$

where $h$ is the **vertical** rise of the manometric fluid in the inclined tube.

**Step 2 — Relate $h$ to the inclined length $L$.**

By trigonometry:

$$h = L \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot L \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Express as a column of the line fluid (alternative form).**

Often the result is reported in mm of the line fluid:

$$\Delta P \ (\text{in mm H}_2\text{O}) = L \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_f} - 1\right)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Multi-Fluid (Compound) Manometer

**Setup:** A complex system has several layers of fluid: line fluid, separator fluid, manometric fluid, and so on.

**Governing principle:** The pressure change across any column of fluid is $\pm \rho g \Delta z$, with sign **+** going *down* and **−** going *up*.

**Algorithm (KTU Board Trick):**
1. Start at the first point (A) at the top.
2. Walk down column by column — add $\rho g h$ for *descending* limbs.
3. Walk up — subtract $\rho g h$ for *ascending* limbs.
4. At the manometric fluid interface in the U-bend, the legs are at the same elevation, so no jump in pressure.
5. Continue until point B is reached. Equate.

**General form:**

$$P_A - P_B = \sum_{\text{descending}} \rho_i g h_i - \sum_{\text{ascending}} \rho_i g h_i - \rho_m g R$$

---

## 3.4 KTU Numerical Problem — Solved Model

**Problem (Sample, similar to KTU Dec 2023 style):**

A U-tube manometer containing mercury ($\rho_{Hg} = 13600\ \text{kg/m}^3$) is connected to two points A and B in a water line ($\rho_w = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$). The mercury level in the limb connected to A is 8 cm lower than the mercury level in the limb connected to B. Point A is 12 cm above the lower mercury meniscus, and point B is 16 cm above the lower mercury meniscus. Calculate the pressure difference $(P_A - P_B)$ in kPa. Take $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\rho_m = 13600\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_f = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $R = 8\ \text{cm} = 0.08\ \text{m}$ (mercury reading)
- $h_1 = 12\ \text{cm} = 0.12\ \text{m}$ (A is above lower meniscus)
- $h_2 = 16\ \text{cm} = 0.16\ \text{m}$ (B is above lower meniscus)
- $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Apply the general U-tube manometer equation.**

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_m g R - \rho_f g (h_1 - h_2)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$P_A - P_B = (13600)(9.81)(0.08) - (1000)(9.81)(0.12 - 0.16)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term.**

$$\rho_m g R = 13600 \times 9.81 \times 0.08 = 10674.24\ \text{Pa}$$

$$\rho_f g (h_1 - h_2) = 1000 \times 9.81 \times (-0.04) = -392.4\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract.**

$$P_A - P_B = 10674.24 - (-392.4) = 10674.24 + 392.4 = 11066.64\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kPa.**

$$P_A - P_B = \dfrac{11066.64}{1000} = 11.07\ \text{kPa}$$

**Answer:** $P_A - P_B \approx 11.07\ \text{kPa}$ (Point A is at higher pressure).

---

## 3.5 KTU Numerical Problem — Inclined Manometer

**Problem (KTU University Exam - May 2024 style):**

An inclined manometer using a fluid of specific gravity 0.85 is connected across an orifice meter. The inclined limb makes an angle of $30°$ with the horizontal. The inclined length between the menisci is $L = 25\ \text{cm}$. The flowing fluid is air ($\rho \approx 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$). Find the differential pressure in Pa.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\rho_m = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_f = 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\theta = 30°$
- $L = 0.25\ \text{m}$

**Apply the inclined manometer equation:**

$$\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot L \cdot \sin\theta$$

$$\Delta P = (850 - 1.2) \times 9.81 \times 0.25 \times \sin 30°$$

$$\Delta P = 848.8 \times 9.81 \times 0.25 \times 0.5$$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$848.8 \times 9.81 = 8326.728$$

$$8326.728 \times 0.25 = 2081.682$$

$$2081.682 \times 0.5 = 1040.841\ \text{Pa}$$

**Answer:** $\Delta P \approx 1040.84\ \text{Pa} \approx 1.041\ \text{kPa}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pressure Measurement System Hierarchy

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of pressure measurement devices as classified under the KTU 2024 syllabus. This is the **concept map** the KTU board examiner expects you to be able to draw during the viva and Part A questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pressure1[Pressure to be measured]
    pressure2[Source System - Pipe Vessel Tank]
    pressure3[Unknown pressure signal]

    pressure1 --> pressure2 --> pressure3

    pressure3 --> dev1[Pressure Measuring Devices]
    
    dev1 --> class1[Fluid Column Manometers]
    dev1 --> class2[Elastic Element Gauges]
    dev1 --> class3[Electrical Transducers]

    class1 --> m1[U-Tube Manometer]
    class1 --> m2[Inverted U-Tube]
    class1 --> m3[Inclined Manometer]
    class1 --> m4[Well Type Micromanometer]
    class1 --> m5[Differential Manometer]

    class2 --> e1[Bourdon Tube Gauge]
    class2 --> e2[Diaphragm Gauge]
    class2 --> e3[Bellows Gauge]
    class2 --> e4[Bourdon Tube Calibrator]

    class3 --> t1[Strain Gauge Type]
    class3 --> t2[Capacitive Type]
    class3 --> t3[Piezoelectric Type]
    class3 --> t4[LVDT Type]

    m1 --> formula1[Equation P difference equals rho m g R]
    m2 --> formula2[Equation P difference equals rho f g h minus rho m g R]
    m3 --> formula3[Equation P difference equals rho m minus rho f times g L sine theta]
    
    e1 --> principle1[Curved tube straightens under pressure]
    e4 --> principle2[Equation P equals M g divided by A]
    
    t1 --> output1[Millivolt signal]
    t2 --> output2[Capacitance change]
    t3 --> output3[Charge output]
    t4 --> output4[Voltage signal]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** Pressure is generated in a source (pipe, vessel, tank). It is sensed by one of three families of devices — fluid-column manometers, elastic-element gauges, or electrical transducers. Each family has its own operating principle and governing equation, which the student must know for the **viva voce**.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Working of a Bourdon Gauge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inlet1[System Pressure Inlet]
    socket1[Socket Connection]
    tube1[C-Shaped Bourdon Tube]
    tip1[Sealed Tip of Tube]
    link1[Link Mechanism]
    lever1[Lever Arm]
    sector1[Sector Gear]
    pinion1[Pinion Gear]
    pointer1[Pointer on Dial]
    dial1[Calibrated Scale]
    hair1[Hair Spring]

    inlet1 --> socket1
    socket1 --> tube1
    tube1 -->|Internal pressure pushes outwards| tip1
    tip1 -->|Tip straightens slightly| link1
    link1 --> lever1
    lever1 --> sector1
    sector1 --> pinion1
    pinion1 --> pointer1
    pointer1 --> dial1
    sector1 -.->|Backlash elimination| hair1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Summary for Viva:** When pressurized fluid enters the Bourdon tube, the C-shape tries to straighten (because internal pressure creates a tensile hoop stress in the curved wall). The sealed tip displaces by a small amount (~1–2 mm). This is amplified by the link-lever, then converted to rotary motion by the sector-pinion pair, rotating the pointer over the calibrated scale. The hair spring takes up gear backlash.

## 4.3 Schematic — Dead Weight Pressure Gauge Tester

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    base1[Heavy Base Disc]
    cyl1[Precision Ground Cylinder]
    pis1[Tightly Fitted Piston]
    weight1[Known Dead Weights]
    plat1[Weight Platform]
    oil1[High Grade Hydraulic Oil]
    port1[Connection to Test Gauge]
    test1[Bourdon Gauge under test]

    pis1 -->|Sits on| cyl1
    cyl1 -->|Bolted to| base1
    weight1 -->|Stacked on| plat1
    plat1 -->|Rests on top of| pis1
    oil1 -->|Fills cylinder and connects via| port1
    port1 --> test1

    test1 -.->|Pointer reading compared with| weight1
```

**Working:** The dead weight tester applies a known pressure $P = Mg/A_p$ to the gauge under test by stacking calibrated weights on the piston. The test gauge reading is compared to this known pressure. Any deviation is the gauge's error.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between absolute pressure, gauge pressure, and vacuum pressure. Establish the mathematical relationship among them.**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Definition | Reference |
|------|------------|-----------|
| Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$) | Total pressure measured from perfect vacuum (zero reference) | Vacuum |
| Gauge Pressure ($P_g$) | Pressure measured above atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ |
| Vacuum Pressure ($P_v$) | Pressure measured below atmospheric (negative gauge) | $P_{atm}$ |

**Mathematical relationships:**

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vacuum}$$

$$P_{gauge} = - P_{vacuum}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table** — it is the most frequently asked Part A question in KTU labs. The board expects the relationship *with* the correct sign convention.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State Pascal's law. How is it used in the working of a hydraulic press and a dead-weight pressure gauge tester?**

**Model Answer:**

**Pascal's Law:** *The pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surfaces of the container.*

**Application 1 — Hydraulic Press:**
A small force $F_1$ applied on a piston of area $A_1$ creates pressure $P = F_1/A_1$. This pressure is transmitted through the hydraulic oil to a larger piston of area $A_2$, producing a magnified force $F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2/A_1)$. Hence mechanical advantage is gained.

**Application 2 — Dead-Weight Pressure Gauge Tester:**
A known weight $M$ on a piston of area $A_p$ creates a known pressure $P = Mg/A_p$ in the hydraulic oil beneath. This calibrated pressure is transmitted to the test gauge connected at the outlet. Hence the pressure can be calculated from base SI units.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length ESE Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B carries 14 marks each and gives **internal choice** between two questions from the same module. The following two questions are mutually exclusive, full-14-mark options.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — U-Tube Manometer with Multi-Fluid System

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]`

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, derive the equation for the differential pressure $(P_A - P_B)$ indicated by a U-tube manometer when the two pressure taps are at different elevations and the manometric fluid is heavier than the line fluid. State all assumptions clearly.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A U-tube manometer with mercury ($\rho_{Hg} = 13600\ \text{kg/m}^3$) connects two points A and B in an oil line ($\rho_{oil} = 850\ \text{kg/m}^3$). The elevation of point A is 0.5 m above point B. The manometer reading is $R = 25\ \text{cm}$, with the mercury higher in the limb connected to A. Calculate:
1. The pressure difference $P_A - P_B$ in kPa.
2. The equivalent head in meters of oil.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (covered in Section 3.1 of these notes):**

The general U-tube manometer equation is:

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot R + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \Delta z$$

where $\Delta z = z_A - z_B$ is the elevation difference between taps.

**Assumptions:**
- Tubes are vertical and of uniform cross-section.
- Manometric and line fluids are immiscible.
- Temperature is uniform; densities are constant.
- No capillary depression.
- Acceleration effects (vibration) are negligible.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:**
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13600\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_{oil} = 850\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\Delta z = z_A - z_B = 0.5\ \text{m}$
- $R = 0.25\ \text{m}$
- $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
- Mercury higher in limb A means: the manometric reading is depressed on side B — so $P_A$ is the higher pressure.

**Sub-step 1:** Compute $(\rho_m - \rho_f) g R$

$$(13600 - 850) \times 9.81 \times 0.25 = 12750 \times 9.81 \times 0.25$$

$$= 12750 \times 2.4525 = 31269.375\ \text{Pa}$$

**Sub-step 2:** Compute $\rho_f g \Delta z$

$$850 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 = 850 \times 4.905 = 4169.25\ \text{Pa}$$

**Sub-step 3:** Sum the two contributions

$$P_A - P_B = 31269.375 + 4169.25 = 35438.625\ \text{Pa} \approx 35.44\ \text{kPa}$$

**Sub-step 4:** Convert to equivalent head of oil

$$h_{oil} = \dfrac{P_A - P_B}{\rho_{oil} \cdot g} = \dfrac{35438.625}{850 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{35438.625}{8338.5} = 4.25\ \text{m of oil}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $P_A - P_B = 35.44\ \text{kPa}$
- $h_{oil} = 4.25\ \text{m}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Bourdon Gauge and Dead-Weight Calibration

> `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]`

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a C-type Bourdon tube pressure gauge. Discuss the function of each component.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A dead-weight pressure gauge tester is used to calibrate a Bourdon gauge. The piston diameter is 10 mm, and the loaded weights total 5.5 kg. Calculate:
1. The pressure generated, in kPa.
2. The number of additional 1-kg weights that must be added to produce a pressure of 1500 kPa.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (covered in Section 2.4 of these notes):**

**Key components** (students should list and explain each):
1. **Bourdon tube** — C-shaped, flattened cross-section, sealed at tip; sensing element.
2. **Socket** — connects gauge to system under test.
3. **Link** — connects tube tip to lever.
4. **Lever** — amplifies tip displacement.
5. **Sector and pinion** — converts linear displacement to rotary motion of pointer.
6. **Pointer and dial** — indicates the pressure on a calibrated scale.
7. **Hair spring** — eliminates gear backlash.
8. **Casing** — protects internal mechanism.

**Working:** When system pressure enters the C-tube, the curved tube tends to straighten (because the outer wall of the curve is stretched more than the inner wall). The sealed tip moves by a small amount. This motion is transmitted to the link, amplified by the lever, and then rotates the sector gear. The pinion mounted on the pointer shaft rotates, sweeping the pointer across the dial.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:**
- $D_p = 10\ \text{mm} = 0.01\ \text{m}$, so $A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_p^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.01)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2$
- $M = 5.5\ \text{kg}$
- $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$

**Sub-step 1:** Calculate the pressure

$$P = \dfrac{M \cdot g}{A_p} = \dfrac{5.5 \times 9.81}{7.854 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{53.955}{7.854 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$P = 687000\ \text{Pa} \approx 687\ \text{kPa}$$

**Sub-step 2:** Calculate the total mass required for 1500 kPa

$$M_{total} = \dfrac{P_{target} \cdot A_p}{g} = \dfrac{1500 \times 10^3 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5}}{9.81}$$

$$M_{total} = \dfrac{1500 \times 0.07854}{9.81} = \dfrac{117.81}{9.81} = 12.01\ \text{kg}$$

**Sub-step 3:** Calculate additional 1-kg weights required

$$n = M_{total} - M_{existing} = 12.01 - 5.5 = 6.51$$

Since weights come as whole 1-kg units, we need **7 additional 1-kg weights** to *reach or exceed* 1500 kPa.

> (With 7 weights, total mass = 12.5 kg, $P = 12.5 \times 9.81 / 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \approx 1561.4\ \text{kPa}$.)

**Final Answers:**
- Initial pressure = **687 kPa**
- Additional 1-kg weights needed = **7**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks in this topic:**
> 
> 1. **Sign convention errors** — In a U-tube, students often forget that mercury *higher* on side A means $P_A > P_B$ (not the reverse). Always remember: the manometric fluid is **pushed toward the lower pressure** side.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the elevation correction** — When the two pressure taps are at different elevations, the term $\rho_f g \Delta z$ must be added. KTU examiners routinely set this as the "twist" in the problem. **Marks lost: 2–3 per question.**
> 
> 3. **Unit conversion mistakes** — Mixing cm and m, or bar and Pa, leads to a 100× or 1000× error. Always convert to SI **before** substituting.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the $\sin\theta$ factor** in inclined manometer problems — a classic error.
> 
> 5. **Confusing Bourdon tube straightening with bending** — It is the *curved C-shape* that *straightens*, not the other way around.
> 
> 6. **In dead-weight calculations**, forgetting the piston area: the weight alone does not give pressure — area is essential.
> 
> 7. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations — the KTU board deducts **1 full mark** if assumptions (isothermal, immiscible, uniform tube) are not listed.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module Experiment 2 — Measurement of Pressure:**

- **Pressure definition:** $P = F/A$ (Pa = N/m²). **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg = 10.33 m H₂O**.

- **Pressure types:** Absolute, Gauge, Vacuum, Differential. Master $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_g$.

- **Hydrostatic equation:** $P = \rho g h$ — the **master equation** underlying all manometers.

- **U-Tube Manometer:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g R + \rho_f g \Delta z$. If taps are at the same elevation, the second term drops out.

- **Inclined Manometer:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g L \sin\theta$. Sensitivity $\propto 1/\sin\theta$.

- **Inverted U-Tube:** Used for *light* manometric fluids and *heavy* line fluids; sign of $(\rho_m - \rho_f)$ reverses.

- **Bourdon Gauge:** Curved C-tube straightens under pressure. Components: tube, link, lever, sector, pinion, pointer, hair spring.

- **Dead-Weight Tester:** $P = Mg/A_p$. This is the **primary standard**; Bourdon is the **secondary instrument**.

- **Algorithm for any manometer problem:** Start at A. Go down — **add** $\rho g h$. Go up — **subtract** $\rho g h$. Equate at B.

- **Conversion factors to memorize:** 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa, 1 atm $\approx$ 1.01325 bar, 1 mmHg = 133.32 Pa, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$.

- **Mercury density:** 13600 kg/m³ (always). Water density: 1000 kg/m³ (always).

- **Always list assumptions** in derivation-type questions: isothermal, immiscible, uniform cross-section, no capillary effects.

- **Sign convention:** Mercury rises on the **low pressure** side. This is your key diagnostic check during numericals.

- **Viva essentials:** Be ready to explain *why* a Bourdon tube straightens (hoop stress / non-uniform curvature), and *why* a dead-weight tester is a primary standard (based on SI base units).

---

> **End of KTU-Premier Notes | PCBBL408 — Module Experiment 2: Measurement of Pressure**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Viscometers for Measurement of Viscosity of Process Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL408 - FLUID FLOW & PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Use of Viscometers for Measurement of Viscosity of Process Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — USE OF VISCOMETERS FOR MEASUREMENT OF VISCOSITY OF PROCESS FLUIDS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Viscosity (μ)** is the internal resistance offered by a fluid layer to the relative motion of an adjacent layer under an applied shear stress. It is a fundamental transport property governing momentum transfer in process fluids.

According to **Newton's Law of Viscosity**, for a one-dimensional laminar shearing flow, the shear stress $\tau$ acting between adjacent fluid layers is directly proportional to the local velocity gradient (shear rate) $\dfrac{dv}{dy}$ perpendicular to the direction of flow.

$$\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress, expressed in **Pa (N/m²)**
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity, expressed in **Pa·s** or **kg/(m·s)**
- $\dfrac{dv}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient or shear rate, expressed in **s⁻¹**

A fluid obeying this linear relationship is classified as a **Newtonian Fluid**. The proportionality constant $\mu$ is independent of the applied shear rate and is purely a function of temperature and pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** The ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density.
> $$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$
> Units: **m²/s** or **Stokes (St)** where 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s = 1 cm²/s. The CGS sub-unit **centistokes (cSt)** = 10⁻⁶ m²/s is most commonly reported in industry.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine **two parallel panes of glass** with a thick layer of honey sandwiched in between. When you try to slide the top pane sideways, the honey right next to the top pane moves with the pane, while the honey at the bottom remains stuck to the stationary bottom pane. The intermediate layers slide progressively slower. The "stickiness" you feel when pushing the top pane is the **viscosity** of honey.

- **High viscosity** (honey, tar, heavy oil) → Strong resistance → Feels thick and gooey.
- **Low viscosity** (water, acetone, gasoline) → Weak resistance → Flows freely.

A **viscometer** is essentially a device that applies a controlled shear stress or controlled shear rate to a known volume of fluid, and then **measures the resulting deformation (flow rate)** or **measures the resisting force** to compute $\mu$ or $\nu$.

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Significance for B.Tech Chemical Engineers:** Viscosity dictates pump power requirements, pipe diameter sizing, heat transfer coefficients in shell-and-tube exchangers, mixing impeller selection in agitated reactors, atomization quality in spray dryers, and even the mouthfeel/texture of pharmaceutical syrups and cosmetic lotions.

### 1.3 Classification of Viscometers (KTU Board Classification Table)

| Family | Working Principle | Typical Instruments | Suitable Fluid Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Capillary (Efflux)** | Time taken for fixed volume to flow through a calibrated orifice/capillary | Ostwald, Ubbelohde, Cannon-Fenske, Pinkevitch | Newtonian, low-to-moderate viscosity (liquids) |
| **Falling Body** | Terminal settling velocity of a calibrated sphere | Höppler (falling ball), Falling needle | Transparent Newtonian liquids |
| **Rotational** | Torque required to rotate a spindle at known rpm in the fluid | Brookfield, concentric cylinder (Searle / Couette), Cone-and-plate | Both Newtonian and Non-Newtonian |
| **Efflux Cup (Empirical)** | Time (in seconds) for a fixed volume to drain through a calibrated orifice | Redwood, Saybolt, Ford Cup, Zahn Cup | Industrial oils, paints, varnishes |
| **Vibrational / Acoustic** | Damping of a vibrating probe | Process viscometers (online) | In-line process monitoring |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Behaviour (Pre-lab Theory)

For any fluid the apparent viscosity is defined as:

$$\mu_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

Where $\dot{\gamma}$ is the shear rate in s⁻¹.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **Newtonian fluid**, the plot of $\tau$ versus $\dot{\gamma}$ is a straight line passing through the origin. The slope = $\mu$, which is **constant** with respect to shear rate.

Non-Newtonian fluids exhibit shear-rate dependent viscosity:
- **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning):** Viscosity drops with increasing shear (e.g., polymer melts, ketchup, blood).
- **Dilatant (shear-thickening):** Viscosity rises with shear (e.g., starch suspensions, wet sand).
- **Bingham plastic:** Behaves as a solid until yield stress $\tau_y$ is exceeded (e.g., toothpaste, drilling mud).
- **Thixotropic / Rheopexic:** Time-dependent viscosity at constant shear.

Rotational viscometers (Brookfield) are essential in lab courses precisely because they can measure these non-Newtonian profiles.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam ready** for KTU 2024 Scheme PCBBL408 lab examination.

| # | Instrument / Concept | Governing Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{dv}{dy}$ | $\tau$ = Pa; $\mu$ = Pa·s; $dv/dy$ = s⁻¹ |
| 2 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\nu$ = m²/s; $\rho$ = kg/m³ |
| 3 | Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (Capillary) | $\mu = \dfrac{\pi \, r^4 \, \Delta P \, t}{8 \, L \, V}$ | $r$ = m; $\Delta P$ = Pa; $t$ = s; $L$ = m; $V$ = m³ |
| 4 | Ostwald Relative Viscosity | $\mu_1 = \mu_w \cdot \dfrac{\rho_1 \, t_1}{\rho_w \, t_w}$ | Subscript $w$ = water; subscript $1$ = test liquid |
| 5 | Saybolt Universal Viscosity | $\nu_{SUS} = 0.226 \, t - \dfrac{195}{t}$ (for $t \le 100$ s) | $\nu$ in **cSt**; $t$ in **seconds** |
| 6 | Redwood No. 1 Viscosity | $\nu = 0.0026 \, t - \dfrac{1.72}{t}$ (kinematic, cSt) | $t$ in **seconds** |
| 7 | Brookfield Rotational | $\mu = \dfrac{T}{K_{sp} \cdot \omega}$ | $T$ = torque (N·m); $K_{sp}$ = spindle constant; $\omega$ = rad/s |
| 8 | Falling Ball (Höppler / Stokes) | $\mu = \dfrac{(\rho_s - \rho_f) \, g \, d^2 \, t}{18 \, L}$ (with correction factors) | $\rho_s, \rho_f$ = kg/m³; $d$ = m; $t$ = s; $L$ = m |
| 9 | Specific Gravity Conversion | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{liquid}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Shear Stress (Rotational) | $\tau = \dfrac{T}{2\pi R_s^2 L_s}$ | Concentric-cylinder geometry |
| 11 | Shear Rate (Rotational) | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{2 \omega R_o^2 R_i^2}{(R_o^2 - R_i^2) x}$ | For narrow-gap approximation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall Avoidance:** In KTU examinations, examiners always cross-check whether you have used the **correct unit system** in Hagen-Poiseuille. SI units (Pa·s) are mandatory in the 2024 scheme. Conversion: **1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s = 1 mPa·s**.

### 2.3 Engineering Significance & Real-World Utility

- **Petroleum Refining:** Kinematic viscosity of crude and lube oils (40 °C and 100 °C) is the single most important physical specification. Redwood and Saybolt viscometers are still the industry standard.
- **Polymer Processing:** Melt flow index (MFI) and capillary rheometry give the viscosity curve needed for extrusion die design.
- **Pharmaceuticals & Cosmetics:** Brookfield viscometers at 25 °C certify syrups, ointments, and creams (USP <911>).
- **Food Industry:** Falling-ball and rotational viscometers control consistency of chocolate, sauces, and dairy products.
- **Process Control:** In-line vibrating viscometers feed back to DCS for real-time quality control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Hagen-Poiseuille Equation (For Capillary Viscometer Analysis)

Consider a Newtonian fluid flowing **laminarly** through a horizontal cylindrical capillary of radius $r$ and length $L$, under a steady pressure drop $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$.

**Step 1 — Force Balance on a fluid cylinder of radius $x$:**

The pressure force acting on the cross-section $\pi x^2$ must be balanced by the viscous shear force acting on the lateral cylindrical surface $2 \pi x L$.

$$(P_1 - P_2) \pi x^2 = -\tau (2 \pi x L)$$

**Step 2 — Express shear stress from Newton's Law:**

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{dv}{dx}$$

The negative sign indicates that velocity decreases as $x$ (radial position) increases.

**Step 3 — Substituting and simplifying:**

$$(P_1 - P_2) \pi x^2 = -\mu \frac{dv}{dx} (2 \pi x L)$$

Divide both sides by $\pi x$:

$$(P_1 - P_2) x = -2 \mu L \frac{dv}{dx}$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate with boundary conditions:**

At $x = r$ (wall), $v = 0$ (no-slip condition). At $x = 0$ (centerline), $v = v_{max}$.

$$\int_{v_{max}}^{v} dv = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{0}^{x} x \, dx$$

$$v = v_{max} - \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} x^2$$

**Step 5 — Apply wall no-slip condition to find maximum velocity:**

At $x = r$, $v = 0$:

$$0 = v_{max} - \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} r^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \, r^2}{4 \mu L}$$

**Step 6 — Volumetric flow rate by integrating across the cross-section:**

$$Q = \int_{0}^{r} v(x) \cdot 2\pi x \, dx$$

$$Q = \int_{0}^{r} \left[\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}(r^2 - x^2)\right] 2\pi x \, dx$$

$$Q = \frac{2 \pi \Delta P}{4 \mu L} \int_{0}^{r} (r^2 x - x^3) \, dx$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[\frac{r^2 x^2}{2} - \frac{x^4}{4}\right]_{0}^{r}$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[\frac{r^4}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4}\right] = \frac{\pi \Delta P r^4}{8 \mu L}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for viscosity:**

$$\mu = \frac{\pi r^4 \Delta P}{8 L Q} = \frac{\pi r^4 \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}$$

where $Q = V/t$ for a fixed discharge volume $V$ collected in time $t$.

> [!TIP]
> This is the **canonical master equation** for all capillary viscometers. For instruments like the Ostwald where $\Delta P$ is provided by gravity ($h \rho g$), this $h$ is held constant, and the time ratio method eliminates the geometric constants.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Ostwald Relative Viscosity Method

For Ostwald and similar capillary viscometers, the driving pressure $\Delta P = h \rho g$ is provided by the hydrostatic head of the liquid itself.

Substituting into the Hagen-Poiseuille equation:

$$\mu = \frac{\pi r^4 h \rho g \, t}{8 L V}$$

Notice that for the **same viscometer**, $\pi$, $r$, $h$, $g$, $L$, $V$ are all constants, giving a viscometer constant $C$:

$$\mu = C \cdot \rho \cdot t$$

**Run the same viscometer with distilled water (reference) at the same temperature:**

$$\mu_w = C \cdot \rho_w \cdot t_w$$

**Divide the two equations to cancel $C$:**

$$\frac{\mu}{\mu_w} = \frac{\rho \, t}{\rho_w \, t_w}$$

**Result — the relative viscosity formula (exam-favourite):**

$$\mu_{liquid} = \mu_w \cdot \frac{\rho_{liquid} \cdot t_{liquid}}{\rho_w \cdot t_w}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This elegant ratio method is the most tested derivation in KTU lab exams. The viscometer geometry and capillary radius completely vanish, removing the largest source of systematic error.

### 3.3 Derivation of Brookfield Viscometer Equation

For a Brookfield-type concentric cylinder (spindle submerged in a large beaker of fluid), the torque $T$ on the rotating spindle is:

$$T = \frac{4 \pi \mu L_s \omega R_s^2 R_c^2}{R_c^2 - R_s^2}$$

where $R_s$ = spindle radius, $R_c$ = cup radius, $L_s$ = effective spindle length, $\omega$ = angular velocity.

Solving for viscosity:

$$\mu = \frac{T (R_c^2 - R_s^2)}{4 \pi \omega L_s R_s^2 R_c^2}$$

The Brookfield instrument consolidates the geometric terms into a **spindle constant $K_{sp}$** supplied by the manufacturer for each spindle-rpm combination. The reading is therefore reported directly as:

$$\mu = \frac{T}{K_{sp} \cdot \omega} \quad \text{(Pa·s)}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Falling-Ball Viscosity (Stokes' Law Application)

A sphere of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_s$ falling through a fluid of density $\rho_f$ reaches terminal velocity $v_t$ when gravitational force = buoyancy + drag.

$$\frac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_s - \rho_f) g = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_f v_t^2}{2}$$

For laminar (creeping) flow, $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re} = \dfrac{24 \mu}{\rho_f v_t d}$.

Substituting and simplifying:

$$\mu = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_f) g d^2 t}{18 L}$$

where $L$ is the distance between timing marks and $t$ is the measured fall time. Wall and finite-fluid corrections (Faxén's law) are applied for narrow tubes.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Newtonian viscosity. With a neat sketch, explain Newton's law of viscosity and state the SI units of dynamic and kinematic viscosity.**

**Model Answer:**

**Newtonian viscosity** is the constant proportionality factor $\mu$ between shear stress and shear rate in a Newtonian fluid, expressed as $\tau = \mu \dfrac{dv}{dy}$.

**Sketch:** Show two parallel plates separated by a distance $dy$; lower plate stationary, upper plate moving at velocity $dv$ under shear stress $\tau$.

**SI Units:**
- Dynamic viscosity $\mu$: **Pa·s** (also accepted: kg·m⁻¹·s⁻¹ or N·s·m⁻²)
- Kinematic viscosity $\nu$: **m²/s** (CGS: Stokes; sub-unit: centistokes, cSt)
- Conversion: **1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s = 1 mPa·s**

---

#### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. Why is the Redwood viscometer classified as an "empirical" viscometer rather than an "absolute" viscometer?**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Internal resistance to flow | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ |
| Units | Pa·s | m²/s |
| Depends on density | No | Yes (inversely via definition) |
| Measured by | Rotational, falling-ball | Capillary, efflux cup |

The **Redwood viscometer** is empirical because its output is the **efflux time in seconds** for 50 mL of oil to pass through a standard orifice. It does **not** directly measure shear stress or shear rate, and the geometry does not satisfy the assumptions of laminar Hagen-Poiseuille flow (hence the empirical correlations involving $t - k/t$ form). Absolute viscometers (e.g., concentric-cylinder rotational viscometer) measure the actual shear stress-shear rate relationship.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Choice Pattern — 14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE (End Semester Evaluation) Module Pattern: Students choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A — Capillary Viscometer (Ostwald)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of the Ostwald viscometer. Explain the principle involved in measuring viscosity.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Ostwald viscometer** is a glass U-tube instrument consisting of:
1. A large **reservoir bulb (A)** in one limb into which the test liquid is pipetted.
2. A **capillary tube (L)** of precise bore beneath bulb A.
3. A **lower receiving bulb (B)** that collects the efflux.
4. Two etched **timing marks (M₁ above, M₂ below)** between which the liquid meniscus passes.
5. An outer jacket connected to a **constant-temperature bath** maintained at the test temperature (typically 25 °C ± 0.1 °C).

**Working Principle:**
- A fixed volume (e.g., 10 mL) of the test liquid is introduced into bulb A.
- The liquid is drawn up by suction until its meniscus rises above mark M₁.
- The liquid is allowed to fall under its own hydrostatic head.
- The **efflux time $t$** for the meniscus to descend from M₁ to M₂ is recorded with a stopwatch.
- The hydrostatic pressure $\Delta P = h \rho g$ drives laminar flow through the capillary.
- Substituting into Hagen-Poiseuille: $\mu = C \cdot \rho \cdot t$.
- Comparing with water under identical conditions: $\mu_{liquid} = \mu_w \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{liquid} \cdot t_{liquid}}{\rho_w \cdot t_w}$.
- The viscosity is thus determined from time measurement alone — no absolute pressure or radius measurement is required.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**In an Ostwald viscometer experiment, the time of flow for water at 25 °C is 50.6 s. For an unknown oil of density 920 kg/m³, the time of flow is 385.4 s. Calculate the dynamic and kinematic viscosity of the oil. Given: $\mu_w$ at 25 °C = 0.8904 cP.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert given data:**
- $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_{oil} = 920$ kg/m³
- $t_w = 50.6$ s, $t_{oil} = 385.4$ s
- $\mu_w = 0.8904 \text{ cP} = 0.8904 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$

**Step 2 — Apply Ostwald relative viscosity formula:**

$$\mu_{oil} = \mu_w \cdot \frac{\rho_{oil} \cdot t_{oil}}{\rho_w \cdot t_w}$$

$$\mu_{oil} = (0.8904 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot \frac{920 \times 385.4}{1000 \times 50.6}$$

$$\mu_{oil} = (0.8904 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot \frac{354568}{50600}$$

$$\mu_{oil} = (0.8904 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 7.0073$$

$$\mu_{oil} = 6.239 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s} = 6.239 \text{ cP}$$

**Step 3 — Compute kinematic viscosity:**

$$\nu_{oil} = \frac{\mu_{oil}}{\rho_{oil}} = \frac{6.239 \times 10^{-3}}{920}$$

$$\nu_{oil} = 6.781 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 6.781 \text{ cSt}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):** Students commonly (i) forget to convert $\mu_w$ from cP to Pa·s before multiplying, leading to a wrong SI answer; (ii) mix up density of water (1000 kg/m³ in SI but 1 g/cc if using cgs); (iii) drop units in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct **½ mark** for missing units. Always write the answer as **$\mu_{oil} = 6.24 \text{ cP}$** with units and **two decimal places**.

---

### **QUESTION B — Rotational Viscometer (Brookfield)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe with a labelled diagram the working of a Brookfield rotational viscometer. List the various spindles supplied and the procedure for selecting a spindle.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Brookfield rotational viscometer** consists of:
1. A **synchronous motor** providing constant rotational speeds (0.3, 0.6, 1.5, 3, 6, 12, 30, 60, 100 rpm).
2. A **torque-sensing beryllium-copper spring** coupled to the motor.
3. A **spindle (spindle 1 to 7)** attached to the lower end of the shaft, immersed in the test fluid.
4. A **guard leg** to prevent flow disturbances.
5. A **dial (0–100 scale)** that indicates spring deflection proportional to viscous drag.
6. A **leveling foot** and **stand** for vertical alignment.

**Working Principle:**
- The spindle rotates at a fixed $\omega$ in the test fluid.
- Viscous drag exerts a torque $T$ on the spindle.
- The spring deflects by an angle $\theta$ proportional to $T$.
- The deflection is read on the dial.
- The viscosity is computed as: $\mu = \theta \times F$, where $F$ is the **multiplier factor** depending on the spindle and rpm combination, read from the standard Brookfield table.

**Spindle Selection Procedure:**
- Spindles are labelled **1 (largest) to 7 (smallest)**.
- For **high-viscosity fluids**, use **smaller spindle numbers (1, 2, 3)**.
- For **low-viscosity fluids**, use **larger spindle numbers (4, 5, 6, 7)**.
- The correct spindle gives a **dial reading between 10 and 100**; if the reading is below 10, switch to a smaller spindle (higher number) or increase rpm.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**The dial reading of a Brookfield viscometer using spindle No. 4 at 60 rpm is 64.5. The corresponding multiplier from the instrument manual is 1.0. Calculate the viscosity in cP. If the same fluid is tested using spindle No. 3 at 12 rpm, the dial reads 22.8 with a multiplier of 50. Comment on the rheological behaviour of the fluid.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Viscosity from first measurement:**

$$\mu_1 = \theta_1 \times F_1 = 64.5 \times 1.0 = 64.5 \text{ cP}$$

**Step 2 — Viscosity from second measurement:**

$$\mu_2 = \theta_2 \times F_2 = 22.8 \times 50 = 1140 \text{ cP}$$

**Step 3 — Compute corresponding shear rates (Brookfield convention):**
- For spindle 4 at 60 rpm: $\dot{\gamma}_1 \approx 26.4$ s⁻¹ (approximate).
- For spindle 3 at 12 rpm: $\dot{\gamma}_2 \approx 2.64$ s⁻¹.

**Step 4 — Comment on rheology:**

The viscosity **decreases dramatically** from 1140 cP at low shear rate (2.64 s⁻¹) to 64.5 cP at high shear rate (26.4 s⁻¹). This **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic) behaviour** is characteristic of polymer solutions, latex paints, and pseudoplastic industrial fluids.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):** KTU evaluators expect **two things** explicitly: (i) final answer in **cP with units**, and (ii) a **clear rheological comment** naming the fluid type — pseudoplastic, dilatant, Newtonian, or Bingham. A calculation with no rheology commentary loses **at least 2 marks**. Also, do not confuse the multiplier's units — Brookfield's $F$ already incorporates everything so that $\theta \times F$ directly gives viscosity in cP.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Viva and Exam**

- [ ] **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{dv}{dy}$. Memorize both dynamic $\mu$ (Pa·s) and kinematic $\nu = \mu/\rho$ (m²/s) units.
- [ ] **Newtonian fluid:** Linear $\tau$-vs-$\dot\gamma$ plot, constant viscosity.
- [ ] **Capillary viscometer formula (master):** $\mu = \dfrac{\pi r^4 \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}$.
- [ ] **Ostwald relative method:** $\mu_{sample} = \mu_w \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{sample} \, t_{sample}}{\rho_w \, t_w}$. No geometry constants needed.
- [ ] **Brookfield equation:** $\mu = \theta \times F$ (in cP). $\theta$ = dial reading (0–100), $F$ = manual multiplier.
- [ ] **Redwood/Saybolt (empirical):** Output is efflux time in seconds; conversions to kinematic viscosity are empirical correlations (e.g., $\nu_{SUS} = 0.226t - 195/t$).
- [ ] **Falling ball:** $\mu = \dfrac{(\rho_s - \rho_f) g d^2 t}{18 L}$, valid only when $Re < 0.1$ (laminar/Stokes regime).
- [ ] **Unit conversions to commit:** 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s; 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s; $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³.
- [ ] **Temperature dependency:** Viscosity of liquids **decreases** exponentially with temperature (Andrade equation); viscosity of gases **increases** with temperature.
- [ ] **Wall slip correction, kinetic energy correction, surface tension correction** may be asked as 2-mark supplementary parts.
- [ ] **Viva trap:** "Can an Ostwald viscometer be used for non-Newtonian fluids?" — Answer: **No**, because the capillary shear rate is not constant along the length.
- [ ] **Common KTU mistakes:** Forgetting to rinse and dry the viscometer between samples (residual water dilutes the test liquid); not waiting for thermal equilibrium; parallax error while reading the meniscus.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Influencing Viscosity of Process Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL408 - FLUID FLOW & PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Influencing Viscosity of Process Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Viscosity ($\mu$)** is the measure of a fluid's internal resistance to deformation (flow) when subjected to an applied shear stress. Mathematically, it is the proportionality constant between shear stress ($\tau$) and the velocity gradient (shear rate, $\dot{\gamma}$) across a fluid layer, as defined by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma} = \mu \cdot \frac{dv}{dy}$$

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of *Process Fluid Mechanics*, viscosity is treated as a **transport property** — alongside thermal conductivity and diffusivity — that quantifies momentum transport at the molecular level. The **SI unit** is **Pa·s (Pascal-second)** or **N·s/m²**, while the **CGS unit** is the **poise (P)**, where 1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP (centipoise).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the lab module, viscosity is *experimentally determined* using capillary tube viscometers (Ostwald / Cannon-Fenske) and rotational viscometers (Brookfield). The factors influencing viscosity directly affect the accuracy of these measurements and the design calculations that follow (e.g., Reynolds number, pressure drop in pipes).

### 1.2 The Key Factors Influencing Viscosity

The viscosity of a process fluid is **NOT a constant property** — it varies significantly based on operational and molecular parameters. The principal factors are:

| # | Factor | Type of Influence |
|---|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Temperature** | Strongest effect — exponential relationship |
| 2 | **Pressure** | Significant for gases & liquids under high P |
| 3 | **Composition / Concentration** | Governed by molecular interactions |
| 4 | **Molecular Structure & Size** | Determines intermolecular forces |
| 5 | **Molecular Weight** | Higher MW → higher viscosity (especially polymers) |
| 6 | **Shear Rate (Non-Newtonian fluids)** | Determines apparent viscosity |
| 7 | **Presence of Additives/Solvents** | Plasticizers, thinners, etc. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Honey vs. Water" Analogy** 🍯💧
>
> Imagine pouring **honey** and **water** from the same container at the same temperature. Honey flows sluggishly — its molecules cling tightly together like commuters packed in a crowded bus (high intermolecular friction). Water flows easily because its molecules glide past one another like ice skaters on a rink.
>
> Now imagine heating both. The honey becomes noticeably more fluid (temperature has weakened its molecular "stickiness"), while water changes very little. This is because **liquid viscosity decreases sharply with temperature, whereas gas viscosity actually increases slightly with temperature** — a counter-intuitive but critical KTU exam point.
>
> Finally, squeeze a tube of toothpaste. It doesn't flow until you apply enough pressure (a *yield stress*), and once flowing, thins down (shear-thinning). That's a **non-Newtonian** behavior — viscosity itself depends on how hard you push.

### 1.4 The Underlying Molecular Picture

At the molecular level, viscosity arises from **momentum exchange between adjacent fluid layers**:

- **In liquids:** Molecules are close together. Viscosity is dominated by **cohesive intermolecular forces** (van der Waals, hydrogen bonding). Higher temperature → more thermal agitation → molecules slip past each other more easily → **viscosity decreases**.
- **In gases:** Molecules are far apart. Viscosity is dominated by **molecular momentum transfer** via collisions. Higher temperature → faster molecules → more momentum transferred per collision → **viscosity increases**.

This dichotomy is one of the **most commonly tested concepts** in KTU University Examinations under this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Factor 1 — Temperature (The Dominant Factor)

#### 2.1.1 For Liquids
The viscosity of liquids **decreases exponentially** with temperature. The two most important empirical/theoretical relations are:

**a) Arrhenius-Type Equation (Andrade's Equation):**

$$\mu = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

Where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential constant (Pa·s)
- $E_a$ = activation energy for viscous flow (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**b) Two-Point Method (Commonly used in KTU lab calculations):**

$$\ln\left(\frac{\mu_1}{\mu_2}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

This form is **board-exam favorite** because viscosity data at two temperatures lets you compute $E_a$ directly.

#### 2.1.2 For Gases
Gas viscosity **increases** with temperature, following **Sutherland's Equation**:

$$\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$$

Where:
- $\mu_0$ = reference viscosity at reference temperature $T_0$ (often 273 K or 298 K)
- $S$ = Sutherland's constant (gas-specific, e.g., **S = 110.4 K for air**)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

A simpler power-law approximation:

$$\mu \propto T^n$$

where $n \approx 0.5$ to $1.0$ for most common gases (e.g., $n = 0.7$ for air).

### 2.2 Factor 2 — Pressure

#### 2.2.1 Effect on Liquids
Liquids are nearly **incompressible**, so pressure has minimal effect at low-to-moderate pressures. However, at very high pressures (e.g., deep-well oil reservoirs, high-pressure polymerization), viscosity **increases sharply** with pressure. An empirical relation used in petroleum engineering:

$$\mu_P = \mu_{P_0} \cdot \exp(\alpha \cdot \Delta P)$$

Where $\alpha$ is the pressure coefficient of viscosity (Pa⁻¹), and $\Delta P$ is the pressure increase from the reference state.

#### 2.2.2 Effect on Gases
For ideal gases, viscosity is **independent of pressure** (a unique property among transport properties). For real gases at high pressures, viscosity increases with pressure. At low pressures (vacuum), the **mean free path** increases, eventually leading to slip flow and rarefied gas dynamics (Knudsen effect).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** "Viscosity of an ideal gas is independent of pressure" — this is a **classic 3-mark question** in KTU exams. Commit this to memory.

### 2.3 Factor 3 — Composition and Concentration

For **solutions, suspensions, and mixtures**, viscosity depends strongly on the **concentration of solute** and the nature of solute-solvent interactions.

- **Dilute suspensions** of rigid spheres (Einstein's Equation):

$$\mu = \mu_0 (1 + 2.5 \phi)$$

Where $\phi$ is the volume fraction of solids and $\mu_0$ is the pure solvent viscosity. Valid only for $\phi < 0.02$.

- **Polymer solutions** often follow the **Huggins equation**:

$$\frac{\mu_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 c$$

Where $\mu_{sp}$ = specific viscosity, $c$ = concentration, $[\eta]$ = intrinsic viscosity, $k_H$ = Huggins constant.

- **Miscible liquid mixtures** (e.g., water-glycerol) are typically non-linear; experimental data correlated by:

$$\ln(\mu_{mix}) = \sum x_i \ln(\mu_i)$$

(Arrhenius mixing rule, a first approximation).

### 2.4 Factor 4 — Molecular Weight and Chain Length

For **polymeric fluids**, the relationship between molecular weight $M$ and viscosity is given by the **Mark-Houwink equation**:

$$[\eta] = K M^a$$

Where $K$ and $a$ are constants for a given polymer-solvent-temperature system. Typically, $0.5 \leq a \leq 0.8$ for flexible polymers in good solvents. **Higher molecular weight → exponentially higher viscosity** — this is why heavy oils and polymer melts are so viscous.

For polymer melts above the critical entanglement molecular weight $M_c$:

$$\mu_0 \propto M^{3.4}$$

### 2.5 Factor 5 — Molecular Structure and Intermolecular Forces

- **Branched molecules** (e.g., iso-paraffins) → lower viscosity than straight-chain analogs.
- **Polar molecules** (e.g., water, glycerol) → higher viscosity due to hydrogen bonding.
- **Aromatic rings** → higher viscosity than aliphatic chains of equivalent size.
- **Compact spherical molecules** (e.g., neopentane) → lower viscosity than long chains.

### 2.6 Factor 6 — Shear Rate (Non-Newtonian Fluids)

For Newtonian fluids, viscosity is constant. For **non-Newtonian fluids**, viscosity depends on shear rate. The **apparent viscosity** is defined as:

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

| Fluid Type | $\mu_a$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ | Examples |
|------------|---------------------------|----------|
| Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning) | Decreases | Tomato ketchup, blood, polymer melts |
| Dilatant (shear-thickening) | Increases | Corn starch + water, wet sand |
| Bingham plastic | Constant after yield | Toothpaste, drilling mud |
| Rheopectic | Increases with time of shear | Gypsum pastes |
| Thixotropic | Decreases with time of shear | Paints, gels |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Connection:** In Module 2 experiments using a **Brookfield viscometer**, you measure torque at multiple rotational speeds. Plotting $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ allows identification of the fluid type — a key analysis step in the lab record.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Applicable To | Variables / Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{dv}{dy}$ | Newton's Law (all fluids) | $\tau$ [Pa], $\mu$ [Pa·s], $v$ [m/s], $y$ [m] |
| 2 | $\mu = A \exp\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Liquids (temperature) | $E_a$ [J/mol], $T$ [K] |
| 3 | $\ln\left(\dfrac{\mu_1}{\mu_2}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Two-point liquid viscosity | Dimensionless inside ln |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \dfrac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$ | Gases (Sutherland) | $S$ [K], $T$ [K] |
| 5 | $\mu \propto T^{0.5 \text{ to } 1.0}$ | Gases (simplified) | $T$ [K] |
| 6 | $\mu_P = \mu_{P_0} \exp(\alpha \cdot \Delta P)$ | Liquids at high pressure | $\alpha$ [Pa⁻¹], $\Delta P$ [Pa] |
| 7 | $\mu_{mix} = \mu_0(1 + 2.5\phi)$ | Dilute suspensions (Einstein) | $\phi$ = volume fraction |
| 8 | $[\eta] = K M^a$ | Polymer solutions (Mark-Houwink) | $M$ = molecular weight [g/mol] |
| 9 | $\mu_0 \propto M^{3.4}$ | Polymer melts (entangled) | $M$ = molecular weight |
| 10 | $\mu_a = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | Apparent viscosity (non-Newtonian) | $\mu_a$ [Pa·s] |
| 11 | $1 \text{ Pa·s} = 1000 \text{ cP} = 10 \text{ P}$ | Unit conversion | — |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pipeline design:** Crude oil viscosity drops 10× from 20°C to 60°C, drastically reducing pumping costs — engineers preheat heavy oils.
- **Polymer processing:** Injection molding temperatures are chosen to push viscosity below the processing window.
- **Lubricant formulation:** Viscosity Index (VI) improvers are added to multi-grade engine oils to minimize temperature sensitivity.
- **Food/pharma industry:** Shear-thinning behavior of blood allows easier flow in capillaries; ketchup bottles are designed to apply yield stress.
- **Geothermal and oil reservoirs:** Both temperature and pressure effects must be coupled for accurate flow modeling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Two-Point Viscosity-Temperature Relation (Liquid)

This is the **derivation most likely asked in a 14-mark KTU question**, because it links the lab data analysis to theoretical formulation.

**Starting Point — Andrade's Equation:**

$$\mu = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 1:** Write the equation for state 1 (temperature $T_1$, viscosity $\mu_1$):

$$\mu_1 = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_1}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Write the equation for state 2 (temperature $T_2$, viscosity $\mu_2$):

$$\mu_2 = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_2}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Take the ratio of (1) by (2) to eliminate the constant $A$:

$$\frac{\mu_1}{\mu_2} = \frac{\exp\left(\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_1}\right)}{\exp\left(\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_2}\right)}$$

**Step 4:** Combine exponents using the law $\dfrac{e^x}{e^y} = e^{x-y}$:

$$\frac{\mu_1}{\mu_2} = \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\mu_1}{\mu_2}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Sign Convention Validation:** For liquids, $\mu_1 > \mu_2$ when $T_1 < T_2$. So LHS is positive. RHS: $\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} > 0$ when $T_1 < T_2$. The identity holds.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sutherland's Equation from Kinetic Theory

**Starting Assumptions (Kinetic Theory of Gases):**
- Molecules are hard spheres of diameter $d$.
- Average molecular speed: $\bar{c} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$
- Mean free path: $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2 n}$, where $n$ is number density.

**Step 1:** From elementary kinetic theory, dynamic viscosity of an ideal gas is:

$$\mu = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{c} \lambda$$

**Step 2:** Substitute expressions for $\rho = nM/N_A$ and $\bar{c}$, $\lambda$:

$$\mu = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{nM}{N_A} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2 n}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify — note $n$ cancels out, giving:

$$\mu = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{M}{N_A} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2}$$

$$\mu = \frac{1}{3 N_A \pi d^2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8 M R T}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Combine constants. This yields $\mu \propto \sqrt{T}$, but experimental data shows $T^n$ with $n > 0.5$. The discrepancy arises because the hard-sphere assumption ignores **attractive intermolecular forces**.

**Step 5:** Sutherland's correction replaces the hard-sphere diameter $d$ with a temperature-dependent effective diameter:

$$d_{eff}^2 \propto \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{S}{T}}$$

Where $S$ = Sutherland's constant encapsulates the attractive potential well depth.

**Step 6:** Substituting back, the corrected viscosity becomes:

$$\mu \propto \frac{T^{3/2}}{T + S}$$

**Step 7:** Normalizing with a reference state $(T_0, \mu_0)$:

$$\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Einstein's Viscosity Equation for Dilute Suspensions

**Starting Point:** Consider a single rigid sphere of radius $r$ in a Newtonian fluid of viscosity $\mu_0$, subjected to a low-shear flow.

**Step 1:** The **Stokes' drag force** on the sphere at low Reynolds number:

$$F_d = 6 \pi \mu_0 r v_s$$

**Step 2:** The **power dissipated** by the sphere in the surrounding fluid is greater than in pure solvent by a factor related to the disturbance field. For a single sphere, the excess dissipation is:

$$\Delta \dot{W}_{single} = \frac{5}{2} \mu_0 \dot{\gamma}^2 \cdot \left(\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3\right)$$

**Step 3:** For a suspension of $N$ spheres per unit volume, the total excess dissipation is additive (dilute limit, no interactions):

$$\Delta \dot{W}_{total} = N \cdot \frac{5}{2} \mu_0 \dot{\gamma}^2 \cdot \left(\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3\right)$$

**Step 4:** The volume fraction of spheres is:

$$\phi = N \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$$

**Step 5:** Substituting, the total dissipation per unit volume is:

$$\dot{W}_{total} = \mu_0 \dot{\gamma}^2 + \frac{5}{2} \mu_0 \dot{\gamma}^2 \phi = \mu_0 (1 + 2.5 \phi) \dot{\gamma}^2$$

**Step 6:** Comparing with $\dot{W} = \mu \dot{\gamma}^2$ (definition of viscosity from energy dissipation):

$$\mu = \mu_0 (1 + 2.5 \phi) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Key Limitation:** Valid only for $\phi \leq 0.02$ and rigid, non-interacting spheres. For higher concentrations, extensions like **Mooney (1951)**, **Krieger-Dougherty**, and **Quemada** models are used.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect Summary

| Factor | Liquids | Gases | Mathematical Form | KTU Weightage |
|--------|---------|-------|-------------------|---------------|
| Temperature $\uparrow$ | $\mu \downarrow \downarrow$ (sharp) | $\mu \uparrow$ (mild) | Arrhenius / Sutherland | **High** |
| Pressure $\uparrow$ | $\mu \uparrow$ (only at high P) | $\mu$ ≈ const. (ideal) | $\mu_P = \mu_0 e^{\alpha \Delta P}$ | **High** |
| Concentration $\uparrow$ | $\mu \uparrow$ | $\mu \uparrow$ (mixtures) | Einstein / Mark-Houwink | Medium |
| Molecular weight $\uparrow$ | $\mu \uparrow \uparrow$ | $\mu \uparrow$ (mild) | $\mu \propto M^{3.4}$ (polymers) | Medium |
| Shear rate (non-Newtonian) | Variable | Variable (minor) | $\mu_a = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | **High** |
| Branching (more branched) | $\mu \downarrow$ | — | Empirical | Low |
| Polarity / H-bonding | $\mu \uparrow$ | — | Empirical | Low |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A process fluid has viscosity $5.0$ cP at $40°C$ and $2.0$ cP at $80°C$. Estimate the activation energy for viscous flow.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $\mu_1 = 5.0$ cP, $T_1 = 40 + 273 = 313$ K
- $\mu_2 = 2.0$ cP, $T_2 = 80 + 273 = 353$ K
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

Using the two-point formula:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\mu_1}{\mu_2}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 1:** Compute LHS:

$$\ln\left(\frac{5.0}{2.0}\right) = \ln(2.5) = 0.9163$$

**Step 2:** Compute the temperature term:

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{313} - \frac{1}{353}$$

$$= 0.003195 - 0.002833 = 0.000362 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $E_a$:

$$E_a = \frac{R \cdot \ln(\mu_1/\mu_2)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)} = \frac{8.314 \times 0.9163}{0.000362}$$

$$E_a = \frac{7.617}{0.000362} = 21{,}041 \text{ J/mol} \approx 21.04 \text{ kJ/mol} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Physical Interpretation:** This is a typical activation energy for viscous flow in organic liquids (range: 10–30 kJ/mol). Higher $E_a$ implies a stronger temperature dependence.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
