## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*"Explain the effect of temperature on the viscosity of liquids and gases. Why do they behave oppositely?"*

**Model Answer:**
The viscosity of liquids **decreases sharply with temperature** (e.g., from 1.0 cP at 20°C to 0.28 cP at 100°C for water). This is because liquid viscosity is governed by **cohesive intermolecular forces**; higher temperature increases thermal motion, weakening these forces and allowing easier molecular slippage. It follows the **Arrhenius-type relation** $\mu = A \exp(E_a / RT)$.

In contrast, the viscosity of gases **increases gently with temperature** (e.g., air at 0°C: 0.017 cP; at 500°C: 0.036 cP). Gas viscosity is governed by **molecular momentum transfer via collisions**; faster-moving molecules at higher T transfer more momentum across a velocity gradient. It follows **Sutherland's equation** $\mu/\mu_0 = (T/T_0)^{3/2}(T_0 + S)/(T + S)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key (3 marks):** 1 mark for stating the trend, 1 mark for explaining the molecular reason, 1 mark for naming the governing equation. Students often lose the third mark by omitting the formula.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*"State Einstein's equation for the viscosity of a dilute suspension. State its limitations."*

**Model Answer:**
**Einstein's equation** for the viscosity of a dilute suspension of rigid, non-interacting spherical particles in a Newtonian fluid of viscosity $\mu_0$ is:

$$\mu = \mu_0 (1 + 2.5 \phi)$$

where $\phi$ is the volume fraction of the dispersed solid phase.

**Limitations:**
1. Valid only for **dilute suspensions** ($\phi \leq 0.02$); fails for concentrated systems.
2. Assumes **spherical particles**; shape effects (rods, flakes) are not captured.
3. Assumes **rigid, non-deformable** particles; invalid for drops, bubbles, or soft particles.
4. Assumes **no slip** at the particle surface (no surfactant effects).
5. Assumes **laminar flow** and **no Brownian motion** influence.
6. Assumes **no particle-particle interactions** (no electrostatic or van der Waals forces).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Always show units** in every intermediate step. KTU examiners deduct up to 1 mark for missing units.
> 2. **Convert temperatures to Kelvin** before substituting into any equation. This is the single most common error.
> 3. For non-Newtonian fluids, clearly state the **classification** (pseudoplastic/dilatant/Bingham) with justification, not just a single number.
> 4. **Do not** confuse kinematic viscosity $\nu = \mu/\rho$ (m²/s) with dynamic viscosity $\mu$ (Pa·s). Read the question carefully.
> 5. The final answer should be boxed/rounded to **3 significant figures** unless otherwise specified.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Derive the two-point viscosity-temperature relation for a liquid starting from Andrade's equation. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A certain lubricating oil has viscosity $20$ cP at $50°C$ and $6$ cP at $100°C$. Calculate:
   (i) the activation energy for viscous flow, and
   (ii) the viscosity of the oil at $75°C$. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from Andrade's equation:

$$\mu = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

At state 1: $\mu_1 = A \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT_1}\right)$

At state 2: $\mu_2 = A \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT_2}\right)$

Dividing: $\dfrac{\mu_1}{\mu_2} = \exp\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$

Taking natural log: $\ln\left(\dfrac{\mu_1}{\mu_2}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right) \quad \blacksquare$

(1 mark: state Andrade; 2 marks: write two states; 2 marks: divide & take ln; 2 marks: final equation + physical interpretation.)

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $\mu_1 = 20$ cP at $T_1 = 50 + 273 = 323$ K; $\mu_2 = 6$ cP at $T_2 = 100 + 273 = 373$ K.

**(i) Activation Energy (3 marks):**

$$\ln\left(\frac{20}{6}\right) = \frac{E_a}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{323} - \frac{1}{373}\right)$$

$$1.2040 = \frac{E_a}{8.314}(0.003096 - 0.002681) = \frac{E_a}{8.314} \times 4.15 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$E_a = \frac{1.2040 \times 8.314}{4.15 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10.010}{4.15 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 24{,}120 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{E_a \approx 24.1 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

**(ii) Viscosity at 75°C (4 marks):**

$T_3 = 75 + 273 = 348$ K. Use $\mu_1$ as reference:

$$\ln\left(\frac{20}{\mu_3}\right) = \frac{24{,}120}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{323} - \frac{1}{348}\right)$$

$$= 2901.4 \times (0.003096 - 0.002874) = 2901.4 \times 2.22 \times 10^{-4} = 0.6441$$

$$\frac{20}{\mu_3} = e^{0.6441} = 1.904$$

$$\mu_3 = \frac{20}{1.904} = 10.50 \text{ cP}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_3 \approx 10.5 \text{ cP at } 75°C}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]**
**(a)** With the help of suitable equations, discuss in detail the **five major factors** that influence the viscosity of process fluids. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A Brookfield viscometer gives the following readings for a polymer solution at $25°C$:

| Spindle Speed (rpm) | Torque (%) |
|---------------------|------------|
| 6 | 18 |
| 12 | 28 |
| 30 | 52 |
| 60 | 85 |

Determine the **type of fluid behavior** and calculate the **apparent viscosity at 30 rpm** if the spindle constant is $K = 0.3$ and the spring constant corresponds to a full-scale torque of $67.7 \times 10^{-4}$ N·m per 100% deflection. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Five Factors (7 marks):**

1. **Temperature:** Liquids — viscosity decreases per Arrhenius $\mu = A\exp(E_a/RT)$. Gases — viscosity increases per Sutherland's equation. *Intermolecular forces vs. molecular momentum transfer.* (1.5 marks)

2. **Pressure:** Liquids at high pressure — $\mu_P = \mu_0 \exp(\alpha \Delta P)$. Gases (ideal) — viscosity **independent of pressure**. Real gases — slight increase at high P. (1.5 marks)

3. **Composition / Concentration:** For dilute suspensions: Einstein's $\mu = \mu_0(1 + 2.5\phi)$. For polymer solutions: Mark-Houwink $[\eta] = KM^a$. (1.5 marks)

4. **Molecular Weight and Chain Structure:** $\mu_0 \propto M^{3.4}$ for entangled polymer melts; branched molecules are less viscous than linear analogs; polar groups increase viscosity. (1 mark)

5. **Shear Rate (Non-Newtonian Fluids):** Apparent viscosity $\mu_a = \tau/\dot{\gamma}$. Pseudoplastic, dilatant, Bingham plastic classifications. (1.5 marks)

**Part (b) — Brookfield Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert shear rate and stress:**

Shear rate (using spindle constant $K$ as a multiplier): $\dot{\gamma} = K \times N$, where $N$ is rpm. So at $N = 6, 12, 30, 60$ rpm, $\dot{\gamma} = 1.8, 3.6, 9.0, 18.0$ s⁻¹.

Torque in N·m: $\mathcal{T} = (\% \text{ torque}/100) \times 67.7 \times 10^{-4}$ N·m

| N (rpm) | $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | $\mathcal{T}$ (N·m) | $\tau$ (Pa) (assumed $\tau \propto \mathcal{T}$) |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 6 | 1.8 | $1.224 \times 10^{-4}$ | 1.224 |
| 12 | 3.6 | $1.896 \times 10^{-4}$ | 1.896 |
| 30 | 9.0 | $3.520 \times 10^{-4}$ | 3.520 |
| 60 | 18.0 | $5.755 \times 10^{-4}$ | 5.755 |

(2 marks for the table and unit conversions.)

**Step 2 — Calculate apparent viscosity $\mu_a = \tau/\dot{\gamma}$:**

- At 6 rpm: $\mu_a = 1.224/1.8 = 0.680$ Pa·s
- At 12 rpm: $\mu_a = 1.896/3.6 = 0.527$ Pa·s
- At 30 rpm: $\mu_a = 3.520/9.0 = 0.391$ Pa·s
- At 60 rpm: $\mu_a = 5.755/18.0 = 0.320$ Pa·s

(2 marks for the calculation — **apparent viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate**.)

**Step 3 — Identify fluid type:**

Since $\mu_a$ decreases monotonically with $\dot{\gamma}$, the fluid is **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**. This is typical of polymer solutions where polymer chains progressively align in the flow direction at higher shear rates, reducing internal friction. (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Final answer:**

$$\boxed{\mu_a \text{ at } 30 \text{ rpm} \approx 0.391 \text{ Pa·s} = 391 \text{ cP, and the fluid is pseudoplastic}}$$

(2 marks: numerical answer boxed + classification stated.)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **Viscosity = internal friction** → SI: Pa·s; CGS: poise; **1 Pa·s = 1000 cP**.
- [ ] **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu (dv/dy)$ — the foundation of all viscosity models.
- [ ] **Temperature effect on liquids:** **DECREASES** viscosity (Arrhenius). Activation energy $E_a$ typically 10–30 kJ/mol.
- [ ] **Temperature effect on gases:** **INCREASES** viscosity (Sutherland). $S_{air} = 110.4$ K.
- [ ] **Pressure effect on gases (ideal):** Viscosity is **INDEPENDENT of pressure** — board-favorite.
- [ ] **Pressure effect on liquids:** Increases only at high pressure via $\mu_P = \mu_0 e^{\alpha \Delta P}$.
- [ ] **Einstein's equation:** $\mu = \mu_0(1 + 2.5\phi)$ — valid for $\phi \leq 0.02$, rigid spheres, no interactions.
- [ ] **Mark-Houwink:** $[\eta] = KM^a$ for polymer solutions; $0.5 \leq a \leq 0.8$.
- [ ] **Polymer melt viscosity:** $\mu_0 \propto M^{3.4}$ (entanglement regime).
- [ ] **Apparent viscosity:** $\mu_a = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ — meaningful for non-Newtonian fluids only.
- [ ] **Non-Newtonian fluid types:** Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning), Dilatant (shear-thickening), Bingham plastic (yield stress), Thixotropic/Rheopectic (time-dependent).
- [ ] **Brookfield viscometer** measures torque at varied rpm; plot $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ to classify.
- [ ] **Capillary viscometers** (Ostwald, Cannon-Fenske) measure flow time; viscosity from $\mu = K \cdot \rho \cdot t$.
- [ ] **Always convert to Kelvin** before temperature-related calculations.
- [ ] **Always state units** in the final answer.
- [ ] **Newtonian fluids:** Water, air, alcohol, glycerol (in some ranges), most simple hydrocarbons.
- [ ] **Non-Newtonian fluids:** Blood, ketchup, toothpaste, polymer melts, slurries, paints.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line memory hook for the exam:**
> *"Liquids: heat thins them; gases: heat thickens them; pressure barely touches gas; polymer chains stretch and slip."* 🧪

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reynolds Experiment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL408 - FLUID FLOW & PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Reynolds Experiment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Reynolds Experiment

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Reynolds Experiment** is a classical fluid mechanics laboratory experiment designed by **Osborne Reynolds (1883)** to visualize and characterize the nature of fluid flow through a closed conduit (pipe). The experiment establishes a dimensionless parameter known as the **Reynolds Number (Re)**, which serves as the universal criterion for distinguishing between **Laminar Flow**, **Transitional Flow**, and **Turbulent Flow** regimes in a pipe.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCBBL408):**
> The Reynolds experiment is a **mandatory core lab experiment** in Module 2. Students are expected to (a) determine the flow regime at various flow rates, (b) calculate the experimental Reynolds number, and (c) compare it with the **critical Reynolds number (Re_cr ≈ 2000–2300)** to validate the flow classification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sipping water through a thin transparent straw while watching the tea leaves at the bottom:

- **Slow sipping (laminar):** The tea leaves glide smoothly in a single, straight streamline along the axis of the straw. The water layers slide over each other neatly, like nested cylindrical sheets of paper sliding inside one another. There is **no mixing** between layers.

- **Medium sipping (transitional):** The streamline begins to wobble and undulate, like a flag fluttering in a gentle breeze. The flow is unstable and on the verge of chaos.

- **Forceful gulping (turbulent):** The tea leaves get tossed in all directions — the dye (or ink) injected into the center instantly smears across the entire cross-section, mixing chaotically. Eddies and swirls dominate, and there is intense cross-stream mixing.

The **Reynolds Number (Re)** is essentially a "ruler" that tells you *which drinking regime* you are in — purely based on the **ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces** in the fluid.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of water at 25 °C (ρ):** **997 kg/m³**
- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 25 °C (μ):** **0.00089 Pa·s (8.9 × 10⁻⁴ kg/m·s)**
- **Kinematic viscosity of water at 25 °C (ν):** **0.89 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
- **Standard acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**
- **Critical Reynolds Number for pipe flow (Re_cr):** **≈ 2000 to 2300** (conventionally taken as **Re_cr = 2000** for engineering design and **Re_cr = 4000** as the upper limit of transitional flow).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for viva:** Reynolds Number is **dimensionless** and depends only on the *flow geometry, fluid properties, and flow velocity* — NOT on the pipe length.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Three Flow Regimes

Osborne Reynolds demonstrated that the flow of a real fluid through a pipe can be classified into **three distinct regimes**, governed by the relative magnitude of **inertial forces** (which promote chaos) and **viscous forces** (which resist motion and promote orderliness).

### A. Laminar Flow (Re < 2000)

- The fluid moves in **parallel, well-defined streamlines** with no macroscopic mixing between adjacent layers.
- Flow is governed by the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**; the velocity profile is **parabolic**.
- The pressure drop is **directly proportional** to the average velocity (ΔP ∝ V).
- Viscous forces **dominate** over inertial forces.

### B. Transitional Flow (2000 < Re < 4000)

- The flow is **unstable** and oscillates between laminar and turbulent behavior.
- Any small disturbance can trigger a switch to turbulence.
- This regime is **avoided in engineering design** because the friction factor is unpredictable.

### C. Turbulent Flow (Re > 4000)

- Fluid particles move in **irregular, random, chaotic paths** with strong cross-stream mixing.
- The velocity profile becomes much **flatter** (nearly uniform across the core).
- Pressure drop is approximately proportional to the **square of velocity** (ΔP ∝ V²).
- Inertial forces **dominate** over viscous forces.

## 2.2 The Operating Principle of the Apparatus

The classic KTU laboratory setup consists of:

1. A **horizontal transparent glass tube** (test section) of known internal diameter **D**.
2. A **constant-head overhead tank** supplying water at steady pressure.
3. A **flow-control valve** at the discharge end to vary the flow rate **Q**.
4. A **dye reservoir** (potassium permanganate solution or methylene blue) connected via a thin hypodermic needle at the **inlet of the test section**.
5. A **measuring cylinder** and **stopwatch** to collect and time the discharge water.
6. A **thermometer** to record the water temperature (needed for viscosity lookup).

**Procedure Logic:**

- Open the control valve slightly → low flow rate → dye appears as a **sharp, straight colored line** along the tube axis → **LAMINAR**.
- Gradually increase the flow rate → the dye line begins to **oscillate and waver** → **TRANSITIONAL**.
- Open the valve fully → high flow rate → dye **disperses instantly** across the entire cross-section, mixing chaotically → **TURBULENT**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these four equations — they constitute 90% of all calculation marks in the KTU lab exam.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | Reynolds Number (Definition) | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu} = \dfrac{v \, D}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless | Ratio of inertial to viscous forces |
| 2 | Reynolds Number (using mass flow) | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{4 \, \dot{m}}{\pi \, D \, \mu}$ | Dimensionless | Alternate form when mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ is known |
| 3 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = \dfrac{V}{t}$ | m³/s | Volume collected $V$ divided by time $t$ |
| 4 | Average Fluid Velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | m/s | Volumetric flow rate divided by pipe cross-sectional area |
| 5 | Cross-sectional Area of pipe | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | m² | Geometric property of the test section |
| 6 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | m²/s | Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density |
| 7 | Critical Reynolds Number (Lower) | $\text{Re}_{cr,lower} \approx 2000$ | Dimensionless | Threshold above which flow becomes unstable |
| 8 | Critical Reynolds Number (Upper) | $\text{Re}_{cr,upper} \approx 4000$ | Dimensionless | Threshold above which flow is fully turbulent |

> **Note on notation:** In standard KTU lab manuals, **G** often denotes *mass velocity* (G = ρv, units: kg/m²·s), in which case the Reynolds number may also be written as $\text{Re} = \dfrac{G \, D}{\mu}$. Always verify which convention is used in your specific lab manual.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical Process Industries:** Determines whether flow in heat exchangers, reactors, and pipelines is laminar (efficient viscous transport) or turbulent (efficient mixing and heat transfer).
- **Hydraulic & Civil Engineering:** Used in designing water distribution networks, sewer systems, and penstocks of hydroelectric plants.
- **Pharmaceuticals & Food Processing:** Ensures predictable mixing, sterilisation, and pumping of viscous fluids.
- **Petroleum Industry:** Critical for designing pipelines (e.g., the **Trans-Alaska Pipeline System** is fully turbulent by design to reduce frictional losses at high throughput).
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Models blood flow through arteries; **Re < 2000** in small arterioles (laminar), turbulence occurs in the aorta only under pathological conditions (stenosis).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Number from First Principles

The Reynolds number is not an arbitrary number — it emerges naturally from the **Navier-Stokes equations** when they are non-dimensionalized.

### Step 1: Start with the General Momentum Equation

For an incompressible, Newtonian fluid, the differential form of the momentum conservation equation along the x-direction is:

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} \right) + \rho g_x$$

**Conversion Logic:** The **left-hand side (LHS)** represents the **inertial forces** (acceleration × mass per unit volume), and the **right-hand side (RHS)** includes the **viscous forces** (the μ ∇²u term) and the pressure and gravity terms.

### Step 2: Define the Characteristic Scales

To non-dimensionalize, we introduce:

- A characteristic length: **L** (for pipe flow, L = D, the pipe diameter).
- A characteristic velocity: **V** (the mean flow velocity).
- A characteristic pressure: **P₀**.

Define dimensionless variables (denoted by *):

$$u^* = \frac{u}{V}, \quad x^* = \frac{x}{D}, \quad y^* = \frac{y}{D}, \quad t^* = \frac{t \, V}{D}, \quad P^* = \frac{P}{\rho V^2}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each dimensional variable is replaced by a dimensionless one multiplied by its characteristic scale. For example, $u = V \cdot u^*$, meaning "the actual velocity u is the dimensionless velocity u* times the reference velocity V."

### Step 3: Substitute into the Momentum Equation

Replace every variable in the x-momentum equation. For example:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \frac{V^2}{D} \frac{\partial u^*}{\partial t^*}, \quad \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} = \frac{V}{D^2} \frac{\partial^2 u^*}{\partial x^{*2}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each derivative brings down a factor of the corresponding scale (V for velocity, 1/D for space). Doing this for the entire equation yields:

$$\frac{\rho V^2}{D} \left( \text{Inertial terms} \right) = -\frac{\rho V^2}{D} \frac{\partial P^*}{\partial x^*} + \frac{\mu V}{D^2} \left( \text{Viscous terms} \right) + \rho g_x$$

### Step 4: Divide Through by the Inertial Scale

Divide the entire equation by the inertial term prefactor $\rho V^2 / D$:

$$\text{(Inertial terms)} = -\frac{\partial P^*}{\partial x^*} + \frac{\mu}{\rho V D} \text{(Viscous terms)} + \frac{\rho g_x D}{\rho V^2}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Grouping the prefactors of the viscous and gravity terms exposes the natural dimensionless groups of the system.

### Step 5: Identify the Reynolds Number

The coefficient of the viscous term is the inverse of the Reynolds number:

$$\frac{\mu}{\rho V D} = \frac{1}{\text{Re}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Re is the only dimensionless group that controls the *relative importance* of inertia vs. viscosity. A large Re means inertia dominates (turbulent); a small Re means viscosity dominates (laminar).

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (Standard KTU Lab Format)

> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Lab Exam Question):**
> In a Reynolds experiment conducted on a horizontal glass tube of internal diameter **D = 25 mm = 0.025 m**, water is collected at the outlet. In a particular run, **V = 800 mL = 8 × 10⁻⁴ m³** of water is collected in **t = 50 seconds**. The water temperature is **25 °C**, at which the density is **ρ = 997 kg/m³** and the dynamic viscosity is **μ = 8.9 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s**. Determine the Reynolds number and identify the flow regime.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Volumetric Flow Rate (Q):**

$$Q = \frac{V_{\text{collected}}}{t} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3}{50 \text{ s}} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The volumetric flow rate is the volume of water discharged per unit time, measured using the measuring cylinder and stopwatch.

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional Area of the pipe (A):**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Conversion Logic:** The pipe cross-section is circular. The diameter is converted from mm to m (multiply by 10⁻³) before squaring.

**Step 3 — Average Linear Velocity (v):**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-5}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0326 \text{ m/s}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The mean velocity equals the volumetric flow rate divided by the cross-sectional area. This is the bulk velocity, not the maximum (centerline) velocity.

**Step 4 — Reynolds Number (Re):**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu} = \frac{997 \times 0.0326 \times 0.025}{8.9 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Substitute all values in SI units: ρ in kg/m³, v in m/s, D in m, μ in Pa·s (= kg/m·s). The units cancel out, leaving a dimensionless number.

$$\text{Re} = \frac{997 \times 0.0326 \times 0.025}{8.9 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.8125}{8.9 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Re} \approx 913}$$

**Step 5 — Flow Regime Identification:**

Since Re = 913 < 2000, the flow is **LAMINAR**.

**Conversion Logic:** Compare the computed Re with the critical values: Re < 2000 → Laminar; 2000 < Re < 4000 → Transitional; Re > 4000 → Turbulent.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How Each Variable Affects the Reynolds Number

| Variable | Symbol | Effect of Increase on Re | Engineering Implication |
|----------|--------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Fluid velocity | $v$ | Re increases **linearly** | Higher pump speed shifts flow toward turbulence |
| Pipe diameter | $D$ | Re increases **linearly** | Larger pipes promote turbulence for the same flow rate |
| Fluid density | $\rho$ | Re increases **linearly** | Denser fluids (e.g., mercury) promote turbulence |
| Fluid viscosity | $\mu$ | Re **decreases linearly** | More viscous fluids (e.g., oils) resist turbulence |
| Pipe length | $L$ | **No effect** on Re | Re is geometry-independent of length |
| Pressure drop | $\Delta P$ | Indirect effect (via $v$) | Higher ΔP → higher $v$ → higher Re |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Reynolds Experiment Apparatus

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of the experiment — illustrating the water path, the dye injection point, the control valve, and the measurement station. This is more pedagogically valuable than a hand-drawn physical sketch, as it emphasizes the **causal relationships** between each component.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[OVERHEAD CONSTANT HEAD TANK] --> B[FLOW STRAIGHTENER HONEYCOMB SECTION]
    B --> C[CALIBRATED TEST SECTION GLASS TUBE D = 25 mm]
    D[DYE RESERVOIR KMnO4 SOLUTION] -->|Thin hypodermic needle| C
    C --> E[FLOW CONTROL VALVE AT DISCHARGE END]
    E --> F[COLLECTION VESSEL GRADUATED CYLINDER]
    G[STOPWATCH] --> F
    H[THERMOMETER] --> I[WATER TEMPERATURE RECORDED]
    I --> J[LOOKUP TABLE mu rho AT MEASURED T]
    F --> K[CALCULATE Q V t]
    K --> L[CALCULATE v Q A]
    L --> M[CALCULATE Re rho v D mu]
    M --> N{FLOW REGIME CLASSIFICATION}
    N -->|Re LESS THAN 2000| O[LAMINAR FLOW STRAIGHT DYE LINE]
    N -->|2000 LESS THAN Re LESS THAN 4000| P[TRANSITIONAL FLOW OSCILLATING DYE]
    N -->|Re GREATER THAN 4000| Q[TURBULENT FLOW FULLY MIXED DYE]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Description (For Lab Notebook / Record)

For the practical record book, the apparatus should be drawn as a labelled line diagram with the following five key elements arranged horizontally:

1. **Source:** Constant-head water tank (maintains steady inlet pressure).
2. **Inlet Section:** Honeycomb flow straightener (removes swirl and ensures fully-developed flow entering the test section).
3. **Test Section:** Long transparent glass tube of known length (typically 1.0–1.5 m) and known internal diameter **D**.
4. **Dye Injection:** A narrow needle inserted at the entrance of the test section, connected to a small dye reservoir above.
5. **Outlet Section:** A control valve to vary flow rate, followed by a discharge pipe leading to the measuring cylinder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Viva Tip:** The honeycomb flow straightener is often overlooked by students but is **essential** — it removes entry-length swirl effects so that the dye line visualization is accurate. Without it, the inlet conditions would not be "fully developed" and the flow regime could be misidentified.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Reynolds number. State its physical significance and write the standard formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Reynolds number is a **dimensionless number** that represents the ratio of **inertial forces to viscous forces** in a flowing fluid. It is used to predict whether the flow will be **laminar or turbulent**.

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$$

where ρ is fluid density, v is mean velocity, D is pipe diameter, μ is dynamic viscosity, and ν is kinematic viscosity.

- **Physical significance:** Re < 2000 → Laminar; Re > 4000 → Turbulent; 2000–4000 → Transitional.
- **Why it matters:** It enables *scaling* — a small lab model can predict the behavior of a large industrial pipe.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between laminar flow and turbulent flow in a pipe.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow |
|---------|--------------|----------------|
| Reynolds number | Re < 2000 | Re > 4000 |
| Streamlines | Smooth, parallel, well-defined | Chaotic, irregular, with eddies |
| Velocity profile | Parabolic | Flatter, nearly uniform across core |
| Pressure drop | ΔP ∝ v | ΔP ∝ v² (approximately) |
| Mixing across layers | Negligible (pure molecular diffusion) | Strong (eddy mixing) |
| Friction factor (f) | f = 64/Re | f = 0.079/Re⁰·²⁵ (Blasius equation) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice is **between** Question A and Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **Reynolds experiment** to demonstrate the different flow regimes. What is the role of the **honeycomb flow straightener** and the **dye injection needle**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a Reynolds experiment, water at 30 °C flows through a glass tube of internal diameter **20 mm**. The volumetric flow rate is **0.30 L/min**. Given: ρ = 996 kg/m³, μ = 7.98 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s. Calculate the Reynolds number and identify the flow regime. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Description (7 marks):**

The Reynolds experiment is performed using the apparatus whose **block diagram is given in Section 4.1** of these notes. The key components and their roles are:

- **Constant-head tank:** Maintains a steady inlet pressure so that the only variable being changed is the flow rate at the control valve.
- **Honeycomb flow straightener:** A bundle of small parallel tubes that remove any **swirl and inlet disturbances** from the entering water, ensuring the flow is **fully developed** before reaching the test section. *Without this, the dye would appear chaotic even at low flow rates, leading to false identification of turbulent flow.*
- **Transparent glass test section:** Allows direct visual observation of the dye.
- **Dye injection needle:** Inserts a thin, continuous stream of **dye (KMnO₄ or methylene blue)** at the *centerline* of the test section. The behaviour of this dye streak reveals the flow regime.
- **Control valve & measuring cylinder:** Used to vary the flow rate and measure it accurately using $Q = V / t$.

The observation sequence is:
- At **low Q** → dye moves in a **straight, undisturbed line** → Laminar.
- At **moderate Q** → dye **oscillates and wavers** → Transitional.
- At **high Q** → dye **diffuses across the entire cross-section** and mixes chaotically → Turbulent.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- D = 20 mm = 0.020 m
- Q = 0.30 L/min = 0.30 × 10⁻³ / 60 m³/s = 5.0 × 10⁻⁶ m³/s
- ρ = 996 kg/m³
- μ = 7.98 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area (A):**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.020)^2}{4} = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Mean velocity (v):**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-6}}{3.142 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01591 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Reynolds number (Re):**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{996 \times 0.01591 \times 0.020}{7.98 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{0.3169}{7.98 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 397$$

**Step 4 — Flow regime:**

Since **Re ≈ 397 < 2000**, the flow is **LAMINAR**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Adapted]`

**(a)** Define the **critical Reynolds number**. Why is the value 2000–2300 used as the design limit for laminar flow in chemical engineering pipelines? Mention the differences in the **velocity profile** and **friction factor correlation** between laminar and turbulent flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal pipe of diameter **50 mm** carries oil (ρ = 880 kg/m³, μ = 0.025 Pa·s) at an average velocity of **0.5 m/s**. Determine: (i) the Reynolds number, (ii) the flow regime, (iii) the maximum velocity at the pipe centerline assuming laminar flow. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

- **Critical Reynolds Number (Re_cr):** The threshold value of Re at which the flow regime changes. For pipe flow, **Re_cr = 2000** (lower limit) to **4000** (upper limit). The conventional design value is **Re_cr = 2000**.
- **Design significance:** Engineers use Re_cr as a *conservative* design limit. Designing for Re < 2000 guarantees fully developed laminar flow with predictable pressure drop. The range 2000–4000 is avoided in practice because the flow is unstable and the friction factor has high uncertainty.
- **Velocity profile differences:**
  - Laminar: **Parabolic** — $u(r) = u_{max} \left[1 - (r/R)^2\right]$, with $u_{max} = 2 \bar{v}$.
  - Turbulent: **Flatter** — approximated by the **1/7th power law**, $u(r) = u_{max} (1 - r/R)^{1/7}$.
- **Friction factor (Darcy) differences:**
  - Laminar: $f = \dfrac{64}{\text{Re}}$ (theoretical, exact — Hagen-Poiseuille).
  - Turbulent (smooth pipe): $f = 0.079 \, \text{Re}^{-0.25}$ (Blasius, valid for Re up to 10⁵).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** D = 50 mm = 0.050 m, ρ = 880 kg/m³, μ = 0.025 Pa·s, v = 0.5 m/s.

**(i) Reynolds number:**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{880 \times 0.5 \times 0.050}{0.025} = \frac{22.0}{0.025} = 880$$

**(ii) Flow regime:**

Since Re = 880 < 2000, the flow is **LAMINAR**.

**(iii) Maximum velocity at centerline:**

For laminar flow in a circular pipe, the velocity profile is parabolic, and the **maximum velocity is exactly twice the average velocity**:

$$u_{max} = 2 \bar{v} = 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 marks typical):** Students frequently forget to convert mm to m before substituting into the formula. The Reynolds number works in **SI units ONLY**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Factor of 2 for u_max (–1 mark):** The relationship $u_{max} = 2\bar{v}$ is **only valid for laminar flow**. In turbulent flow, this ratio is closer to 1.15–1.25.
> 3. **Using the wrong density/viscosity (–2 marks):** Always read the **water temperature** at the start of the experiment and use the table values for **that specific temperature**, not the standard 25 °C values.
> 4. **Not stating the flow regime (–1 mark):** The numerical answer is incomplete without the explicit classification: "Laminar / Transitional / Turbulent."
> 5. **Mixing up G and v in the formula:** Some lab manuals use $\text{Re} = GD/\mu$ where G is mass velocity (kg/m²·s). Confirm which version your examiner prefers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Save this for the night before the exam!):**

- **Definition:** Reynolds number is the dimensionless ratio of **inertial forces to viscous forces**, expressed as $\text{Re} = \rho v D / \mu$.
- **Critical values:** Re < 2000 → **Laminar**; 2000 < Re < 4000 → **Transitional**; Re > 4000 → **Turbulent**.
- **Experiment essentials:** Constant-head tank, transparent test tube, dye injection, control valve, measuring cylinder, stopwatch, thermometer.
- **Observation key:**
  - *Laminar:* straight dye line.
  - *Transitional:* oscillating / wavering dye.
  - *Turbulent:* dye instantly mixes across the entire cross-section.
- **Key formulas to memorize (4 total):**
  1. $Q = V/t$
  2. $A = \pi D^2 / 4$
  3. $v = Q / A$
  4. $\text{Re} = \rho v D / \mu$
- **Parabolic velocity profile (laminar):** $u(r) = u_{max}\left[1 - (r/R)^2\right]$, and $u_{max} = 2\bar{v}$.
- **Friction factor (laminar):** $f = 64/\text{Re}$ — exact and theoretical.
- **Hagen-Poiseuille pressure drop (laminar only):** $\Delta P = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{D^2}$.
- **Always measure water temperature first** — viscosity and density are temperature-dependent.
- **Honeycomb flow straightener** is essential to remove inlet swirl and ensure fully developed flow.
- **Reynolds number is independent of pipe length** — depends only on diameter, velocity, and fluid properties.
- **Practical application:** Predicting whether flow in industrial pipelines, heat exchangers, and blood vessels is laminar or turbulent.

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip for Lab Records:** Always tabulate your observations in the format: Run No. → Volume (mL) → Time (s) → Q (m³/s) → v (m/s) → Re → Flow regime. This is the **exact format KTU examiners expect** in the record book. A clean, well-labelled table typically earns **2–3 grace marks** even if a minor calculation error is present.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Particle Size Analysis by Sieve Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL408 - FLUID FLOW & PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Particle Size Analysis by Sieve Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

**Sieve Analysis** is a standardized gravimetric particle size distribution (PSD) determination technique in which a representative, dry, free-flowing particulate sample is fractionated by passing it through a vertically stacked, calibrated series of woven-wire test sieves arranged in descending order of aperture size. The mass of material retained on each sieve is measured, and from this data the **cumulative weight percent undersize (passing)** and **cumulative weight percent retained (oversize)** distributions are computed as a function of the geometric mean aperture of each sieve interval.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Sieve analysis is a mechanical method of separating dry, granular solids into size fractions by passing the sample through a stack of standard sieves having progressively smaller openings, the results of which are expressed as cumulative percent retained or percent finer than (passing) versus particle size."*

The test conforms to international standards: **IS 460 (Part 1)**, **ASTM E11**, **ISO 3310-1**, and uses the **Tyler Standard Screen Scale** (root-2 ratio series) as the canonical mesh series for B.Tech lab work.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you have a mixed handful of **black gram (urad dal), green gram (moong), and rice** poured into a kitchen colander set with increasingly finer mesh trays. The largest grains (rice) stay on top, the medium grains (black gram) get caught in the middle, and the tiny broken bits fall to the bottom pan. By simply **weighing** how much is caught on each tray, you know exactly what percentage of the mixture is "large," "medium," or "small."

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Analogy:**
> - The **sieve stack** = a *colander with multiple stacked trays*
> - The **sieve aperture** = the *size of the holes in the mesh*
> - The **sieve shaker (Ro-Tap)** = a *mechanical hand shaking the colander for 15 minutes*
> - The **mass retained** = *what you scoop off each tray and weigh*
> - The **undersize fraction** = *everything that has fallen through to the next tray*

Sieve analysis is the workhorse of the **cement industry, detergent manufacture, fertilizer granulation, ore beneficiation, pharmaceutical powder sizing, and food processing (sugar, flour, salt)**. It is rapid, inexpensive, requires no calibration chemicals, and provides a complete distribution curve in a single 15-minute run.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Aperture (d)** | Clear opening dimension of the sieve wire mesh (μm or mm) |
| **Mesh Number** | Number of openings per linear inch (e.g., 100 mesh = 150 μm) |
| **Oversize / Retained** | Particles that did **not** pass through a given sieve |
| **Undersize / Passing** | Particles that **did** pass through a given sieve |
| **Pan** | Bottommost solid tray collecting the finest fraction (< 75 μm) |
| **Lid / Cover** | Topmost solid cover preventing sample loss during shaking |
| **Ro-Tap Sieve Shaker** | Mechanical vibratory + tapping device providing reproducible agitation |
| **Geometric Mean Diameter (d_g)** | Statistical mean of a size interval on a logarithmic scale |
| **Recovery / Mass Balance** | Ratio of (sum of masses retained + pan) to (initial mass), ideally 100 ± 1 % |

> [!TIP]
> The KTU board examiner will **deduct marks** if you write "size of the hole" instead of the correct technical term **"aperture."** Always use **"aperture," "mesh number," "undersize," and "cumulative percent passing."**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' Behind Sieve Analysis — The Operating Principle

Sieve analysis is governed by two coupled physical principles:

1. **Geometric Sieving (Size Exclusion):** A spherical or near-spherical particle will pass through a square aperture only if its *diameter* is *less than* the aperture dimension. This is the fundamental separation law.

2. **Statistical Kinetic Agitation:** The sieve shaker imparts a controlled 3-D vibratory + percussive motion (Ro-Tap: 290 oscillations/min + 150 taps/min) that:
   - Orients particles into their most stable orientation (long axis parallel to the mesh plane).
   - Forces near-mesh particles to find and traverse apertures.
   - Prevents blinding (clogging of apertures by near-size particles).

A separation is considered **complete** when additional shaking time produces < 1 % additional mass change on any sieve — typically 10–15 minutes for Ro-Tap on a 100–200 g sample.

### 2.2 The Standard Tyler Sieve Series (KTU High-Yield Reference)

The Tyler series is built on a **√2 geometric progression** of aperture sizes:

$$d_{n+1} = d_n \times \sqrt{2} \quad \text{or} \quad d_{n+2} = d_n \times 2$$

| Tyler Mesh | Aperture (μm) | Aperture (mm) |
|---:|---:|---:|
| 4 | 4750 | 4.75 |
| 6 | 3350 | 3.35 |
| 8 | 2360 | 2.36 |
| 10 | 1700 | 1.70 |
| 14 | 1180 | 1.18 |
| 20 | 850 | 0.85 |
| 28 | 600 | 0.60 |
| 35 | 425 | 0.425 |
| 48 | 300 | 0.30 |
| 65 | 212 | 0.212 |
| 100 | 150 | 0.15 |
| 150 | 106 | 0.106 |
| 200 | 75 | 0.075 |
| 270 | 53 | 0.053 |
| 400 | 38 | 0.038 |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE 7 GOLDEN FORMULAS OF SIEVE ANALYSIS — Memorize These for KTU Board Exam**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Units |
|---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | $w_i = \dfrac{m_i}{M} \times 100$ | Weight percent retained on sieve $i$ | % |
| 2 | $W_{\text{ret}}(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{i} w_j$ | Cumulative weight percent **retained** (oversize) on sieve $i$ | % |
| 3 | $W_{\text{pass}}(i) = 100 - W_{\text{ret}}(i)$ | Cumulative weight percent **passing** (undersize) through sieve $i$ | % |
| 4 | $d_{\text{geom},i} = \sqrt{d_{\text{top},i} \times d_{\text{bottom},i}}$ | Geometric mean of an interval | μm |
| 5 | $\log \bar{d}_g = \sum_{i} x_i \cdot \log d_{\text{geom},i}$ | Mass-weighted overall geometric mean diameter | μm |
| 6 | $\sigma_g = \sqrt{\dfrac{d_{84.13}}{d_{15.87}}}$ | Geometric standard deviation of the full distribution | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{\sum m_i + m_{\text{pan}}}{M} \times 100$ | Mass recovery / sieve efficiency | % |

> **Notation key:** $m_i$ = mass retained on sieve $i$; $M$ = initial total mass; $x_i$ = mass fraction of interval $i$; $d_{84.13}$ = particle size at the 84.13 % undersize point; $d_{15.87}$ = particle size at the 15.87 % undersize point.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Why Sieve Analysis is Used |
|---|---|
| **Cement (OPC / PPC manufacturing)** | Quality control of raw meal, clinker, and finished cement fineness (75 μm residue < 10 %) |
| **Pharmaceutical Tablets** | Granulation uniformity ensures consistent drug dissolution kinetics |
| **Detergents (Soda Ash, Zeolites)** | Prevents caking, controls dissolution rate in washing machines |
| **Iron Ore Beneficiation** | Determines ball-mill feed size, predicts grinding energy (Bond's Work Index) |
| **Food (Flour, Sugar, Salt, Spices)** | Removes lumps, ensures consumer-perceived smoothness, controls mesh grade |
| **Foundry Sand** | AFS (American Foundrymen's Society) grain fineness number = 0.932 × Σ(weight % × sieve number) |
| **Soil Mechanics** | Particle size classification: gravel (> 4.75 mm), sand (0.075–4.75 mm), silt (< 0.075 mm) |

> [!TIP]
> Always add this **industry context** in your KTU viva answer — examiners reward it with **½ to 1 extra mark** for demonstrating engineering application awareness.

### 2.5 Limiting Conditions & Assumptions

For valid sieve analysis results, the following must be strictly observed:

1. **Particle shape:** Assumes **near-spherical** particles. Highly acicular (needle-like) or flaky particles give biased results (long axis controls passage).
2. **Particle size range:** Valid for **38 μm to 125 mm**. Below 38 μm, electrostatic agglomeration, blinding, and airflow through apertures dominate. Above 125 mm, hand-sieving is recommended.
3. **Moisture:** Sample must be **oven-dried at 105–110 °C for 1 hour** and cooled in a desiccator. Moisture causes clumping and falsifies "fine" results.
4. **Sample mass:** Should **not exceed 6 sieve loads** (load = mass that gives ≤ 25 mm bed depth after shaking) to prevent overloading and blinding.
5. **Agglomeration:** For cohesive powders (cement, talc), a 1 % addition of dispersing agent (e.g., fumed silica) or wet-sieving may be required.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Experimental Procedure (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Sample Preparation (Drying)**
Place 200 g (≥ 100 g) of the particulate solid in a hot-air oven at **105 ± 5 °C** for 1 hour to drive off free moisture. Cool in a desiccator for 30 minutes to prevent moisture re-adsorption.

**Why?** Moisture acts as a cementing binder between fines, causing them to form agglomerates that report as "coarse" — distorting the entire PSD.

**Step 2 — Sieve Stack Assembly**
Arrange the sieves vertically with the **largest aperture at the top**, decreasing downward, terminating with the **pan** at the bottom. The standard stack is:

$$\text{Lid} \rightarrow 850 \rightarrow 600 \rightarrow 425 \rightarrow 300 \rightarrow 212 \rightarrow 150 \rightarrow 75 \rightarrow \text{Pan}$$

**Step 3 — Mass Recording**
Weigh the **empty receiving pan** and the **empty sieves** individually (or, more practically, tare the balance). Record the **initial sample mass** $M = 200$ g (typical KTU lab value).

**Step 4 — Sieving Operation**
Pour the sample quantitatively onto the topmost sieve, fit the lid, mount the stack on the **Ro-Tap sieve shaker**, and run for **10–15 minutes** at standard amplitude (Ro-Tap: ~290 rpm + 150 taps/min).

**Step 5 — Mass Determination**
After shaking, remove each sieve carefully without disturbing the retained layer. Weigh the sieve + retained material. The retained mass on each sieve is:

$$m_i = m_{\text{sieve+retained},i} - m_{\text{empty sieve},i}$$

**Step 6 — Mass Balance Check**
Sum the retained masses plus the pan mass. Compare to the initial mass $M$. A loss of **> 2 %** indicates incomplete transfer or sample loss — repeat the test.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (FULLY SOLVED — KTU Board Pattern)

**Given (Sample KTU Lab Data):**
- Initial sample mass: $M = 200$ g (river sand, oven-dried)
- Sieve stack used (μm): 850, 600, 425, 300, 212, 150, 75, Pan

**Observed Masses Retained (after 15 min Ro-Tap):**

| Sieve (μm) | Mass Retained $m_i$ (g) |
|---:|---:|
| 850 | 0.0 |
| 600 | 14.6 |
| 425 | 36.8 |
| 300 | 58.2 |
| 212 | 47.4 |
| 150 | 28.0 |
| 75 | 12.6 |
| Pan | 1.8 |
| **Total** | **199.4 g** |

**Step 7 — Compute Individual Weight Percent Retained** (using Formula 1)

$$w_{850} = \frac{0.0}{199.4} \times 100 = 0.00\ \%$$

$$w_{600} = \frac{14.6}{199.4} \times 100 = 7.32\ \%$$

$$w_{425} = \frac{36.8}{199.4} \times 100 = 18.46\ \%$$

$$w_{300} = \frac{58.2}{199.4} \times 100 = 29.19\ \%$$

$$w_{212} = \frac{47.4}{199.4} \times 100 = 23.77\ \%$$

$$w_{150} = \frac{28.0}{199.4} \times 100 = 14.04\ \%$$

$$w_{75} = \frac{12.6}{199.4} \times 100 = 6.32\ \%$$

$$w_{\text{pan}} = \frac{1.8}{199.4} \times 100 = 0.90\ \%$$

**Step 8 — Compute Cumulative Weight Percent Retained** (Formula 2)

$$W_{\text{ret}}(850) = 0.00\ \%$$

$$W_{\text{ret}}(600) = 0.00 + 7.32 = 7.32\ \%$$

$$W_{\text{ret}}(425) = 7.32 + 18.46 = 25.78\ \%$$

$$W_{\text{ret}}(300) = 25.78 + 29.19 = 54.97\ \%$$

$$W_{\text{ret}}(212) = 54.97 + 23.77 = 78.74\ \%$$

$$W_{\text{ret}}(150) = 78.74 + 14.04 = 92.78\ \%$$

$$W_{\text{ret}}(75) = 92.78 + 6.32 = 99.10\ \%$$

$$W_{\text{ret}}(\text{pan}) = 99.10 + 0.90 = 100.00\ \%$$

**Step 9 — Compute Cumulative Weight Percent Undersize (Passing)** (Formula 3)

$$W_{\text{pass}}(i) = 100 - W_{\text{ret}}(i)$$

| Sieve (μm) | $W_{\text{ret}}$ (%) | $W_{\text{pass}}$ (%) |
|---:|---:|---:|
| 850 | 0.00 | 100.00 |
| 600 | 7.32 | 92.68 |
| 425 | 25.78 | 74.22 |
| 300 | 54.97 | 45.03 |
| 212 | 78.74 | 21.26 |
| 150 | 92.78 | 7.22 |
| 75 | 99.10 | 0.90 |
| Pan | 100.00 | 0.00 |

**Step 10 — Plot the Semi-Log PSD Curve**
Plot **cumulative % passing** on the **linear y-axis** vs. **sieve aperture (μm)** on the **logarithmic x-axis**. The resulting S-curve is the **particle size distribution (PSD)** of the sample.

### 3.3 Derivation: Mass-Weighted Geometric Mean Diameter

For each size interval $i$ between two adjacent sieves, the **geometric mean of the interval** is:

$$d_{\text{geom},i} = \sqrt{d_{\text{top},i} \cdot d_{\text{bottom},i}}$$

**Derivation:** If we have $N$ particles with sizes uniformly distributed on a **log scale** between $d_{\text{top}}$ and $d_{\text{bottom}}$, the mean of $\log d$ is:

$$\overline{\log d} = \frac{\log d_{\text{top}} + \log d_{\text{bottom}}}{2} = \log \sqrt{d_{\text{top}} \cdot d_{\text{bottom}}}$$

Therefore the geometric mean of the interval is the antilog of this mean:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{geom},i} & = 10^{\,\overline{\log d}} \\
& = 10^{\,(1/2)(\log d_{\text{top}} + \log d_{\text{bottom}})} \\
& = \sqrt{d_{\text{top}} \cdot d_{\text{bottom}}}
\end{aligned}$$

**For our worked example**, the interval geometric means are:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{geom}}(850{-}600) & = \sqrt{850 \times 600} = 714.1\ \mu m \\
d_{\text{geom}}(600{-}425) & = \sqrt{600 \times 425} = 505.0\ \mu m \\
d_{\text{geom}}(425{-}300) & = \sqrt{425 \times 300} = 357.1\ \mu m \\
d_{\text{geom}}(300{-}212) & = \sqrt{300 \times 212} = 252.2\ \mu m \\
d_{\text{geom}}(212{-}150) & = \sqrt{212 \times 150} = 178.3\ \mu m \\
d_{\text{geom}}(150{-}75) & = \sqrt{150 \times 75} = 106.1\ \mu m \\
d_{\text{geom}}(75{-}\text{pan}) & = \frac{75}{\sqrt{2}} = 53.0\ \mu m \quad \text{(estimated)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Overall mass-weighted geometric mean diameter** (Formula 5):

$$\begin{aligned}
\log \bar{d}_g & = \sum_i x_i \cdot \log d_{\text{geom},i} \\
& = 0.0732 \cdot \log(714.1) + 0.1846 \cdot \log(505.0) + 0.2919 \cdot \log(357.1) \\
& \quad + 0.2377 \cdot \log(252.2) + 0.1404 \cdot \log(178.3) \\
& \quad + 0.0632 \cdot \log(106.1) + 0.0090 \cdot \log(53.0)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\log \bar{d}_g & = 0.0732 \times 2.8537 + 0.1846 \times 2.7033 + 0.2919 \times 2.5527 \\
& \quad + 0.2377 \times 2.4017 + 0.1404 \times 2.2513 + 0.0632 \times 2.0257 + 0.0090 \times 1.7243
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\log \bar{d}_g & = 0.2089 + 0.4990 + 0.7452 + 0.5709 + 0.3161 + 0.1280 + 0.0155 \\
& = 2.4836
\end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \bar{d}_g = 10^{2.4836} = 304.6\ \mu m$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Geometric Standard Deviation

By the **lognormal distribution definition**, the geometric standard deviation captures the spread of a log-normal size distribution:

$$\sigma_g = \sqrt{\frac{d_{84.13}}{d_{15.87}}}$$

**Why 84.13 % and 15.87 %?** These are the *first* and *second* standard-deviation points of a normal distribution ($\mu \pm 1\sigma$ for a log-transformed size). On the cumulative % passing vs. $\log d$ plot, these correspond to the *linear* distance of $\pm 1\sigma$ from $\log \bar{d}_g$:

$$\log d_{84.13} - \log d_{15.87} = 2 \log \sigma_g$$

Exponentiating:

$$\frac{d_{84.13}}{d_{15.87}} = \sigma_g^{\,2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_g = \sqrt{\frac{d_{84.13}}{d_{15.87}}}$$

**Reading $d_{15.87}$ and $d_{84.13}$ from the PSD curve (graphical interpolation):**

- $d_{15.87}$ = sieve aperture at which cumulative % passing = 15.87 %. From the worked example's PSD curve, this falls between 300 μm (45.03 %) and 212 μm (21.26 %). Log-linear interpolation:

$$\log d_{15.87} = \log(300) - \frac{45.03 - 15.87}{45.03 - 21.26} \times [\log(300) - \log(212)]$$

$$= 2.4771 - \frac{29.16}{23.77} \times 0.1514 = 2.4771 - 0.1858 = 2.2913$$

$$d_{15.87} = 10^{2.2913} = 195.6\ \mu m$$

- $d_{84.13}$ = sieve aperture at which cumulative % passing = 84.13 %. This falls between 212 μm (21.26 %) and 150 μm (7.22 %):

$$\log d_{84.13} = \log(212) - \frac{21.26 - 84.13}{21.26 - 7.22} \times [\log(212) - \log(150)]$$

$$= 2.3263 - \frac{-62.87}{14.04} \times 0.1500 = 2.3263 + 0.6715 = 2.9978$$

Wait — note that 84.13 % passing means 15.87 % retained, which is the *undersize* axis. Since 84.13 % lies *below* 92.68 % (600 μm) and *above* 74.22 % (425 μm):

$$\log d_{84.13} = \log(600) - \frac{92.68 - 84.13}{92.68 - 74.22} \times [\log(600) - \log(425)]$$

$$= 2.7782 - \frac{8.55}{18.46} \times 0.1498 = 2.7782 - 0.0694 = 2.7088$$

$$d_{84.13} = 10^{2.7088} = 511.3\ \mu m$$

**Therefore:**

$$\sigma_g = \sqrt{\frac{511.3}{195.6}} = \sqrt{2.614} = 1.617$$

**Interpretation:** A $\sigma_g = 1.617$ indicates a **moderately broad** size distribution ($\sigma_g = 1$ is perfectly monodisperse; $\sigma_g > 1.5$ is polydisperse).

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Sieve Analysis

| Material Type | Recommended? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Dry sand, gravel, mineral ore | ✅ Yes (ideal) | Free-flowing, no agglomeration |
| Cement (OPC) | ✅ Yes | Standard quality control test |
| Detergent powder | ✅ Yes | With anti-caking pretreatment |
| Wet clay slurry | ❌ No | Agglomerates; use **sedimentation** or **laser diffraction** |
| Sub-micron pigment (TiO₂) | ❌ No | Below 38 μm limit; use laser diffraction |
| Flaky mica / graphite | ⚠️ Cautious | Anisotropic shape biases results |
| Sticky powder (sugar, salt) | ⚠️ With care | Sieve blinding; use clean sieves & shorter intervals |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Experimental Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Receive particulate sample"] --> B["Oven dry at 105 C for 1 hour"]
    B --> C["Cool in desiccator for 30 min"]
    C --> D["Weigh initial sample mass M"]
    D --> E["Assemble sieve stack: Lid on top, largest aperture at top, Pan at bottom"]
    E --> F["Pour sample onto top sieve"]
    F --> G["Mount stack on Ro-Tap sieve shaker"]
    G --> H["Run for 10 to 15 minutes at standard amplitude"]
    H --> I["Disassemble stack carefully"]
    I --> J["Weigh each sieve and pan individually"]
    J --> K["Compute mass retained m_i for each sieve"]
    K --> L{"Mass balance check: sum m_i within 2 percent of M"}
    L -- "Yes" --> M["Compute weight percent retained w_i"]
    L -- "No" --> N["Repeat test with fresh sample"]
    N --> A
    M --> O["Compute cumulative percent retained and percent passing"]
    O --> P["Plot PSD curve: percent passing vs log aperture"]
    P --> Q["Compute geometric mean diameter d_g"]
    Q --> R["Compute geometric standard deviation sigma_g"]
    R --> S["Report results and interpret distribution"]
    S --> T["END"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Sieve Stack Physical Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["LID: Solid cover to prevent dust loss"] --> S1["Sieve 850 um: Coarse particles retained"]
    S1 --> S2["Sieve 600 um: Coarse to medium particles"]
    S2 --> S3["Sieve 425 um: Medium sand fraction"]
    S3 --> S4["Sieve 300 um: Fine to medium fraction"]
    S4 --> S5["Sieve 212 um: Fine sand fraction"]
    S5 --> S6["Sieve 150 um: Very fine fraction"]
    S6 --> S7["Sieve 75 um: Silt boundary fraction"]
    S7 --> P1["PAN: Collects all particles finer than 75 um"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Ro-Tap Sieve Shaker

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1["Electric Motor: 0.25 HP, 1440 rpm"] --> G1["Gear Reducer Assembly"]
    G1 --> G2["Eccentric Cam: 290 vertical oscillations per minute"]
    G1 --> G3["Hammer Striker: 150 hammer taps per minute"]
    G2 --> S1["Sieve Stack Platform: Vertical motion imparts vertical hop to particles"]
    G3 --> S1
    S1 --> S2["Particle Stratification: Large particles rise, fines descend through apertures"]
    S2 --> S3["PSD Output: Mass distribution across 8 to 10 size intervals"]
```

```mermaid
graph LR

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define sieve analysis. List any four standard sieve series used for particle size analysis and state the geometric ratio of the Tyler standard series.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Sieve analysis is a **mechanical method of separating dry granular solids into size fractions** by passing a known mass of the sample through a stack of standard test sieves with progressively decreasing aperture sizes, and expressing the result as cumulative weight percent retained (oversize) or percent passing (undersize) versus particle size.

Four standard sieve series:

1. **Tyler Standard Screen Scale** (USA, 1910) — root-2 ratio
2. **ASTM E11** (USA) — root-2 or root-2^(1/4) ratio
3. **IS 460 (Part 1)** (India, BIS) — root-2 ratio
4. **ISO 3310-1** (International) — root-2 ratio (R20/3 series)

The **geometric ratio of the Tyler standard series is √2 = 1.414**, meaning each successive finer sieve has an aperture $\sqrt{2}$ times smaller than the coarser one above it. Every alternate sieve therefore has exactly half (or double) the aperture of its neighbour.

**[Distribution: 1 mark definition + 1.5 marks four series + 0.5 mark ratio = 3 marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the formula for geometric mean diameter of a particulate sample. Why is geometric mean preferred over arithmetic mean in particle size analysis?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula:**

$$\log \bar{d}_g = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot \log d_{\text{geom},i} \quad \text{where} \quad d_{\text{geom},i} = \sqrt{d_{\text{top},i} \cdot d_{\text{bottom},i}}$$

Here $x_i$ is the mass fraction in the $i^{\text{th}}$ size interval and $d_{\text{geom},i}$ is the geometric mean of the two bounding sieves.

**Why geometric mean is preferred (1.5 marks):**

1. Particle size distributions are typically **lognormal**, not normal — particle sizes span 2–4 orders of magnitude (e.g., 1 μm to 1000 μm).
2. The arithmetic mean is **dominated by the largest particles** in a lognormal distribution, giving misleading central-tendency values.
3. The geometric mean **equal-weight treats each size decade**, providing an unbiased representation of the population on a logarithmic scale.
4. The geometric mean is **dimensionally consistent** with the **multiplicative nature** of particle size phenomena (e.g., settling, sieving, filtration all involve size ratios, not differences).

**[Distribution: 1 mark formula + 1.5 marks justification = 3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to log₁₀ before summing** in the geometric mean formula — the most common loss-of-marks error (–2 to –3 marks).
> 2. **Using arithmetic mean** of bounding sieves (e.g., (d_top + d_bottom)/2) instead of **geometric mean** — penalised heavily (–2 marks).
> 3. **Neglecting mass balance check** at the end of calculation — examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong x-axis scale** on the PSD plot (linear instead of log) — −1.5 marks.
> 5. **Confusing percent retained with percent passing** — common conceptual error (−1 mark).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Calculation + Theory** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the geometric mean diameter and the geometric standard deviation of a lognormally distributed particulate sample. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 250 g sample of granite aggregate was subjected to sieve analysis using the Tyler standard series. The mass retained on each sieve is tabulated below. Plot the PSD curve (semi-log) and determine the **geometric mean diameter** and the **geometric standard deviation** of the distribution. **(7 marks)**

| Sieve (μm) | Mass Retained (g) |
|---:|---:|
| 1180 | 0.0 |
| 850 | 12.5 |
| 600 | 38.0 |
| 425 | 64.5 |
| 300 | 72.0 |
| 212 | 41.0 |
| 150 | 18.5 |
| 75 | 3.0 |
| Pan | 0.5 |
| **Total** | **250.0** |

**Model Solution (Part A):**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

For a lognormal distribution, the particle size $d$ follows:

$$\ln d \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$$

where $\mu$ is the mean of $\ln d$ and $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of $\ln d$. By definition:

$$\bar{d}_g = e^{\mu} \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_g = e^{\sigma}$$

From the normal distribution, the **z-scores** corresponding to cumulative probability levels 15.87 % and 84.13 % are $\pm 1$:

$$P(\ln d \leq \mu - \sigma) = 15.87\ \% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln d_{15.87} = \mu - \sigma$$

$$P(\ln d \leq \mu + \sigma) = 84.13\ \% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln d_{84.13} = \mu + \sigma$$

Subtracting:

$$\ln d_{84.13} - \ln d_{15.87} = 2\sigma \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma = \frac{1}{2}\ln\left(\frac{d_{84.13}}{d_{15.87}}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_g = e^{\sigma} = \sqrt{\frac{d_{84.13}}{d_{15.87}}}$$

Similarly, the geometric mean is read at 50 % passing:

$$\bar{d}_g = \sqrt{d_{15.87} \times d_{84.13}}$$

This derivation is the **canonical KTU answer** — write the full normal distribution logic, the 15.87/84.13 origin, and the final two boxed formulas. **Full marks: 7.**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Verify mass balance:**
$$\sum m_i = 12.5 + 38.0 + 64.5 + 72.0 + 41.0 + 18.5 + 3.0 + 0.5 = 250.0\ g = M \ \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute cumulative % passing:**

| Sieve (μm) | $m_i$ (g) | $w_i$ (%) | $W_{\text{ret}}$ (%) | $W_{\text{pass}}$ (%) |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1180 | 0.0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 100.00 |
| 850 | 12.5 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 95.00 |
| 600 | 38.0 | 15.20 | 20.20 | 79.80 |
| 425 | 64.5 | 25.80 | 46.00 | 54.00 |
| 300 | 72.0 | 28.80 | 74.80 | 25.20 |
| 212 | 41.0 | 16.40 | 91.20 | 8.80 |
| 150 | 18.5 | 7.40 | 98.60 | 1.40 |
| 75 | 3.0 | 1.20 | 99.80 | 0.20 |
| Pan | 0.5 | 0.20 | 100.00 | 0.00 |

**Step 3 — Plot PSD:** cumulative % passing (linear y-axis) vs. log aperture in μm (log x-axis). **S-curve drawn, properly labelled with axes and grid.**

**Step 4 — Read $d_{15.87}$, $d_{50}$, $d_{84.13}$ by log-linear interpolation:**

- **$d_{15.87}$** lies between 300 μm (25.20 %) and 425 μm (54.00 %):

$$\log d_{15.87} = \log(300) + \frac{25.20 - 15.87}{25.20 - 54.00} \times [\log(425) - \log(300)]$$

$$= 2.4771 + \frac{9.33}{-28.80} \times 0.1515 = 2.4771 - 0.0491 = 2.4280$$

$$d_{15.87} = 10^{2.4280} = 268.0\ \mu m$$

- **$d_{50}$** lies between 300 μm (25.20 %) and 425 μm (54.00 %):

$$\log d_{50} = \log(300) + \frac{50 - 25.20}{54.00 - 25.20} \times [\log(425) - \log(300)]$$

$$= 2.4771 + \frac{24.80}{28.80} \times 0.1515 = 2.4771 + 0.1305 = 2.6076$$

$$\bar{d}_g = 10^{2.6076} = 405.4\ \mu m$$

- **$d_{84.13}$** lies between 425 μm (54.00 %) and 600 μm (79.80 %):

$$\log d_{84.13} = \log(425) + \frac{84.13 - 54.00}{79.80 - 54.00} \times [\log(600) - \log(425)]$$

$$= 2.6284 + \frac{30.13}{25.80} \times 0.1498 = 2.6284 + 0.1750 = 2.8034$$

$$d_{84.13} = 10^{2.8034} = 636.4\ \mu m$$

**Step 5 — Compute geometric mean and standard deviation:**

$$\bar{d}_g = \sqrt{d_{15.87} \times d_{84.13}} = \sqrt{268.0 \times 636.4} = \sqrt{170,555} = 413.0\ \mu m$$

$$\sigma_g = \sqrt{\frac{636.4}{268.0}} = \sqrt{2.375} = 1.541$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**
$$\boxed{\bar{d}_g \approx 413\ \mu m \quad ; \quad \sigma_g \approx 1.54}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Procedure + Viva Style** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **complete experimental procedure** for determining the particle size distribution of a given sample of dry sand by sieve analysis. List the apparatus, chemicals (if any), and the precautions to be observed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the **common sources of error** in a sieve analysis experiment? Explain how each error affects the reported particle size distribution. Briefly describe **two industrial applications** of sieve analysis in process industries. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part B):**

**(a) Procedure + Apparatus + Precautions (7 marks):**

**Apparatus Required (1.5 marks):**
- Ro-Tap mechanical sieve shaker (290 osc/min, 150 taps/min)
- Tyler standard sieve set (8 sieves: 850, 600, 425, 300, 212, 150, 75 μm + Pan)
- Sieve lid (cover) and receiving pan
- Electronic balance (readability 0.01 g)
- Hot-air oven (105 ± 5 °C)
- Desiccator with silica gel
- Soft-bristle brush for sieve cleaning
- Stopwatch

**Reagent:** None (dry method). For cohesive powders, a few drops of dispersing agent (e.g., 1 % fumed silica or isopropanol) may be used.

**Procedure (4 marks):**

1. Take a representative sample of dry sand ≥ 100 g (typically 200 g) by **coning-and-quartering** or **riffle splitting** to ensure homogeneity.
2. **Oven-dry** at 105 °C for 1 hour to constant mass; cool in desiccator for 30 min.
3. Weigh the sample accurately: $M$ g.
4. Assemble the **sieve stack in descending order of aperture** (largest at top, smallest just above pan); place the lid on top.
5. **Pour** the entire sample onto the top sieve; cover with lid.
6. Mount the stack on the Ro-Tap shaker; **run for 15 min** (or until < 1 % mass change on any sieve on a 1-min re-shake).
7. Disassemble the stack carefully, **weighing each sieve + retained material individually** on the balance.
8. Compute the mass retained $m_i$ on each sieve.
9. Perform a **mass balance check**: $\sum m_i + m_{\text{pan}}$ should be within 1–2 % of $M$.
10. Compute weight percent retained, cumulative percent retained, cumulative percent passing.
11. **Plot** the PSD curve (semi-log) and compute geometric mean diameter and geometric standard deviation.

**Precautions (1.5 marks):**

- Sieves must be **calibrated and undamaged**; inspect for torn mesh or distorted frames before use.
- Do **not overload** sieves — load should give ≤ 25 mm bed depth after shaking.
- Sample must be **completely dry**; moisture causes agglomeration.
- Handle sieves gently after shaking to **avoid loss of fines**.
- Clean sieves thoroughly with the soft brush between tests; never wash with water unless specified by the mesh material.
- **Mass balance** must be verified — losses > 2 % invalidate the run.

**(b) Sources of Error + Applications (7 marks):**

**Sources of Error (4 marks):**

| # | Source of Error | Effect on PSD |
|---:|---|---|
| 1 | **Insufficient shaking time** | Over-reports coarse fraction; under-reports fines |
| 2 | **Excessive shaking time** | Attrition of particles; over-reports fines; sieves may wear out |
| 3 | **Moisture in sample** | Fine particles agglomerate; over-reports coarse fraction |
| 4 | **Overloaded sieves** | Bed depth too high; blinding; coarse fraction over-reported |
| 5 | **Damaged / clogged sieves** | Aperture size altered; arbitrary shifts in mass distribution |
| 6 | **Non-representative sampling** (insufficient coning-quartering) | Reported PSD is not the population's true PSD |
| 7 | **Vibratory loss of fines** during disassembly | Under-reports fines; mass balance fails |
| 8 | **Operator bias** in transferring sample to top sieve | Random loss; affects all size fractions equally |

**Industrial Applications (3 marks):**

1. **Cement Industry — Quality Control of Finished Cement:**
   The residue on the **90 μm (170 mesh) sieve** must be **< 10 %** for OPC 43-grade and **< 5 %** for OPC 53-grade cement as per IS 269. Sieve analysis is the **standard acceptance test** before cement is dispatched to customers. Excess residue indicates under-grinding in the ball mill, which reduces the rate of hydration and the 28-day compressive strength of concrete.

2. **Pharmaceutical Tablet Granulation:**
   Sieve analysis is used to control the **size distribution of wet granules** before tablet compression. A typical target is **d₅₀ = 200–400 μm with σ_g < 2.0**. Deviations cause **uneven die-fill** in the tablet press, leading to **weight variation, capping, and lamination defects** in the final tablets. The FDA Process Analytical Technology (PAT) framework mandates sieve-based PSD control for validated tablet manufacturing.

> [!TIP]
> Adding a **third industrial application** (e.g., **AFS grain fineness number for foundry sand**, or **flour grade classification for food industry**) can earn a **bonus half mark** in KTU valuation — examiners appreciate it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Sieve Analysis**

- ✅ **Definition:** Gravimetric separation of dry granular solids using stacked standard sieves; result expressed as cumulative % passing vs. sieve aperture.
- ✅ **Sieve Standards:** Tyler, ASTM E11, IS 460, ISO 3310-1 — all based on **√2 ratio**.
- ✅ **Stack Order:** Lid → 850 → 600 → 425 → 300 → 212 → 150 → 75 μm → Pan (descending aperture).
- ✅ **Sample Prep:** Oven-dry at 105 °C for 1 hour, cool in desiccator 30 min, weigh.
- ✅ **Shaking Time:** 10–15 min on Ro-Tap (290 osc/min + 150 taps/min).
- ✅ **Key Formulas:** $w_i = (m_i/M) \times 100$; $W_{\text{pass}} = 100 - W_{\text{ret}}$; $d_{\text{geom}} = \sqrt{d_{\text{top}} d_{\text{bottom}}}$; $\log \bar{d}_g = \sum x_i \log d_i$; $\sigma_g = \sqrt{d_{84.13}/d_{15.87}}$.
- ✅ **PSD Plot:** Linear y (% passing) vs. **log x (aperture in μm)** — semi-log plot.
- ✅ **Mass Balance:** $\sum m_i + m_{\text{pan}} \approx M$ within ± 1–2 %; otherwise repeat.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Not valid for < 38 μm (use laser diffraction) or for wet / sticky / flaky samples.
- ✅ **Industrial Uses:** Cement fineness (90 μm residue), pharmaceutical granule control, ore beneficiation, soil classification, foundry sand AFS number, food/feed sizing.
- ✅ **KTU Viva Favorites:** Difference between arithmetic vs geometric mean, why Ro-Tap is preferred over hand sieving, why mass balance check is mandatory, why sample must be oven-dried, what is AFS grain fineness number.
- ✅ **Common Examiner Traps:** Wrong x-axis scale (linear vs log), arithmetic mean of bounding sieves (must be geometric), missing mass balance check, forgetting % sign on the y-axis.
- ✅ **Highest-Mark Tip:** Always **re-derive** the geometric standard deviation formula in the exam — it shows conceptual understanding and earns **full 7 marks** on the derivation question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sub-sieve Particle Size Analysis using Beaker Decantation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL408 - FLUID FLOW & PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Sub-sieve Particle Size Analysis using Beaker Decantation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Sub-sieve Particle Size Analysis** refers to the determination of the size distribution of solid particles that are finer than the aperture of the finest standard sieve (typically **44 µm**, corresponding to **325 mesh**). Because ordinary sieving fails to physically separate such fine particles, alternative indirect techniques based on **sedimentation principles** are employed.

**Beaker Decantation** is the simplest and most pedagogically valuable of these sedimentation techniques. It relies on **Stokes' Law of resistance** for a rigid sphere moving at low Reynolds number through a viscous fluid. The particle size is inferred from the **terminal settling velocity**, which is itself deduced from the **settling depth and elapsed time**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme — PCBBL408):**
> Sub-sieve analysis is taught under the broader theme of *"indirect methods of particle size analysis"* alongside Andreasen pipette, sedimentation balance, and elutriation. Beaker decantation is universally treated as the **conceptual prototype** from which the more refined methods (pipette, balance) are derived.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you shake a snow globe filled with glitter and water. The biggest flakes sink first, the medium ones next, and the tiny specks drift down slowly. Now suppose, after a fixed time `t₁`, you carefully pour off the top layer — that liquid carries only the *tiny specks*. You refill, shake again, wait a shorter time `t₂`, and pour off the next-shallowest band of medium flakes. By repeating this "decant-and-weigh" cycle, you physically partition the glitter into size fractions — *without ever touching a sieve*. That is exactly what beaker decantation does to a powdered solid suspended in water or glycerol.

### Boundary of Applicability

> [!NOTE]
> **Particle Domain for Stokes' Regime:**
> - Particle diameter range: **≈ 1 µm to 44 µm**
> - Density difference (ρₚ − ρf): should be significant (typically > 0.5 g/cm³)
> - Flow regime: **viscous (laminar)** — Reynolds number of the particle **Reₚ < 0.2**
> - Concentration: dilute enough to ensure **free settling** (no hindered settling)

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Acceleration due to gravity, **g = 981 cm/s² = 9.81 m/s²**
- Density of water at 30 °C, **ρf ≈ 0.996 g/cm³**
- Viscosity of water at 30 °C, **µ ≈ 0.80 cP = 0.0080 g/(cm·s)**
- Viscosity of glycerol-water mixtures may be tuned to slow down ultrafine settling
- The "**sub-sieve cut-off**" universally accepted in industry: **44 µm (325 mesh)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Underlying Physics

The technique is founded on three interconnected principles:

1. **Stokes' Law of Resistance** — describes the drag force on a small sphere in viscous flow.
2. **Force balance at terminal velocity** — gravity (minus buoyancy) is exactly balanced by viscous drag.
3. **Quiescent settling in a beaker** — particles distribute vertically according to size, enabling *physical partition* by timed decantation.

### Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1 — Suspension Preparation:**
A weighed amount of dry powder (W₁) is dispersed in a known volume of liquid inside a tall beaker (typically 500 mL or 1 L). A wetting/dispersing agent (e.g., sodium hexametaphosphate or sodium silicate, ~0.1 % w/v) is added to **break agglomerates** and stabilize the suspension.

**Step 2 — First Decantation (largest sub-sieve size d₁):**
After stirring thoroughly and stopping the stirrer, the suspension is allowed to stand for a *predetermined* time `t₁`. This time is chosen such that *all particles larger than d₁* settle below the decanting height `h`, while particles *smaller than d₁* remain in suspension. The supernatant (containing fines < d₁) is carefully decanted into a separate container.

**Step 3 — Second Decantation (next size d₂):**
Fresh liquid is added to the settled bed, the suspension is re-stirred, and allowed to settle for a shorter time `t₂ < t₁`. This retains particles in the range d₂ to d₁, which are again decanted into another container.

**Step 4 — Iteration:**
The process is repeated for progressively smaller cuts (d₃, d₄, …) until all fractions have been isolated. Each fraction is filtered, dried, and weighed (W₂, W₃, …).

**Step 5 — Mass Distribution:**
The mass fraction in each size band yields the **cumulative undersize** or **oversize distribution** of the original powder.

### Why Settling Time is Pre-calculated

Because Stokes' Law gives a deterministic link between particle diameter and settling velocity, the *maximum* particle size that can still be present in the supernatant after time `t` and depth `h` is uniquely determined. By choosing `t`, we *choose the cut size*.

### Real-World Utility

- **Mineral processing** — fast preliminary grading of milled ore before flotation.
- **Cement industry** — quality control of raw mix and clinker fineness (Blaine complement).
- **Ceramic powder production** — ensuring narrow size distribution of slips and glazes.
- **Pharmaceutical powders** — API (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient) flow and dissolution depend on sub-sieve distribution.
- **Soil science** — hydrometer and pipette techniques are mathematically identical to beaker decantation.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units (CGS)** | **Boundary / Note** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Stokes' terminal velocity | $v_t = \dfrac{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g}{18\,\mu}$ | cm/s | Reₚ < 0.2 |
| 2 | Particle diameter from vₜ | $d_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\,\mu\,v_t}{(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g}}$ | cm | Invert of eq. 1 |
| 3 | Settling time for cut dₚ | $t = \dfrac{h}{v_t} = \dfrac{18\,\mu\,h}{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g}$ | s | h = settling depth |
| 4 | Maximum size suspended after time t | $d_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\,\mu\,h}{(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g\,t}}$ | cm | Used to predict cut |
| 5 | Particle Reynolds number | $Re_p = \dfrac{d_p\,v_t\,\rho_f}{\mu} = \dfrac{d_p^{\,3}\,(\rho_p-\rho_f)\,\rho_f\,g}{18\,\mu^{2}}$ | dimensionless | Must be < 0.2 |
| 6 | Mass fraction in a fraction | $x_i = \dfrac{W_i}{\sum W_i}$ | dimensionless | Cumulative check: Σxᵢ = 1 |
| 7 | Cumulative undersize | $U(d) = \sum_{d_i < d} x_i$ | % | Non-decreasing |
| 8 | Cumulative oversize | $O(d) = 100 - U(d)$ | % | Non-increasing |
| 9 | Differential fraction | $\Delta x_i = x_i$ between two consecutive cuts | % | Plotted vs. mean size |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Note on Units:**
> Examiners routinely award *partial marks* when CGS units (cm, g, s, poise) are used **consistently** inside one equation. Mixing SI (m, kg) with CGS in a single line is the #1 cause of arithmetic errors in this experiment. **Pick a system and stay loyal to it.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Stokes Diameter

Consider a rigid spherical particle of diameter `dₚ` and density `ρₚ` falling under gravity through a fluid of density `ρf` and viscosity `µ`. Three forces act on the particle:

1. **Gravitational (downward) force:**
$F_g = \dfrac{\pi}{6}\,d_p^{\,3}\,\rho_p\,g$

2. **Buoyant (upward) force:**
$F_b = \dfrac{\pi}{6}\,d_p^{\,3}\,\rho_f\,g$

3. **Viscous drag (Stokes' resistance, upward):**
$F_d = 3\,\pi\,\mu\,d_p\,v_t$

At **terminal velocity**, the net force is zero:

$$F_g - F_b - F_d = 0$$

Substituting the three expressions:

$$\dfrac{\pi}{6}\,d_p^{\,3}\,\rho_p\,g \;-\; \dfrac{\pi}{6}\,d_p^{\,3}\,\rho_f\,g \;-\; 3\,\pi\,\mu\,d_p\,v_t \;=\; 0$$

Grouping the gravity terms and cancelling `dₚ`:

$$\dfrac{\pi}{6}\,d_p^{\,3}\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g \;=\; 3\,\pi\,\mu\,d_p\,v_t$$

Dividing both sides by π and `dₚ`:

$$\dfrac{1}{6}\,d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g \;=\; 3\,\mu\,v_t$$

Solving explicitly for `vₜ`:

$$v_t \;=\; \dfrac{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g}{18\,\mu}$$

Inverting to obtain `dₚ` in terms of the *measured* velocity `vₜ = h/t`:

$$d_p \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{18\,\mu\,h}{(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g\,t}}$$

This is the **master equation** for all Stokes-regime sedimentation analyses.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem (typical ESE-style):**
