A quartz powder (ρₚ = 2.65 g/cm³) is dispersed in water at 30 °C (ρf = 0.996 g/cm³, µ = 0.0080 poise) in a beaker of liquid depth **h = 10 cm**. The suspension is allowed to settle for **t = 600 s**. Calculate the largest particle size still suspended in the supernatant and verify that Stokes' Law applies.

**Solution — Step (a): Maximum diameter remaining in suspension**

Apply the master equation directly:

$$d_{\max} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{18\,\mu\,h}{(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g\,t}}$$

Substituting values (CGS):

$$d_{\max} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \times 0.0080 \times 10}{(2.65 - 0.996) \times 981 \times 600}}$$

Compute numerator:

$$18 \times 0.0080 \times 10 \;=\; 1.440$$

Compute denominator:

$$(2.65 - 0.996) \times 981 \times 600 \;=\; 1.654 \times 981 \times 600$$

Breaking this down step-by-step:

$$1.654 \times 981 \;=\; 1622.574$$

$$1622.574 \times 600 \;=\; 973544.4$$

Therefore:

$$d_{\max} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{1.440}{973544.4}}$$

$$\dfrac{1.440}{973544.4} \;=\; 1.479 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{cm}^2$$

$$d_{\max} \;=\; \sqrt{1.479 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$d_{\max} \;=\; 1.216 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{cm}$$

Converting to micrometres:

$$d_{\max} \;=\; 1.216 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{cm} \times \dfrac{10^{4}\,\mu m}{1\,\text{cm}} \;=\; 12.16\,\mu m$$

**Solution — Step (b): Verification of Stokes' regime**

First compute the terminal velocity:

$$v_t \;=\; \dfrac{h}{t} \;=\; \dfrac{10\,\text{cm}}{600\,\text{s}} \;=\; 0.01667\,\text{cm/s}$$

Then compute particle Reynolds number:

$$Re_p \;=\; \dfrac{d_p\,v_t\,\rho_f}{\mu}$$

$$Re_p \;=\; \dfrac{1.216 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.01667 \times 0.996}{0.0080}$$

Numerator:

$$1.216 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.01667 \;=\; 2.027 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$2.027 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.996 \;=\; 2.019 \times 10^{-5}$$

Denominator = 0.0080. Therefore:

$$Re_p \;=\; \dfrac{2.019 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0080} \;=\; 2.524 \times 10^{-3}$$

Since **Reₚ ≈ 2.5 × 10⁻³ ≪ 0.2**, Stokes' Law is **valid**.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Multi-Fraction Decantation Schedule

**Problem:**
For the same quartz/water system, design a four-cut decantation schedule targeting maximum sizes d₁ = 30 µm, d₂ = 20 µm, d₁ = 12 µm, d₄ = 6 µm. Settling depth h = 10 cm. Find the times `t₁, t₂, t₃, t₄`.

**Master time formula (from Stokes' Law):**

$$t_i \;=\; \dfrac{18\,\mu\,h}{d_i^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g}$$

Compute the common numerator once:

$$K \;=\; 18 \times 0.0080 \times 10 \;=\; 1.440\,\text{g/cm·s}^2$$

Common denominator constant:

$$L \;=\; (2.65 - 0.996) \times 981 \;=\; 1622.574\,\text{g/cm}^2\text{·s}^2$$

**Cut 1: d₁ = 30 µm = 30 × 10⁻⁴ cm = 3.0 × 10⁻³ cm**

$$d_1^{\,2} \;=\; 9.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{cm}^2$$

$$t_1 \;=\; \dfrac{1.440}{9.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 1622.574} \;=\; \dfrac{1.440}{0.014603} \;=\; 98.6\,\text{s}$$

**Cut 2: d₂ = 20 µm = 2.0 × 10⁻³ cm**

$$d_2^{\,2} \;=\; 4.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{cm}^2$$

$$t_2 \;=\; \dfrac{1.440}{4.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 1622.574} \;=\; \dfrac{1.440}{0.006490} \;=\; 221.9\,\text{s}$$

**Cut 3: d₃ = 12 µm = 1.2 × 10⁻³ cm**

$$d_3^{\,2} \;=\; 1.44 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{cm}^2$$

$$t_3 \;=\; \dfrac{1.440}{1.44 \times 10^{-6} \times 1622.574} \;=\; \dfrac{1.440}{0.002336} \;=\; 616.4\,\text{s}$$

**Cut 4: d₄ = 6 µm = 6.0 × 10⁻⁴ cm**

$$d_4^{\,2} \;=\; 3.6 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{cm}^2$$

$$t_4 \;=\; \dfrac{1.440}{3.6 \times 10^{-7} \times 1622.574} \;=\; \dfrac{1.440}{5.841 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; 2465\,\text{s} \approx 41.1\,\text{min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Settling time scales **inversely with the square of the diameter**. Halving the cut size quadruples the wait. This is why **beaker decantation is impractical below ~5 µm** — wait times balloon into hours, and the assumption of undisturbed quiescence breaks down (thermal convection kicks in).

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Method

| **Property** | **Beaker Decantation** | **Andreasen Pipette** | **Sedimentation Balance** | **Laser Diffraction** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Lower size limit | ≈ 5 µm | ≈ 1 µm | ≈ 1 µm | ≈ 0.04 µm |
| Accuracy | Low–moderate | High | High | Very high |
| Cost | Very low | Moderate | High | Very high |
| Operator skill | Basic | Moderate | Moderate | Low |
| Sample mass required | 10–50 g | 5–10 g | 1–5 g | < 1 g |
| Time for full distribution | 1–3 h | 4–24 h | Continuous | < 1 min |
| Principle | Stokes' decant | Stokes' withdrawal | Stokes' cumulative weight | Mie scattering |
| KTU typical use | **Pedagogical prototype** | Lab experiment | Reference method | Industrial quality control |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Beaker Decantation Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Weigh Dry Powder Sample] --> A1[Disperse in Liquid in Tall Beaker]
    A1 --> A2[Add Dispersing Agent and Stir Well]
    A2 --> A3[Stop Stirrer and Start Timer]
    A3 --> A4{Wait Predetermined Time t1 for Cut d1}
    A4 --> A5[Decant Supernatant into Container F1]
    A5 --> A6[F1 holds all particles smaller than d1]
    A5 --> A7[Refill Beaker with Fresh Liquid]
    A7 --> A8[Stir and Allow to Settle for Time t2]
    A8 --> A9[Decant Supernatant into Container F2]
    A9 --> A10[F2 holds particles between d2 and d1]
    A9 --> A11[Repeat Refill and Decant for Cuts d3 d4 up to dn]
    A11 --> A12[Filter Each Fraction Fi]
    A12 --> A13[Dry in Oven to Constant Mass]
    A13 --> A14[Weigh Each Fraction Wi]
    A14 --> A15[Compute Mass Fractions and Cumulative Distribution]
    A15 --> A16[Plot Cumulative Undersize vs Particle Diameter]
    A16 --> A17[Report d10 d50 d90 from Distribution]

    classDef decision fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#336699,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#339933,color:#000

    class A4 decision
    class A0,A1,A2,A3,A5,A7,A8,A9,A11,A12,A13,A14,A15,A16 process
    class A6,A10,A17 output
```

### 4.2 Force-Balance Schematic on a Single Settling Particle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Rigid Spherical Particle Diameter dp Density rhop] --> P2[Gravitational Force Fg downward]
    P2 --> P3[Net Driving Force equals Fg minus Fb]
    P4[Fluid of Density rof and Viscosity mu] --> P5[Buoyant Force Fb upward]
    P5 --> P3
    P3 --> P6[Viscous Drag Force Fd equals 3 pi mu dp vt upward]
    P6 --> P7{Terminal Condition Fg equals Fb plus Fd}
    P7 --> P8[Solve for vt equals dp squared times rhop minus rof times g divided by 18 mu]

    classDef physics fill:#f9e6ff,stroke:#660066,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef boundary fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc3300,color:#000

    class P1,P4,P2,P5,P6 physics
    class P3,P7 boundary
    class P8 result
```

### 4.3 Suspension Stratification During a Decantation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Liquid Surface Height h above Sediment]
    L2[Zone A: Particles larger than dmax have settled below h in time t]
    L3[Zone B: Particles smaller than dmax are still in suspension in zone of height h]
    L4[Sediment Bed at Bottom contains all particles greater than dmax]
    L5[Decant Line at depth h collects only Zone B liquid]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5

    classDef surface fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef zone fill:#fffbe6,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    classDef bed fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000

    class L1 surface
    class L2,L3 zone
    class L4 bed
    class L5 action
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:**
> The horizontal "decant line" physically separates the supernatant (which we keep) from the sediment (which stays in the beaker for the next cycle). The depth `h` from the suspension surface to this line is the `h` that appears in the Stokes' equation above.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
*Define "sub-sieve particle size analysis." Why is ordinary sieving inadequate below 44 µm?*

**Model Answer:**
Sub-sieve particle size analysis refers to the group of indirect techniques used to determine the size distribution of solid particles finer than 44 µm (the smallest aperture in standard sieve series, ASTM 325 mesh).

Ordinary sieving fails below this size because: (i) the woven wires of 325-mesh screens are extremely fragile and blind easily, (ii) particles in this range tend to agglomerate due to surface forces (van der Waals, electrostatic), and (iii) the apertures themselves become non-uniform at the 5 µm scale. Hence, **sedimentation-based methods** (beaker decantation, pipette, balance) relying on **Stokes' Law** are used instead. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
*State Stokes' Law. What is the upper limit of the particle Reynolds number for its validity?*

**Model Answer:**
Stokes' Law states that the terminal settling velocity of a small rigid sphere in a viscous fluid is:

$$v_t = \dfrac{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g}{18\,\mu}$$

It is valid in the **laminar (viscous) regime** where the particle Reynolds number satisfies:

$$Re_p \;=\; \dfrac{d_p\,v_t\,\rho_f}{\mu} \;<\; 0.2$$

Above this value, the drag coefficient departs from the Stokes regime and the simplified inverse-linear relationship breaks down. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> In this experiment, students most often lose marks because they: (a) **forget to verify Reₚ < 0.2** at the end of a calculation, (b) **mix SI and CGS units** mid-equation, (c) **omit the dispersing agent step** when describing the procedure, and (d) **fail to express the answer in µm** when the question expects it. Each missing item typically costs **1–2 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]

**(a)** Describe the principle, procedure, and precautions involved in the determination of sub-sieve particle size distribution by the **beaker decantation method**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A suspension of limestone powder (ρₚ = 2.70 g/cm³) in water at 30 °C (ρf = 0.996 g/cm³, µ = 0.0080 poise) is decanted from a settling depth h = 12 cm after a settling time t = 1200 s. Calculate (i) the diameter of the largest particle present in the decanted supernatant, and (ii) verify that Stokes' Law is applicable. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
The method is based on **Stokes' Law** for the terminal settling velocity of a sphere in a viscous fluid. By allowing a dilute suspension to settle for a *calculated* time `t`, all particles larger than a *predetermined* diameter `dₘₐₓ` settle below a *predetermined* height `h`. The supernatant is decanted, retaining only the fines. Repeating the cycle with progressively shorter settling times partitions the powder into size fractions.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Weigh a representative dry sample of powder (W₁) — typically 20–50 g.
2. Transfer to a 1 L tall-form beaker; add ~500 mL of water and a few drops of dispersing agent (sodium hexametaphosphate).
3. Stir vigorously to disperse and de-agglomerate; immediately stop and start a stopwatch.
4. Allow the suspension to stand for time `t₁` corresponding to the largest cut diameter `d₁`.
5. Carefully decant the supernatant at the original liquid level into a clean beaker; this contains all particles smaller than `d₁`.
6. Refill the original beaker with fresh water to the same level, stir, allow to settle for time `t₂ < t₁`, and decant again into another labelled beaker. Repeat for cuts d₂, d₃, d₄.
7. Filter each fraction through pre-weighed filter paper, dry in an oven at 110 °C to constant mass, cool in a desiccator, and weigh (W₂, W₃, …).
8. Compute mass fractions `xᵢ = Wᵢ / W₁ × 100%` and construct cumulative undersize curve.

**Precautions (2 marks):**
- Maintain **quiescent conditions**; no vibration, no drafts, no temperature gradients.
- Always **verify the Stokes regime** by computing Reₚ at the end.
- Use **consistent units** throughout (CGS recommended).
- **Calibrate the settling depth** `h` accurately after each refill.
- Add **dispersing agent** to prevent agglomeration, which would otherwise mimic coarser particles.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Largest diameter in supernatant:**

$$d_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\,\mu\,h}{(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g\,t}}$$

Substituting:

$$d_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \times 0.0080 \times 12}{(2.70 - 0.996) \times 981 \times 1200}}$$

Numerator:

$$18 \times 0.0080 \times 12 = 1.728$$

Denominator:

$$(2.70 - 0.996) = 1.704$$

$$1.704 \times 981 = 1671.624$$

$$1671.624 \times 1200 = 2.006 \times 10^{6}$$

Therefore:

$$d_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.728}{2.006 \times 10^{6}}} = \sqrt{8.614 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$d_{\max} = 9.281 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{cm} = 9.28\,\mu\text{m}$$

**(ii) Verification of Stokes' regime:**

$$v_t = \dfrac{h}{t} = \dfrac{12}{1200} = 0.010\,\text{cm/s}$$

$$Re_p = \dfrac{d_p\,v_t\,\rho_f}{\mu} = \dfrac{9.281 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.010 \times 0.996}{0.0080}$$

$$Re_p = \dfrac{9.244 \times 10^{-6}}{0.0080} = 1.156 \times 10^{-3}$$

Since Reₚ ≈ **1.16 × 10⁻³ ≪ 0.2**, Stokes' Law is valid. **(7 marks — distributed: 2 marks formula, 3 marks substitution + arithmetic, 2 marks verification)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, explain how the **beaker decantation** experiment is conducted. Discuss the **limitations** of this method. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A coal powder sample (ρₚ = 1.40 g/cm³) is suspended in water at 25 °C (ρf = 0.997 g/cm³, µ = 0.00894 poise). The settling depth is h = 15 cm. Calculate the time required to settle out particles larger than (i) 40 µm and (ii) 10 µm. Comment on the practicality of isolating the 10 µm fraction. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Flow Diagram (3 marks):** (See Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**Methodology description (2 marks):**
Weighed sample → dispersion with dispersing agent → stir thoroughly → quiescent settling for pre-calculated time → decant at fixed depth `h` → refill → repeat → filter, dry, weigh each fraction → cumulative distribution.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Time-consuming** for fine fractions (sizes < 5 µm may require hours).
2. **Assumes free settling** — cannot handle concentrated slurries (hindered settling).
3. **Sensitive to convection currents** in the beaker caused by temperature gradients.
4. **Agglomeration** between particles of dissimilar size biases the cut.
5. **Decant depth** is operator-dependent and affects the cut size.
6. **Stokes' Law validity** must be re-checked for each cut.
7. **Inadequate reproducibility** compared to pipette or balance methods.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Common master equation:

$$t = \dfrac{18\,\mu\,h}{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g}$$

Common numerator:

$$18 \times 0.00894 \times 15 = 2.4138$$

Common denominator constant:

$$(1.40 - 0.997) \times 981 = 0.403 \times 981 = 395.343$$

**(i) For dₚ = 40 µm = 4 × 10⁻³ cm:**

$$d_p^{\,2} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{cm}^2$$

$$t_1 = \dfrac{2.4138}{1.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 395.343}$$

Denominator:

$$1.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 395.343 = 6.325 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$t_1 = \dfrac{2.4138}{6.325 \times 10^{-3}} = 381.6\,\text{s} \approx 6.36\,\text{min}$$

**(ii) For dₚ = 10 µm = 1 × 10⁻³ cm:**

$$d_p^{\,2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{cm}^2$$

$$t_2 = \dfrac{2.4138}{1.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 395.343}$$

Denominator:

$$1.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 395.343 = 3.953 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$t_2 = \dfrac{2.4138}{3.953 \times 10^{-4}} = 6105\,\text{s} \approx 101.8\,\text{min} \approx 1\,\text{h}\,42\,\text{min}$$

**Comment on practicality:**
While theoretically possible, isolating the **10 µm** fraction requires maintaining a *completely vibration-free* environment for over **100 minutes**. Thermal convection in the beaker is virtually guaranteed over such long intervals, which would disturb the sedimentation column and invalidate the Stokes assumption. Therefore, beaker decantation for sizes below ~5 µm is **impractical**, and methods like the Andreasen pipette or sedimentation balance are preferred. **(7 marks — 2 marks setup, 2 marks each sub-part, 1 mark discussion, 2 marks for units and Re verification)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sub-sieve** = particles finer than **44 µm** (below 325 mesh), where ordinary sieving fails.
- **Beaker decantation** = simplest Stokes-based sedimentation method; physically partitions powder into size fractions by **timed decanting**.
- **Master equation:** $d_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\,\mu\,h}{(\rho_p-\rho_f)\,g\,t}}$ — keep this on your formula sheet.
- **Settling time scales inversely with d²**, so halving the cut size quadruples the wait — this is why fine fractions are impractical in a beaker.
- **Stokes' regime validity:** always verify **Reₚ < 0.2** at the end of every calculation; forgetting this is the most common valuation penalty.
- **Dispersing agent** (sodium hexametaphosphate, sodium silicate, ~0.1 % w/v) is essential to break agglomerates.
- **Use consistent units** throughout (CGS: cm, g, s, poise is the safest choice in this experiment).
- **Settling depth `h`** must be carefully measured after each refill, because errors in `h` propagate directly as errors in `dₘₐₓ`.
- **Drying protocol:** filter each fraction, dry at **110 °C** to constant mass, cool in a **desiccator** before weighing.
- **Result presentation:** plot **cumulative % undersize vs d** on semi-log paper; report **d₁₀, d₅₀, d₉₀** as the standard descriptor set.
- **Limitations:** time-consuming, agglomeration-sensitive, prone to convection below 5 µm, free-settling assumption.
- **Industrial relevance:** cement, ceramics, pharmaceutical APIs, mineral processing, soil mechanics — virtually every powdered-material industry needs sub-sieve analysis.
- **Predecessor methods:** Andreasen pipette (withdrawal of fixed volume at fixed depth), sedimentation balance (continuous cumulative weighing); both are *mathematical descendants* of the beaker decantation principle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sub-sieve Particle Size Analysis using Pipette Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL408 - FLUID FLOW & PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Sub-sieve Particle Size Analysis using Pipette Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipette Analysis** (also known as **Andreasen Pipette Method**) is a **sedimentation-based, gravimetric technique** used to determine the **particle size distribution** of fine powders whose particle diameter is **less than 75 µm** (i.e., sub-sieve range, falling below the resolution limit of standard woven-wire sieves).

The technique is governed fundamentally by **Stokes' Law**, which relates the terminal settling velocity of a spherical particle in a viscous fluid to the square of its diameter. By drawing out small aliquots of suspension from a fixed depth at predetermined time intervals and measuring the mass of solids remaining in suspension, the cumulative undersize mass fraction corresponding to a specific equivalent Stokes diameter is determined.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The Pipette Method is classified under **sedimentation methods of particle size analysis**, which also include the Hydrometer Method and the Sedimentation Balance. It is the **reference standard (bench-mark method)** against which other sub-sieve techniques are often validated in the chemical, pharmaceutical, ceramic, and mineral processing industries.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Settling Pond"

Imagine a **deep, perfectly still rainwater pond** in which a handful of mixed gravel, sand, and fine clay has been dispersed uniformly. Now watch carefully:

- **Large stones (big particles)** sink to the bottom almost **instantaneously** because gravity acts strongly on their heavy mass.
- **Medium sand grains (medium particles)** drift down **slowly**, taking minutes to reach the floor.
- **Fine clay (small particles)** remain suspended for **hours**, barely moving downward.

If you were to **scoop a small water sample from a specific depth, say 10 cm below the surface, after 5 minutes**, you would find:
- **All stones and large sand** are already **gone** (they have settled past your sampling depth).
- **Only the medium and fine particles** that haven't yet settled to your depth are present.

By **weighing** the captured solids at various times and depths, you can mathematically **back-calculate** which size particles were still floating at that moment — and hence, what fraction of the sample is smaller than a particular size.

> **This is exactly what the Andreasen Pipette does — mechanically, precisely, and quantitatively.**

---

## 1.3 Standard Reference Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water (at 20 °C) | $\rho_f$ | **998.2 kg/m³** |
| Dynamic viscosity of water (at 20 °C) | $\mu$ | **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** |
| Sub-sieve range | $d_p$ | **< 75 µm (often 1–75 µm)** |
| Typical sampling depth | $h$ | **0.10 m (10 cm)** |
| Maximum measurable size | $d_{max}$ | **~ 75 µm** |
| Minimum measurable size | $d_{min}$ | **~ 1 µm** (limited by Brownian motion) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Principle — Stokes' Law

When a single spherical particle falls under gravity through a quiescent viscous fluid, three forces act on it:

1. **Gravitational (weight) force** acting downward: $F_g = V_p \, \rho_s \, g = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{3} \rho_s \, g$
2. **Buoyancy force** acting upward: $F_b = V_p \, \rho_f \, g = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{3} \rho_f \, g$
3. **Drag force** (Stokes) acting upward: $F_D = 3 \pi \, \mu \, d_p \, v$

At **terminal (constant) velocity**, the net force becomes zero:

$$F_g - F_b - F_D = 0$$

Substituting and solving for the settling velocity $v_t$ yields the famous **Stokes' Law**:

$$v_t = \frac{d_p^{2} \, (\rho_s - \rho_f) \, g}{18 \, \mu}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Criterion (Reynolds Number Re ≤ 0.2):** Stokes' Law is **strictly valid only for laminar, viscous flow** around the particle. This sets the upper bound of approximately **75 µm** for typical solid-water systems. Above this, turbulent/eddy effects invalidate the assumption.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

1. A known mass of dry powder ($M_0$) is uniformly dispersed in a known volume of liquid (often water with a dispersing agent like sodium hexametaphosphate).
2. The uniform suspension is poured into a **graduated settling cylinder** (Andreasen cylinder).
3. The cylinder is agitated, then left **completely undisturbed**. Sedimentation begins at $t = 0$.
4. At pre-selected times $t_1, t_2, t_3, \dots$, the **pipette is inserted to a fixed sampling depth $h$** (measured from the liquid surface downward), and a small fixed volume $V$ (typically 10 mL) is withdrawn.
5. Each withdrawn sample is **evaporated to dryness** and weighed ($m_i$) to determine the **concentration of solids still in suspension** at that depth and time.
6. Since larger particles settle faster, the **concentration decreases with time**. The residual mass $m_i$ corresponds to all particles with settling velocity $\leq v_t = h/t_i$, i.e., all particles **smaller than the Stokes diameter $d_i$**.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

The pipette method is the **workhorse technique** in industries where fine powders are critical:

- **Cement & ceramics:** Blaine fineness, particle packing, hydration kinetics.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Drug bioavailability is strongly dependent on particle size (sub-25 µm fractions).
- **Pigments & paints:** Opacity and tinting strength depend on sub-sieve distribution.
- **Soil science & geology:** USDA/Wentworth scale classifications rely on sedimentation data.
- **Mineral processing:** Liberation analysis of ores for flotation feed.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the only ones you need for any numerical problem on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_t = \dfrac{d_p^{2}(\rho_s - \rho_f)g}{18\mu}$ | **Stokes' Law** — settling velocity |
| 2 | $v_t = \dfrac{h}{t}$ | Settling velocity = depth / time |
| 3 | $d_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \, \mu \, h}{(\rho_s - \rho_f) \, g \, t}}$ | **Equivalent Stokes diameter** (working formula) |
| 4 | $C_0 = \dfrac{M_0}{V_{\text{total}}}$ | Initial uniform concentration of suspension |
| 5 | $\dfrac{M_i}{M_0} = \dfrac{c_i}{c_0}$ | Mass fraction remaining at time $t_i$ |
| 6 | $F(d_i) = \dfrac{m_i - m_\infty}{m_0 - m_\infty}$ | Cumulative **undersize mass fraction** below $d_i$ |
| 7 | $\Delta F = F(d_{i-1}) - F(d_i)$ | Mass fraction in size interval $[d_i, \, d_{i-1}]$ |
| 8 | $Re_p = \dfrac{d_p \, v_t \, \rho_f}{\mu} \leq 0.2$ | **Stokes' Law validity check** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units — Use SI throughout:** $d_p$ in metres (m), $\mu$ in Pa·s, $h$ in m, $t$ in s, $g = 9.81$ m/s², densities in kg/m³. Converting from cgs (cm, g, poise) to SI is a common exam trap.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Working Particle-Size Equation

Starting from the **force balance** on a single spherical particle at terminal velocity in a quiescent fluid:

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^{3} \, \rho_s \, g - \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^{3} \, \rho_f \, g - 3 \pi \, \mu \, d_p \, v_t = 0$$

**Step 1 — Factor out the common term $\dfrac{\pi d_p^{3}}{6} g$:**

$$\frac{\pi \, d_p^{3} \, g}{6} \left( \rho_s - \rho_f \right) - 3 \pi \, \mu \, d_p \, v_t = 0$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate $v_t$:**

$$3 \pi \, \mu \, d_p \, v_t = \frac{\pi \, d_p^{3} \, g}{6} \left( \rho_s - \rho_f \right)$$

$$v_t = \frac{d_p^{2} \, (\rho_s - \rho_f) \, g}{18 \, \mu}$$

This is **Stokes' Law** — confirmed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Particle Diameter from Pipette Data

In the Andreasen cylinder, a particle initially at the **top surface** that is **just about to fall past the sampling depth $h$** at time $t$ must have a settling velocity equal to $h/t$.

**Step 1 — Express settling velocity geometrically:**

$$v_t = \frac{h}{t}$$

**Step 2 — Equate with Stokes' Law:**

$$\frac{h}{t} = \frac{d_p^{2} \, (\rho_s - \rho_f) \, g}{18 \, \mu}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d_p$:**

$$d_p^{2} = \frac{18 \, \mu \, h}{(\rho_s - \rho_f) \, g \, t}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_p = \sqrt{\frac{18 \, \mu \, h}{(\rho_s - \rho_f) \, g \, t}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Every particle with a Stokes diameter **smaller than $d_p$** at time $t$ and depth $h$ is **still present in the withdrawn sample**. Hence, the mass measured represents the **cumulative undersize fraction** below that diameter.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Cumulative Mass Fraction $F(d_p)$

**Step 1 — Define the initial concentration.** When $t = 0$, the suspension is perfectly uniform:

$$c_0 = \frac{M_0}{V_{\text{total}}}$$

**Step 2 — At time $t_i$, particles larger than $d_i$** (where $d_i = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \mu h}{(\rho_s - \rho_f) g t_i}}$) **have already settled below depth $h$**. They are no longer in the pipetted sample.

**Step 3 — The pipette withdraws a volume $V_p$** at depth $h$. The mass of solids in this volume is:

$$m_i = c_i \, V_p$$

where $c_i$ is the concentration of solids at depth $h$ at time $t_i$.

**Step 4 — The ratio of concentration at time $t_i$ to initial concentration** equals the mass fraction of all particles smaller than $d_i$:

$$\frac{m_i}{M_0 / V_{\text{total}} \cdot V_p} = \frac{m_i \cdot V_{\text{total}}}{M_0 \cdot V_p}$$

After algebraic simplification (and noting $c_i/c_0$ is the appropriate ratio for the same withdrawal volume):

$$\boxed{\,F(d_i) = \frac{m_i}{m_0} \times 100 \, (\%) \,}$$

where $m_0$ is the mass withdrawn at $t = 0$ (representing the full particle population).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mass of solids remaining** = mass of solids **smaller than** $d_p$ (since larger ones have already fallen below the pipette tip).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

**Given:**
- Particle density: $\rho_s = 2650 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (quartz sand)
- Fluid: water at 20 °C, $\rho_f = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$
- Sampling depth: $h = 0.10$ m
- Time of withdrawal: $t = 300$ s
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Find:** Equivalent Stokes diameter $d_p$.

**Solution:**

$$d_p = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.10}{(2650 - 998) \times 9.81 \times 300}}$$

**Step 1 — Compute numerator:**

$$18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.10 = 1.8036 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator:**

$$(2650 - 998) \times 9.81 \times 300 = 1652 \times 9.81 \times 300$$

$$= 1652 \times 2943 = 4.8618 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio and square root:**

$$d_p^{2} = \frac{1.8036 \times 10^{-3}}{4.8618 \times 10^{6}} = 3.7098 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^2$$

$$d_p = \sqrt{3.7098 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.926 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_p \approx 19.26 \, \mu\text{m}\,}$$

---

## 3.5 Verification — Stokes' Law Validity Check

$$Re_p = \frac{d_p \, v_t \, \rho_f}{\mu}$$

$$v_t = \frac{h}{t} = \frac{0.10}{300} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$$

$$Re_p = \frac{(1.926 \times 10^{-5})(3.333 \times 10^{-4})(998)}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$= \frac{6.407 \times 10^{-6}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.394 \times 10^{-3}$$

Since $Re_p = 6.4 \times 10^{-3} \ll 0.2$, **Stokes' Law is fully valid** ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of the Andreasen Pipette Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Powder Sample] --> B[Weigh and Record M0]
    B --> C[Disperse in Liquid with Dispersant]
    C --> D[Transfer to Andreasen Cylinder]
    D --> E[Shake Thoroughly for Uniformity]
    E --> F[Place on Vibration-Free Stand]
    F --> G[Start Stopwatch t equals 0]
    G --> H{Time Check t equals ti}
    H --> I[Insert Pipette to Depth h]
    I --> J[Withdraw Fixed Volume Vp]
    J --> K[Transfer to Pre-weighed Dish]
    K --> L[Evaporate to Dryness in Oven]
    L --> M[Weigh Residue mi]
    M --> N[Calculate dp via Stokes Equation]
    N --> O[Compute Mass Fraction Fi]
    O --> P[Plot Cumulative Undersize Curve]
    P --> Q[Determine d10, d50, d90]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#fff4e1
    style N fill:#e8f5e9
    style P fill:#fce4ec
    style Q fill:#f3e5f5
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Calculation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Raw Data: ti, mi] --> R2[Compute dp at each ti]
    R2 --> R3[Sort dp in Descending Order]
    R3 --> R4[Compute Cumulative Fi equals mi over m0]
    R4 --> R5[Tabulate dp versus Fi]
    R5 --> R6[Plot on Semi-Log Graph]
    R6 --> R7[Read Distribution Parameters]

    style R1 fill:#e3f2fd
    style R7 fill:#fff9c4
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic of the Andreasen Pipette Cylinder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CYL[Andreasen Settling Cylinder]
        TOP[Liquid Surface Mark] --> DOWN[Settling Zone]
        DOWN --> TIP[Pipette Tip at Depth h]
        TIP --> BOTTOM[Graduated Base]
    end

    subgraph PROC[Sedimentation Stages]
        STG1[Stage 1: t small, only large particles settled]
        STG2[Stage 2: t medium, medium particles settled]
        STG3[Stage 3: t large, only fines remain at depth h]
    end

    CYL --> PROC

    style CYL fill:#e0f7fa
    style PROC fill:#f1f8e9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual reading guide:** As time increases, progressively smaller particles fall past the pipette tip. Therefore, the **mass measured at later times is smaller**, and the cumulative mass fraction $F$ **decreases monotonically** with decreasing $d_p$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]

**State Stokes' Law. Mention the assumptions and the validity criterion under which it is applicable.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Stokes' Law:** The terminal settling velocity of a spherical particle falling under gravity through a viscous fluid is given by
>
> $$v_t = \frac{d_p^{2}(\rho_s - \rho_f)g}{18\mu}$$
>
> **Assumptions:**
> 1. Particle is **rigid, smooth, and spherical**.
> 2. Particle falls in a **quiescent, infinite medium** (no wall effects).
> 3. Flow around the particle is **laminar and streamline** (no wake or turbulence).
> 4. Particle is **large enough** that Brownian motion is negligible.
> 5. **No slip** at the particle-fluid interface.
>
> **Validity Criterion:** The **Particle Reynolds Number must be $\leq 0.2$** (i.e., $Re_p = \dfrac{d_p v_t \rho_f}{\mu} \leq 0.2$).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]

**Differentiate between sieve analysis and pipette analysis. In which particle-size range is each technique preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Sieve Analysis | Pipette Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Mechanical separation by aperture size | Sedimentation under gravity (Stokes' Law) |
| Size range | **> 75 µm** (coarse) | **< 75 µm** (fine / sub-sieve) |
| Method | Weighing retained fraction on each sieve | Withdrawing aliquots at depth $h$, time $t$ |
| Time required | 15–30 min | Several hours to days |
| Limitation | Cannot resolve below 75 µm | Requires uniform dispersion, temperature control |
| Standards | IS 460 / ASTM E11 | IS 4333 / ASTM D422 |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2] — 14 Marks

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Derive the equation used in the Andreasen Pipette method for calculating the equivalent particle diameter from the measured depth and time of withdrawal. State clearly the assumptions made.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** In a pipette analysis experiment, the following data was obtained. Plot the cumulative undersize distribution curve and determine $d_{50}$ (median size).

| Time $t$ (min) | Mass of solids in 10 mL sample (mg) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 250 |
| 2 | 230 |
| 10 | 180 |
| 30 | 120 |
| 90 | 60 |
| 300 | 30 |

Take $\rho_s = 2600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_f = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$, $h = 0.10$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Derivation

**Assumptions:**
1. Particles are **spherical and smooth**.
2. **Laminar flow** regime around each particle ($Re_p \leq 0.2$).
3. Particles settle **independently** (dilute suspension, no hindered settling).
4. Cylinder walls have **negligible effect** on settling velocity.
5. Suspension is **uniform** at $t = 0$.
6. Temperature is **constant** throughout the experiment.

**Derivation:**

The terminal settling velocity from Stokes' Law:

$$v_t = \frac{d_p^{2}(\rho_s - \rho_f)g}{18\mu}$$

In the time $t$, a particle settles from the surface to depth $h$, so:

$$v_t = \frac{h}{t}$$

Equating both:

$$\frac{h}{t} = \frac{d_p^{2}(\rho_s - \rho_f)g}{18\mu}$$

Solving for $d_p$:

$$\boxed{\,d_p = \sqrt{\frac{18 \, \mu \, h}{t(\rho_s - \rho_f)g}}\,}$$

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate $d_p$ at each time** using the working formula:

$$d_p = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.10}{t \times (2600 - 1000) \times 9.81}}$$

$$d_p = \sqrt{\frac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{15691.8 \times t}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.1471 \times 10^{-7}}{t}}$$

**Step 2 — Tabulate** $d_p$ and $F = (m_i/m_0) \times 100$:

| $t$ (s) | $d_p$ (µm) | $m_i$ (mg) | $F$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | ∞ | 250 | 100.0 |
| 120 | 30.92 | 230 | 92.0 |
| 600 | 13.83 | 180 | 72.0 |
| 1800 | 7.98 | 120 | 48.0 |
| 5400 | 4.61 | 60 | 24.0 |
| 18000 | 2.52 | 30 | 12.0 |

**Step 3 — Plot** cumulative undersize % vs. $d_p$ (µm) on semi-log paper.

**Step 4 — Determine $d_{50}$:** From the curve, at $F = 50\%$, the corresponding particle size is read. By interpolation between $d = 7.98 \text{ µm}$ ($F = 48\%$) and $d = 4.61 \text{ µm}$ ($F = 24\%$), and assuming log-linear interpolation:

$$\log d_{50} = \log(7.98) + \frac{48 - 50}{48 - 24} \times [\log(4.61) - \log(7.98)]$$

$$= 0.9021 + \frac{-2}{24} \times [0.6636 - 0.9021]$$

$$= 0.9021 - \frac{2}{24} \times (-0.2385) = 0.9021 + 0.0199 = 0.9220$$

$$\boxed{\,d_{50} \approx 8.36 \, \mu\text{m}\,}$$

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2] — 14 Marks

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Describe with a neat labelled diagram the **Andreasen Pipette apparatus** and the experimental procedure to determine the particle size distribution of a fine powder.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Explain the **limitations of the pipette method**. Also state why dispersing agents are added to the suspension and name two commonly used ones.

---

#### Model Solution (a)

**Apparatus Description:**

The Andreasen Pipette consists of:

1. **Graduated glass cylinder** (≈ 500 mL capacity) with a stopper at the top to prevent evaporation.
2. **Pipette assembly** — a 10 mL pipette with a stopcock, mounted through the stopper, with its tip positioned at a **fixed depth $h$ = 10 cm** below the liquid surface.
3. **Sampling bulb** to collect the aliquot.
4. **Vibration-free, levelled stand** to ensure undisturbed sedimentation.
5. **Pre-weighed evaporating dishes** for drying samples.
6. **Stopwatch** for time recording.
7. **Oven / desiccator** for drying samples to constant weight.

**Experimental Procedure:**

1. **Weigh** an accurately known mass ($M_0 \approx 2$–5 g) of the oven-dried powder.
2. **Disperse** in about 500 mL of distilled water (with a few drops of dispersant like sodium hexametaphosphate).
3. **Transfer** to the Andreasen cylinder. Top up with water to the marked level.
4. **Agitate** by inverting the sealed cylinder 5–6 times to ensure uniform suspension.
5. **Place** the cylinder on the levelled, vibration-free stand. **Start the stopwatch immediately.**
6. **Withdraw 10 mL samples** at the pipette tip at preset times: typically 2, 5, 15, 30, 60, 120, 240, 480 min.
7. **Transfer** each sample to a pre-weighed dish, evaporate to dryness, cool in desiccator, and weigh.
8. **Record** time $t_i$, mass $m_i$, and calculate $d_p$ from the working formula.
9. **Plot** cumulative % undersize vs. $d_p$.

---

#### Model Solution (b)

**Limitations:**

1. **Time-consuming** — full experiment may require 8–24 hours.
2. **Temperature sensitivity** — viscosity $\mu$ changes with temperature, affecting results.
3. **Convection disturbances** — even small temperature gradients cause fluid currents that disrupt settling.
4. **Assumes spherical particles** — irregular particles give an "equivalent" diameter, not the true size.
5. **Concentration effects** — at high solids loading, **hindered settling** invalidates Stokes' Law.
6. **Lower limit** — particles below ~1 µm undergo **Brownian motion** and do not settle by Stokes' Law.
7. **Initial dispersion** — incomplete dispersion leads to errors (agglomerates settle as one).
8. **Decantation losses** during pipette withdrawal can introduce systematic error.

**Why Dispersing Agents Are Added:**

To **break down agglomerates** and ensure that each particle settles **independently** according to its own Stokes diameter. Without dispersion, fine particles clump together and settle as larger aggregates, giving **artificially large size values**.

**Two Common Dispersing Agents:**
1. **Sodium hexametaphosphate** (Calgon) — most widely used for soil and ceramic powders.
2. **Sodium silicate** (water glass) — used for clays and pigments.
3. *(Also acceptable: Sodium pyrophosphate, Sodium oxalate, Ammonium hydroxide.)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency** — Mixing cgs (cm, g, poise) with SI. Always convert to SI first. Mismatch in units is the **#1 cause of incorrect numerical answers** in KTU scripts.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify $Re_p \leq 0.2$** — Examiners specifically check whether you validated Stokes' Law applicability. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing "undersize" vs. "oversize"** — Cumulative undersize % *decreases* with decreasing $d_p$. Many students plot the curve inverted.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation** — In Part B, when derivation is asked, writing only the final formula earns **zero marks**. Always show force balance step-by-step.
> 5. **Not mentioning Brownian motion limit** — When asked about limitations, omitting the **lower size limit (~1 µm)** is a common omission.
> 6. **Drawing the apparatus without labelling** — Neat, **labelled** diagrams are compulsory for full marks in lab-based questions.

---

## Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin these in your memory before every KTU exam on this topic:**

- ✅ Pipette Analysis is the **standard sub-sieve (< 75 µm) sedimentation technique**.
- ✅ Based on **Stokes' Law** — derived from a balance of gravity, buoyancy, and viscous drag.
- ✅ Working formula: $\,d_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \mu h}{t(\rho_s - \rho_f)g}}\,$
- ✅ Settling velocity: $v_t = h/t$ — used to link geometry and time.
- ✅ Cumulative undersize fraction: $F = (m_i / m_0) \times 100$.
- ✅ Validity range: $Re_p \leq 0.2$ → $d_p \leq 75$ µm for water systems.
- ✅ Lower size limit: ~1 µm (Brownian motion sets in).
- ✅ **Dispersing agents** (e.g., sodium hexametaphosphate) are essential to prevent agglomeration.
- ✅ Plot **cumulative % undersize vs. $d_p$ on semi-log paper** for distribution curve.
- ✅ Read $d_{10}$, $d_{50}$, $d_{90}$ directly from the cumulative curve.
- ✅ Key parameters for design: $\rho_s, \rho_f, \mu, g, h, t$.
- ✅ Limitations include long duration, temperature sensitivity, spherical assumption, hindered settling, and Brownian motion at very fine sizes.
- ✅ Industrial relevance: cement, ceramics, pharmaceuticals, pigments, soils, mineral processing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Studies on Flocculation - Analysis of Orthokinetic and Perikinetic Aggregation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL408 - FLUID FLOW & PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Studies on Flocculation - Analysis of Orthokinetic and Perikinetic Aggregation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Flocculation?

**Flocculation** is a physico-chemical process in which finely dispersed colloidal or suspended particles in a liquid medium are brought into intimate contact, allowing them to agglomerate into larger, heavier masses called **flocs**. These flocs subsequently settle more rapidly under gravity, facilitating solid–liquid separation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Flocculation is the process of aggregation of destabilized colloidal particles into microflocs and then into larger settleable flocs through inter-particle collisions induced either by Brownian motion (Perikinetic) or by fluid shear / velocity gradients (Orthokinetic).*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sticky Dust" Picture

Imagine a glass of muddy water. The mud particles are so tiny they refuse to settle. Now:
- **Leave it alone (no stirring):** The particles jiggle randomly on their own, occasionally bumping into each other and clumping — this is **Perikinetic flocculation** (Brownian-motion-driven).
- **Gently stir with a spoon:** Fluid currents sweep particles into each other, dramatically increasing the clumping rate — this is **Orthokinetic flocculation** (velocity-gradient-driven).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The destabilized particles must first be **charge-neutralized** by a **coagulant** (e.g., alum, FeCl₃) before flocculation can occur. Coagulation → Flocculation → Sedimentation is the standard train of unit operations in water treatment.

## 1.3 The Two Regimes of Aggregation

| Regime | Driving Force | Occurs In | Typical Particle Size | Rate Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Perikinetic** | Brownian (thermal) motion | Stagnant fluid | $d \le 1\,\mu m$ | Very slow (hours–days) |
| **Orthokinetic** | Induced fluid velocity gradient (shear) | Mechanically stirred fluid | $d > 1\,\mu m$ | Faster (minutes) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *Peri* = *Peripheral random walk*; *Ortho* = *Ordered, directional flow*.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

- Boltzmann constant: **k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- Absolute viscosity of water at 20 °C: **μ = 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
- Density of water: **ρ = 998.2 kg/m³**
- Collision efficiency factor: **α (0 ≤ α ≤ 1)**
- Velocity gradient: **G, s⁻¹**
- Camp–Stein number (dimensionless): **G·t**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Smoluchowski Framework

Both perikinetic and orthokinetic aggregation rates are derived from the classical **Smoluchowski collision theory (1917)**, which assumes:
1. Particles are spherical, monodisperse.
2. Every collision produces a successful aggregate (collision efficiency α = 1).
3. Fluid flow is laminar and isotropic.

The general **rate of disappearance of particles** is written as:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -\frac{1}{2} \cdot K \cdot N^2$$

where $N$ = number concentration of particles (m⁻³), and $K$ = **collision frequency function** (m³/s), which differs fundamentally between the two regimes.

## 2.2 Perikinetic Aggregation — Brownian-Motion Driven

### Step-by-Step Logic
- Each particle of diameter $d$ acts as a "sphere of influence" of radius $\lambda$ (the mean free diffusion path).
- Brownian diffusivity follows the **Stokes–Einstein equation**: $D = \dfrac{kT}{3\pi\mu d}$.
- Collision frequency for a target particle = flux of other particles into its sphere of influence.
- Integrating over a population of $N$ particles yields the perikinetic rate constant:

$$K_P = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{kT}{\mu}$$

### Key Observation
> [!NOTE]
> Strikingly, the perikinetic collision frequency $K_P$ is **independent of particle size $d$**. It depends only on temperature and viscosity. This is the mathematical reason why Brownian flocculation is only meaningful for sub-micron colloids — the number density of such particles in a real suspension is enormous, but the absolute rate is still small.

## 2.3 Orthokinetic Aggregation — Velocity-Gradient Driven

### Step-by-Step Logic
- Under laminar shear, particles follow approximately linear streamlines separated by the local velocity gradient $G = \dfrac{du}{dy}$ (s⁻¹).
- Two particles collide when their centre-to-centre distance in the gradient direction is ≤ $d$.
- Volume swept per unit time per particle = $\dfrac{4}{3} G d^3$.
- The orthokinetic rate constant is:

$$K_O = \frac{4}{3} \cdot G \cdot d^3$$

### Key Observation
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Unlike perikinetic flocculation, the orthokinetic rate is **cubic in particle diameter** ($d^3$). Hence, as flocs grow, orthokinetic aggregation accelerates — the process is **autocatalytic** until breakup forces balance aggregation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{kT}{3\pi\mu d}$ | Brownian diffusivity | m²/s | Perikinetic |
| 2 | $K_P = \dfrac{4}{3} \cdot \dfrac{kT}{\mu}$ | Perikinetic rate constant | m³/s | All $d$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{dN}{dt}\bigg\vert_P = -\dfrac{1}{2} K_P N^2 = -\dfrac{2kT}{3\mu} N^2$ | Perikinetic disappearance rate | m⁻³·s⁻¹ | $d \le 1\,\mu m$ |
| 4 | $K_O = \dfrac{4}{3} G d^3$ | Orthokinetic rate constant | m³/s | Laminar shear |
| 5 | $\dfrac{dN}{dt}\bigg\vert_O = -\dfrac{1}{2} K_O N^2 = -\dfrac{2}{3} G d^3 N^2$ | Orthokinetic disappearance rate | m⁻³·s⁻¹ | $d > 1\,\mu m$ |
| 6 | $G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu \cdot V}}$ | Mean velocity gradient (Camp–Stein) | s⁻¹ | Turbulent stirred tank |
| 7 | $P = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\rho \, N_p^3 \, D_i^5}{g_c}$ | Power input to impeller | W | Paddle/turbine |
| 8 | $N_t = \dfrac{N_0}{1 + \dfrac{1}{2} K N_0 t}$ | Particle count at time $t$ (α = 1) | m⁻³ | Smoluchowski |
| 9 | $G \cdot t = \dfrac{4 V}{\pi Q} \left( h_0 - h_t \right)$ | Camp dimensionless number | — | Settling jar test |
| 10 | $\alpha = \dfrac{K_{exp}}{K_{theory}}$ | Collision efficiency factor | dimensionless | Comparison |

> [!WARNING]
> In Markdown tables, the absolute-value bars have been written as `\vert` (and `\vert dN/dt \vert`) to avoid breaking the table cell syntax. Do not use `|` inside a table cell.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water & Wastewater Treatment Plants:** Optimizing coagulant/flocculant dose and $G$-value to maximize turbidity removal.
- **Mineral Beneficiation:** Selective flocculation of fine ores (e.g., iron ore, kaolin).
- **Pulp & Paper:** Retention of fines and fillers on the wire.
- **Pharmaceutical & Biotech:** Clarification of fermentation broths and cell harvesting.
- **Food Industry:** Yeast harvesting in brewing, casein aggregation in cheese making.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation — Perikinetic Rate Equation

### Start: Fick's First Law + Spherical Symmetry
The steady-state diffusive flux $J$ of particles toward a stationary target particle of diameter $d$ is given by Fick's law:

$$J = -D \frac{dn}{dr}$$

where $n(r)$ is the particle number concentration at radial distance $r$ from the target, and $D$ is the Brownian diffusivity.

### Boundary Conditions
- Far field: $n(r \to \infty) = N$
- Contact surface: $n(r = d) = 0$ (perfectly absorbing target)

### Solve the Diffusion Equation
At steady state, the total flux through every concentric sphere is constant, so:

$$J \cdot 4\pi r^2 = -D \frac{dn}{dr} \cdot 4\pi r^2 = \text{constant} = \Phi$$

### Integrate
Separate variables and integrate from $r = d$ to $r \to \infty$:

$$\int_0^N dn = -\frac{\Phi}{4\pi D} \int_d^\infty \frac{dr}{r^2}$$

$$N = -\frac{\Phi}{4\pi D} \left[ -\frac{1}{r} \right]_d^\infty = -\frac{\Phi}{4\pi D} \left( 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{d}\right) \right) = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi D d}$$

### Solve for Flux
$$\Phi = 4\pi D d \cdot N$$

### Substitute Stokes–Einstein Diffusivity
Using $D = \dfrac{kT}{3\pi\mu d}$:

$$\Phi = 4\pi \cdot \frac{kT}{3\pi\mu d} \cdot d \cdot N = \frac{4kT N}{3\mu}$$

### Total Rate of Collision
For $N$ target particles, the total disappearance rate is:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -\frac{1}{2} \Phi \cdot N = -\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{4kT N}{3\mu} \cdot N$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{dN}{dt} = -\frac{2kT}{3\mu} N^2 = -\frac{1}{2} K_P N^2\;}$$

where the factor $\dfrac{1}{2}$ prevents double-counting of each collision (Smoluchowski correction).

> [!NOTE]
> The rate constant is $K_P = \dfrac{4kT}{3\mu}$, which depends **only** on absolute temperature $T$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$ — not on particle size $d$.

## 3.2 Full Derivation — Orthokinetic Rate Equation

### Geometric Setup
Consider two particles of diameter $d$ in a laminar shear field with velocity gradient $G$ (s⁻¹). Place the target at origin. A second particle at position $(x, y, z)$ relative to the target moves with relative velocity $u = G y$ in the x-direction (Taylor's linearised flow field).

### Condition for Collision
A collision occurs if the second particle's trajectory passes within a centre-to-centre distance of $d$. The volume swept per unit time by a single particle moving through a uniform linear shear field is:

$$\dot{V}_{single} = \frac{4}{3} d^3 \cdot G$$

### Why the factor $\frac{4}{3}$?
Imagine the particle of diameter $d$ at the origin. It sweeps a cylinder of radius $d$ along its own velocity axis. However, because surrounding particles also move at different speeds depending on their $y$-position, the *relative* swept volume is bounded by a parallelepiped of cross-section $\frac{4}{3}d^2$ (not $\pi d^2$), so:

$$\dot{V}_{single} = \left(\frac{4}{3}d^2\right) \cdot (G d) = \frac{4}{3} G d^3$$

### Collision Frequency for the Population
For $N$ particles per unit volume, the total collision frequency per unit volume is:

$$R = \frac{1}{2} N \cdot N \cdot \frac{4}{3} G d^3 = \frac{2}{3} G d^3 N^2$$

### Final Rate Equation

$$\boxed{\;\frac{dN}{dt} = -\frac{2}{3} G d^3 N^2 = -\frac{1}{2} K_O N^2\;}$$

with $K_O = \dfrac{4}{3} G d^3$.

## 3.3 Integrated Form — Time Evolution of Particle Count

The Smoluchowski equation is a separable ODE:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -\frac{1}{2} K N^2$$

### Integrate

$$\int_{N_0}^{N_t} \frac{dN}{N^2} = -\frac{K}{2} \int_0^t dt$$

$$\left[ -\frac{1}{N} \right]_{N_0}^{N_t} = -\frac{K t}{2}$$

$$\frac{1}{N_t} - \frac{1}{N_0} = \frac{K t}{2}$$

### Solve for $N_t$

$$\boxed{\;N_t = \frac{N_0}{1 + \dfrac{1}{2} K N_0 t}\;}$$

### Half-Life of Flocculation
Setting $N_t = N_0 / 2$:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{2}{K N_0}$$

## 3.4 Camp–Stein Velocity Gradient Derivation

The mean velocity gradient $G$ in a stirred vessel of volume $V$ and power input $P$ is derived by equating the rate of viscous dissipation of mechanical energy to the rate of energy input:

$$P = \mu \cdot \phi = \mu \cdot G^2 \cdot V$$

where $\phi$ = total rate of viscous dissipation per unit volume.

### Rearrange for $G$:

$$\boxed{\;G = \sqrt{\frac{P}{\mu V}}\;}$$

### Power Number for a Paddle Impeller
For a flat-blade paddle of diameter $D_i$ rotating at speed $N_p$ in a baffled tank:

$$P = C_D \cdot \rho \cdot N_p^3 \cdot D_i^5$$

where $C_D$ is the drag coefficient (≈ 1.8 for flat blades in turbulent regime).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Typical KTU Exam Style

**Problem:** A jar test is performed at $T = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$ with $\mu = 0.89 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s}$. Initial particle count $N_0 = 10^{15}\,\text{m}^{-3}$ of diameter $d = 2\,\mu\text{m}$. The mean velocity gradient in the paddle region is $G = 50\,\text{s}^{-1}$. Compute the particle count after $t = 30\,\text{min}$ for **orthokinetic** and **perikinetic** cases and compare.

### Step 1 — Perikinetic Rate Constant
At $T = 298\,K$, $kT = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 298 = 4.112 \times 10^{-21}\,\text{J}$.

$$K_P = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4.112 \times 10^{-21}}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.16 \times 10^{-18}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 2 — Orthokinetic Rate Constant
$$K_O = \frac{4}{3} \times 50 \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^3 = \frac{4}{3} \times 50 \times 8 \times 10^{-18} = 5.33 \times 10^{-16}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

> **Comparison:** $K_O / K_P \approx 86.5$. Orthokinetic dominates for $2\,\mu m$ particles in stirred flow.

### Step 3 — Particle Count after $t = 1800\,\text{s}$

**Perikinetic:**
$$N_{t,P} = \frac{10^{15}}{1 + 0.5 \times 6.16 \times 10^{-18} \times 10^{15} \times 1800} = \frac{10^{15}}{1 + 0.00554} = 9.945 \times 10^{14}\,\text{m}^{-3}$$

**Orthokinetic:**
$$N_{t,O} = \frac{10^{15}}{1 + 0.5 \times 5.33 \times 10^{-16} \times 10^{15} \times 1800} = \frac{10^{15}}{1 + 0.480} = 6.76 \times 10^{14}\,\text{m}^{-3}$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion
Orthokinetic flocculation reduces the particle count by **~32 %**, whereas perikinetic flocculation reduces it by only **~0.55 %** over the same 30 minutes. This numerically confirms the KTU textbook result that **stirring (G) is the dominant engineering lever** for water treatment clarifiers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Flocculation Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Water Sample] --> B[Coagulant Dosing Unit - Alum FeCl3]
    B --> C[Rapid Mix Chamber - G1 700 s-1, t 30 s]
    C --> D[Flocculation Tank with Variable Speed Paddle]
    D --> E[Velocity Gradient Control - G 10 to 100 s-1]
    D --> F[Sampling Port at Fixed Time Intervals]
    F --> G[Nephelometer / Turbidity Meter]
    F --> H[Particle Counter or Settling Column]
    G --> I[Data Acquisition - Residual Turbidity NTU]
    H --> J[Data Acquisition - Settled Height h_t]
    I --> K[Compute G times t - Camp Number]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Plot Residual Turbidity vs Gt]
    K --> M[Compare Perikinetic vs Orthokinetic Kinetics]
    L --> N[Optimize Coagulant Dose and G]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Report - Optimum Gt Range 2 times 10^4 to 2 times 10^5]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Choosing the Dominant Regime

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Particle in Suspension] --> Q{Particle diameter d}
    Q -->|d less than 1 micrometer| R[Perikinetic Dominant]
    Q -->|d greater than 1 micrometer| S[Orthokinetic Dominant]
    R --> R1[Brownian Diffusion Controls]
    S --> S1[Induced Velocity Gradient Controls]
    R1 --> T[Count Collision Frequency via KP]
    S1 --> U[Count Collision Frequency via KO]
    T --> V[Integrate Smoluchowski to get Nt]
    U --> V
    V --> W[Compare to Experimental Nt to find alpha]
```

## 4.3 Mechanism Diagram — Perikinetic vs Orthokinetic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PERI[PERIKINETIC REGIME - Brownian motion]
        P1[Particle 1] -->|Random Walk| P2[Particle 2]
        P2 -->|Random Walk| P3[Particle 3]
        P3 --> P4[Floc Nucleus]
    end
    subgraph ORTHO[ORTHOKINETIC REGIME - Fluid Shear]
        O1[Particle 1] -->|Streamline 1 faster| O2[Particle 2 on Streamline 2]
        O2 --> O3[Collision and Aggregation]
        O3 --> O4[Larger Floc]
        O4 --> O5[Grows by sweeping more particles]
    end
    PERI -.->|Eventually joins| ORTHO
```

## 4.4 Jar Test Apparatus — Schematic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph JT[Jar Test Apparatus]
        J1[Beaker 1 - 1000 mL]
        J2[Beaker 2 - 1000 mL]
        J3[Beaker 3 - 1000 mL]
        J4[Beaker 4 - 1000 mL]
        J5[Beaker 5 - 1000 mL]
        J6[Beaker 6 - 1000 mL]
    end
    JT --> MP[Variable Speed Motor Drive]
    MP --> PAD[Paddle Impellers - one per beaker]
    PAD --> SS[Stainless Steel Shaft]
    SS --> RC[Speed Controller sets Np rpm]
    RC --> PW[Compute Power Input P from CD rho Np^3 Di^5]
    PW --> GC[Compute G from sqrt P over mu V]
    GC --> T1[Set t1 = 30 s at G1 = 700 s-1 rapid mix]
    GC --> T2[Set t2 = 20 min at G2 = 30 s-1 slow stir]
    T1 --> SET[Switch off stir - settle 30 min]
    T2 --> SET
    SET --> READ[Read supernatant turbidity NTU and settled height h_t]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define flocculation. Distinguish between perikinetic and orthokinetic flocculation.
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
*Flocculation* is the process by which destabilised colloidal particles in a suspension are brought into contact and aggregated into larger, settleable masses called flocs.
- *Perikinetic flocculation:* Aggregation driven by the **random Brownian motion** of particles themselves; dominant for $d \le 1\,\mu m$; rate constant $K_P = \dfrac{4kT}{3\mu}$.
- *Orthokinetic flocculation:* Aggregation driven by the **induced fluid velocity gradient** (shear); dominant for $d > 1\,\mu m$; rate constant $K_O = \dfrac{4}{3} G d^3$.
- *Key engineering difference:* Orthokinetic is ~50–100× faster and is the basis of stirred-tank clarifier design.

### Q2. What is the Camp–Stein velocity gradient $G$? Why is the product $G \cdot t$ called the Camp number?
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The mean velocity gradient $G$ (s⁻¹) inside a flocculation basin is defined by the Camp–Stein equation:

$$G = \sqrt{\frac{P}{\mu V}}$$

where $P$ is the power dissipated (W), $\mu$ the dynamic viscosity, and $V$ the basin volume.
The product $G \cdot t$ (dimensionless), where $t$ is the flocculation time, is called the **Camp number**. It is a design correlating parameter: for a given raw water and coagulant, the residual turbidity depends only on $G \cdot t$ (not $G$ and $t$ separately). Typical optimum range: $2 \times 10^4 \le G \cdot t \le 2 \times 10^5$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following two.

---

### Q.A) `[14 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Smoluchowski rate equation for perikinetic flocculation. State clearly the assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A suspension contains $N_0 = 5 \times 10^{14}\,\text{m}^{-3}$ monodisperse particles of diameter $d = 0.8\,\mu\text{m}$ at $T = 293\,K$. The dynamic viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s}$. Compute (i) the perikinetic rate constant $K_P$, (ii) the half-life $t_{1/2}$ of the suspension, and (iii) the residual particle concentration after 2 hours of stagnant settling.

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)
Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.1** above. Key steps to write in the exam:
1. Fick's first law with spherical symmetry, $\Phi = 4\pi D d N$.
2. Stokes–Einstein substitution $D = \dfrac{kT}{3\pi\mu d}$ giving $\Phi = \dfrac{4kT N}{3\mu}$.
3. Population-level rate: $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -\dfrac{1}{2} \Phi \cdot N = -\dfrac{2kT}{3\mu} N^2$.
4. Identification $K_P = \dfrac{4kT}{3\mu}$.
5. Assumptions: monodisperse spheres, no hydrodynamic interaction, $\alpha = 1$, isothermal, dilute suspension.

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Step (i) — Compute $K_P$:**
$kT = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 293 = 4.043 \times 10^{-21}\,\text{J}$.

$$K_P = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4.043 \times 10^{-21}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.39 \times 10^{-18}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step (ii) — Half-life:**

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{2}{K_P N_0} = \frac{2}{5.39 \times 10^{-18} \times 5 \times 10^{14}} = \frac{2}{2.695 \times 10^{-3}} = 742\,\text{s} \approx 12.4\,\text{min}$$

**Step (iii) — Residual count after $t = 7200\,\text{s}$:**

$$N_t = \frac{N_0}{1 + 0.5 \, K_P N_0 \, t} = \frac{5 \times 10^{14}}{1 + 0.5 \times 5.39 \times 10^{-18} \times 5 \times 10^{14} \times 7200}$$

$$N_t = \frac{5 \times 10^{14}}{1 + 9.70} = \frac{5 \times 10^{14}}{10.70} = 4.67 \times 10^{13}\,\text{m}^{-3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always convert $t$ to seconds and report $K$ in m³/s, $N$ in m⁻³, to keep SI consistency.

---

### Q.B) `[14 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the principle and working of a **jar test apparatus** for the determination of optimum coagulant dose. State the role of $G$-value in the flocculation stage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a jar test, a paddle of diameter $D_i = 7.5\,\text{cm}$ rotates at $N_p = 60\,\text{rpm}$ in a $V = 1\,\text{L}$ beaker. The drag coefficient $C_D = 1.8$, $\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s}$. Find (i) the power input $P$, (ii) the mean velocity gradient $G$, and (iii) the Camp number $G \cdot t$ for a flocculation time of 20 minutes.

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)
- **Principle:** A standard jar test uses 4–6 identical beakers filled with the same raw water but dosed with different coagulant concentrations. All beakers are subjected to identical rapid mix (G₁ ≈ 700 s⁻¹, 30 s) followed by slow flocculation (G₂ ≈ 20–100 s⁻¹, 20 min), settling, and supernatant turbidity measurement.
- **Diagram (in words for exam):** Square beaker on a gang-stirrer base → motor-driven shaft → flat-blade paddle inside each beaker → speed-control knob → baffles to prevent vortexing.
- **Role of G in flocculation stage:** $G$ controls the collision frequency; an optimum G produces strong, dense flocs without breaking them. Too low → no flocs; too high → floc breakup. Optimum flocculation G ≈ 20–75 s⁻¹.

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Step (i) — Convert units and compute $P$:**

$N_p = 60\,\text{rpm} = 1\,\text{rev/s} = 1\,\text{s}^{-1}$.
$D_i = 0.075\,\text{m}$, $V = 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$.

$$P = C_D \cdot \rho \cdot N_p^3 \cdot D_i^5 = 1.8 \times 1000 \times 1^3 \times (0.075)^5$$

$(0.075)^5 = 2.373 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^5$.

$$\boxed{P = 1.8 \times 1000 \times 2.373 \times 10^{-6} = 4.27 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{W} = 4.27\,\text{mW}}$$

**Step (ii) — Compute $G$:**

$$G = \sqrt{\frac{P}{\mu V}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.27 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{4270} = 65.3\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step (iii) — Compute Camp number:**

$t = 20 \times 60 = 1200\,\text{s}$.

$$G \cdot t = 65.3 \times 1200 = 7.84 \times 10^4$$

This value falls in the standard design window $2 \times 10^4 \le G t \le 2 \times 10^5$, so the flocculation is well-designed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. *Unit conversion* — $N_p$ in rpm must be converted to rev/s (rps) before substituting into $P$ equation. Forgetting this yields an error of a factor of 60³ = 216 000 — the most common mark-losing mistake in lab viva calculations.
> 2. *Viscosity value* — At 25 °C use $\mu = 0.89 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s; at 20 °C use $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Do not default to 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s unless the problem statement specifies "at 20 °C".
> 3. *Sign convention* — $\dfrac{dN}{dt}$ is **negative** (particles are disappearing). Do not drop the minus sign when reporting the rate.
> 4. *Mis-application* — Perikinetic equations are only valid for $d \le 1\,\mu m$. For a 10 µm suspension in a stirred tank, do not compute $K_P$ — it is negligible.
> 5. *Reporting $G t$* — Always express as a pure number (e.g., $7.84 \times 10^4$), not in s⁻¹·min.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Flocculation** is the post-coagulation aggregation of destabilised colloids into settleable flocs.
- **Perikinetic** regime: Brownian-driven, $K_P = \dfrac{4kT}{3\mu}$, $d \le 1\,\mu m$, slow.
- **Orthokinetic** regime: shear-driven, $K_O = \dfrac{4}{3} G d^3$, $d > 1\,\mu m$, fast.
- **Smoluchowski equation:** $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -\dfrac{1}{2} K N^2$, integrated as $N_t = \dfrac{N_0}{1 + \frac{1}{2} K N_0 t}$.
- **Camp–Stein velocity gradient:** $G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu V}}$, with $P = C_D \rho N_p^3 D_i^5$.
- **Camp number $G \cdot t$:** Typical optimum $2 \times 10^4$ to $2 \times 10^5$.
- **Collision efficiency $\alpha$:** Ratio of experimental to theoretical rate constant; $\alpha = 1$ for Smoluchowski ideal case.
- **Lab observation:** Measure residual turbidity vs $G \cdot t$ to find the optimum point.
- **Design rule of thumb:** Rapid mix $G \approx 700\,\text{s}^{-1}$ for 30 s; slow stir $G \approx 20–75\,\text{s}^{-1}$ for 20–30 min.
- **Physical constants to memorise:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$; $\mu_{water,20} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s}$; $\rho_{water} = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$.
- **Key Mnemonics:** *P*eri = *P*articles moving on their own; *O*rtho = *O*rdered flow imposed externally.
- **Engineering Outcome (CO mapping):** Maps directly to **CO2** (Apply particle–fluid interaction principles to design separation equipment) and **CO3** (Analyse flocculation kinetics for water-treatment applications).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Drag Coefficient and Verification of Stokes Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL408 - FLUID FLOW & PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Drag Coefficient and Verification of Stokes Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Stokes' Law** is the fundamental low-Reynolds-number fluid dynamics law expressing the viscous drag force experienced by a spherical particle moving at constant velocity through a viscous Newtonian fluid. For a sphere of diameter $d$ moving with terminal velocity $v_t$ through a fluid of dynamic viscosity $\mu$, the resisting viscous drag force is given by:

$$F_d = 3\pi \mu d v_t$$

The **Drag Coefficient ($C_D$)** is the dimensionless quantity that quantifies the resistance of a fluid flow around a body. It is defined as the ratio of the drag force to the product of the dynamic pressure and the projected frontal area of the body:

$$C_D = \frac{F_d}{\frac{1}{2}\rho_f v^2 A_p}$$

where $A_p = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$ is the projected (frontal) area of the sphere, and $\rho_f$ is the fluid density.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The experiment simultaneously addresses two pedagogical objectives: (i) Verification of Stokes' Law by experimentally determining the terminal velocity of falling spheres, and (ii) Determination of the drag coefficient and validation of the $C_D$ vs. Re relationship in the creeping flow regime.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine dropping a steel ball bearing into a column of thick honey versus a column of water. In honey, the ball sinks agonizingly slowly because the **viscous resistance** is enormous. In water, the ball sinks quickly because viscous resistance is small. **Stokes' Law quantifies exactly how much "sticky resistance" the fluid applies** to a moving sphere.

A more striking analogy: Think of a skydiver in free fall. The skydiver accelerates until the upward air drag equals the downward gravitational pull, after which they fall at a **constant terminal velocity**. In our laboratory experiment, we simply use tiny steel or glass spheres in place of skydivers, and a viscous oil (e.g., castor oil or glycerine) in place of air, scaled down to fit a measuring cylinder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the sphere reach a constant terminal velocity?**
> Three forces act on the falling sphere: (1) downward **weight** (W), (2) upward **buoyancy** (F_b), and (3) upward **viscous drag** (F_d). At terminal velocity, the net force is zero: $W = F_b + F_d$. The sphere no longer accelerates, hence "terminal".

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (Castor Oil, 30°C) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water (4°C) | $\rho_w$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Stokes regime limit | $Re$ | **Re < 0.1 (creeping flow)** |
| Density of steel sphere | $\rho_s$ | **~7800 kg/m³** |
| Density of glass sphere | $\rho_s$ | **~2500 kg/m³** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physics — Force Balance on a Falling Sphere

When a spherical particle of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_s$ is allowed to fall under gravity through a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$, three principal forces act upon it:

1. **Gravitational force (Weight):** Acts downward.
2. **Buoyant force (Archimedes' upthrust):** Acts upward, equal to the weight of displaced fluid.
3. **Viscous drag force:** Acts upward, opposing the motion of the sphere.

The physical setup ensures that, after a brief initial transient, the sphere settles into a regime of constant **terminal velocity** $v_t$, where the net force is zero.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Force Balance

**Step 1 — Compute the gravitational (weight) force:**
The weight of the sphere is the product of its mass and gravitational acceleration.

$$F_g = m \cdot g = \rho_s \cdot V_s \cdot g = \rho_s \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Compute the buoyant force:**
By Archimedes' principle, the upward buoyant force equals the weight of the fluid displaced by the sphere.

$$F_b = \rho_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Apply Stokes' drag force:**
The viscous drag in the creeping flow regime (low Reynolds number) is given by Stokes' law.

$$F_d = 3\pi \mu d v_t$$

**Step 4 — Equate net force to zero at terminal velocity:**
Since the sphere is no longer accelerating, Newton's second law gives:

$$F_g - F_b - F_d = 0$$

Substituting all three expressions:

$$\rho_s \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g - \rho_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g - 3\pi \mu d v_t = 0$$

**Step 5 — Isolate the terminal velocity:**
After algebraic rearrangement, the terminal velocity emerges in the canonical Stokes form.

$$v_t = \frac{d^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the experimental use of the result:**
The above equation allows us to compute the fluid viscosity by measuring the terminal velocity of a sphere of known diameter and density.

$$\mu = \frac{d^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{18 v_t}$$

**Step 7 — Connect to Drag Coefficient:**
At terminal velocity, the drag force equals the net effective weight (weight minus buoyancy):

$$F_d = \frac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_s - \rho_f) g$$

Dividing this by $\frac{1}{2}\rho_f v_t^2 A_p$ yields:

$$C_D = \frac{\frac{\pi d^3}{6}(\rho_s - \rho_f)g}{\frac{1}{2}\rho_f v_t^2 \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}}$$

$$C_D = \frac{4 d (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{3 \rho_f v_t^2}$$

**Step 8 — Establish the Reynolds number for the sphere:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho_f v_t d}{\mu}$$

**Step 9 — Verify the Stokes relationship:**
Theoretically, for the creeping flow regime, it can be shown that:

$$C_D = \frac{24}{Re}$$

Taking logarithms of both sides:

$$\log C_D = \log 24 - \log Re$$

This predicts a straight line of slope $-1$ on a log-log plot of $C_D$ vs. $Re$, passing through the intercept $\log 24 \approx 1.38$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Stokes' Drag Force | $F_d = 3\pi \mu d v_t$ | N ; valid only for $Re < 0.1$ |
| 2 | Terminal Velocity (Stokes) | $v_t = \dfrac{d^2(\rho_s - \rho_f)g}{18\mu}$ | m/s |
| 3 | Fluid Viscosity from $v_t$ | $\mu = \dfrac{d^2(\rho_s - \rho_f)g}{18 v_t}$ | Pa·s or kg/(m·s) |
| 4 | Drag Coefficient (from force balance) | $C_D = \dfrac{4 d (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{3 \rho_f v_t^2}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Reynolds Number for Sphere | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_f v_t d}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Theoretical Stokes Regime Relation | $C_D \cdot Re = 24$ | Valid for $Re < 0.1$ |
| 7 | Projected Area of Sphere | $A_p = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | m² |
| 8 | Volume of Sphere | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}$ | m³ |
| 9 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho_f}$ | m²/s |
| 10 | Settling Time between Marks | $t = \dfrac{L}{v_t}$ | s ; $L$ = distance between marks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick for Terminal Velocity:** "**D-squared Delta-rho G over 18 mu**" — the terminal velocity scales with the **square** of diameter, the density **difference** between solid and fluid, and inversely with viscosity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Stokes' Law framework is the foundation of numerous industrial and environmental engineering applications:

- **Sedimentation and settling tanks** in water treatment plants (design of clarifiers).
- **Classification of particles** by size in mineral processing (cyclones, elutriators).
- **Aerosol and dust collection** in air pollution control equipment.
- **Pharmaceutical manufacturing** for controlled drug delivery via microparticles.
- **Meteorology** for predicting the fall velocity of raindrops, snowflakes, and volcanic ash.
- **Viscosity measurement** via the falling-ball viscometer — a direct industrial application of the very experiment you are performing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Newton's Second Law to Stokes' Terminal Velocity

We begin with the second law applied to a falling sphere. The sphere accelerates under gravity, but viscous drag grows with velocity. The point where the net force vanishes defines the terminal velocity.

**Step A — Newton's Second Law on the sphere:**

$$m \frac{dv}{dt} = F_g - F_b - F_d$$

**Step B — Substitute the mass $m = \rho_s V_s$ and all forces:**

$$\rho_s \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = \rho_s \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g - \rho_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g - 3\pi \mu d v$$

**Step C — Simplify the common factor $\frac{\pi d^3}{6}$:**

$$\frac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot \rho_s \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_s - \rho_f) g - 3\pi \mu d v$$

**Step D — Divide throughout by $\frac{\pi d^3}{6}$:**

$$\rho_s \frac{dv}{dt} = (\rho_s - \rho_f) g - \frac{18 \mu v}{d^2}$$

**Step E — Recognise that the right-hand side is a linear first-order ODE in $v$:**

$$\frac{dv}{dt} + \frac{18 \mu}{\rho_s d^2} v = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{\rho_s}$$

**Step F — Identify the steady-state (terminal) velocity, where $\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$:**

$$0 + \frac{18 \mu}{\rho_s d^2} v_t = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{\rho_s}$$

**Step G — Solve algebraically for $v_t$:**

$$v_t = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{\rho_s} \cdot \frac{\rho_s d^2}{18 \mu}$$

**Step H — Cancel $\rho_s$ in numerator and denominator:**

$$v_t = \frac{d^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the celebrated **Stokes' Terminal Velocity Formula** — the cornerstone of the experiment. The transient solution (exponential approach to $v_t$) can be obtained, but the steady-state result is what the laboratory measures.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: The Drag Coefficient in Terms of Measured Quantities

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of drag coefficient:**

$$C_D = \frac{F_d}{\frac{1}{2} \rho_f v_t^2 A_p}$$

**Step 2 — At terminal velocity, the drag force equals the effective weight:**

$$F_d = \frac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_s - \rho_f) g$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the projected area $A_p = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$:**

$$C_D = \frac{\frac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{\frac{1}{2} \rho_f v_t^2 \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}}$$

**Step 4 — Combine the constants in the denominator:**

$$\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi d^2}{8}$$

**Step 5 — Invert and multiply:**

$$C_D = \frac{\frac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{\frac{\pi d^2}{8} \rho_f v_t^2}$$

**Step 6 — Cancel the common $\pi$ and reduce the powers of $d$:**

$$C_D = \frac{d^3 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g \cdot 8}{6 d^2 \rho_f v_t^2} = \frac{8 d (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{6 \rho_f v_t^2}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the numerical coefficient $\frac{8}{6} = \frac{4}{3}$:**

$$C_D = \frac{4 d (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{3 \rho_f v_t^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This final compact expression is **experimentally powerful** because it requires no knowledge of the fluid viscosity $\mu$ to compute $C_D$ — only the sphere diameter, sphere density, fluid density, terminal velocity, and $g$.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Theoretical Link Between $C_D$ and $Re$

**Step 1 — Begin with the definitions:**

$$C_D = \frac{2 F_d}{\rho_f v_t^2 A_p}, \quad Re = \frac{\rho_f v_t d}{\mu}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Stokes' drag $F_d = 3\pi \mu d v_t$ and $A_p = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$:**

$$C_D = \frac{2 \cdot 3\pi \mu d v_t}{\rho_f v_t^2 \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the numerator and denominator constants:**

$$C_D = \frac{6\pi \mu d v_t}{\frac{\pi d^2 \rho_f v_t^2}{4}} = \frac{6 \pi \mu d v_t \cdot 4}{\pi d^2 \rho_f v_t^2}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel common factors:**

$$C_D = \frac{24 \mu}{\rho_f v_t d}$$

**Step 5 — Recognise $\rho_f v_t d / \mu = Re$, hence $\mu / (\rho_f v_t d) = 1/Re$:**

$$C_D = \frac{24}{Re}$$

**Step 6 — Take the base-10 logarithm of both sides to obtain a log-linear form:**

$$\log C_D = \log 24 - \log Re$$

$$\log C_D = 1.3802 - \log Re$$

> [!NOTE]
> On a log-log plot, this relation appears as a straight line of **slope $-1$** and **y-intercept $\log 24 \approx 1.380$**. The experimental graph should match this prediction if Stokes' law is obeyed.

## 3.4 Sample Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

Suppose a steel ball of diameter $d = 2 \text{ mm} = 0.002 \text{ m}$ and density $\rho_s = 7800 \text{ kg/m}^3$ falls through castor oil of density $\rho_f = 960 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and viscosity $\mu = 0.65 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. The sphere falls $L = 0.3 \text{ m}$ in $t = 4.5 \text{ s}$.

**Step 1 — Terminal velocity:**

$$v_t = \frac{L}{t} = \frac{0.3}{4.5} = 0.0667 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Drag coefficient:**
