$$C_D = \frac{4 \times 0.002 \times (7800 - 960) \times 9.81}{3 \times 960 \times (0.0667)^2}$$

$$C_D = \frac{0.008 \times 6840 \times 9.81}{2880 \times 0.00445} = \frac{536.78}{12.81} \approx 41.9$$

**Step 3 — Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{960 \times 0.0667 \times 0.002}{0.65} = \frac{0.1281}{0.65} \approx 0.197$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical $C_D$ from Stokes formula:**

$$C_D^{\text{theory}} = \frac{24}{Re} = \frac{24}{0.197} \approx 121.8$$

**Step 5 — Note the deviation:** $C_D^{\text{exp}} = 41.9$ vs. $C_D^{\text{theory}} = 121.8$. The Reynolds number $Re = 0.197$ slightly exceeds the strict Stokes limit ($Re < 0.1$), introducing a deviation that students are expected to **comment on critically** in the viva.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Experimental Apparatus

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the typical arrangement used to verify Stokes' Law in the laboratory.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Long Borosilicate Glass Cylinder] --> A2[Transparent Graduated Column 1500 mm height]
    A2 --> A3[Working Fluid: Castor Oil or Glycerine]
    A3 --> A4[Fill Height 1200 mm]
    A4 --> B1[Upper Reference Mark A at 200 mm below fluid surface]
    B1 --> B2[Lower Reference Mark B at 800 mm below fluid surface]
    B2 --> B3[Measurement Length L between Marks A and B]
    C1[Spherical Steel or Glass Balls] --> C2[Diameters: 1 mm, 1.5 mm, 2 mm, 2.5 mm, 3 mm]
    C2 --> C3[Density: 7800 kg per cubic metre for steel]
    C3 --> D1[Ball released gently at centre of fluid surface using tweezers]
    D1 --> D2[Stopwatch started when ball crosses Upper Mark A]
    D2 --> D3[Stopwatch stopped when ball crosses Lower Mark B]
    D3 --> E1[Time t recorded for each ball diameter]
    E1 --> E2[Repeat three times and take mean time t average]
    E2 --> F1[Thermometer records fluid temperature T]
    F1 --> F2[Hydrometer measures fluid density rho f]
    F2 --> G1[Data Reduction: v sub t equals L over t]
    G1 --> G2[Plot v sub t versus d squared for linear check]
    G2 --> G3[Plot log C sub D versus log Re for slope minus one check]
    G3 --> H1[Result: Stokes Law Verified and C sub D Determined]
```

## 4.2 Force Balance Flowchart on the Falling Sphere

The following Mermaid chart visualises the dynamic equilibrium that establishes the terminal velocity.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Sphere Released at Top] --> node2[Weight F sub g acts Downward]
    node2 --> node3[Buoyancy F sub b acts Upward]
    node3 --> node4[Drag F sub d acts Upward]
    node4 --> node5{Is F sub g greater than F sub b plus F sub d}
    node5 -- Yes --> node6[Sphere Accelerates Downward]
    node6 --> node7[Velocity v increases]
    node7 --> node8[Drag F sub d increases with v]
    node8 --> node5
    node5 -- No --> node9[Sphere reaches Constant Terminal Velocity v sub t]
    node9 --> node10[Net force becomes zero]
    node10 --> node11[Apply Stokes Equation: v sub t equals d squared times delta rho times g over 18 mu]
```

## 4.3 Experimental Procedure Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepA[Step A: Clean the Glass Column] --> stepB[Step B: Fill Column with Castor Oil and Allow to Stand]
    stepB --> stepC[Step C: Mark Two Reference Lines A and B]
    stepC --> stepD[Step D: Measure Distance L between Marks]
    stepD --> stepE[Step E: Select a Sphere of Known Diameter d]
    stepE --> stepF[Step F: Determine Sphere Density rho sub s]
    stepF --> stepG[Step G: Measure Fluid Density rho sub f and Temperature]
    stepG --> stepH[Step H: Drop Sphere Centrally into Fluid]
    stepH --> stepI[Step I: Start Stopwatch at Mark A and Stop at Mark B]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step J: Record Time t]
    stepJ --> stepK[Step K: Repeat Three Times and Average]
    stepK --> stepL[Step L: Repeat for All Sphere Diameters]
    stepL --> stepM[Step M: Compute v sub t, mu, C sub D, and Re for Each Trial]
    stepM --> stepN[Step N: Plot v sub t versus d squared to Verify Stokes Law]
    stepN --> stepO[Step O: Plot log C sub D versus log Re and Compare with Theory]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: Define Stokes' Law. State the assumptions and limitations under which it is valid.**

**Model Answer:**

Stokes' Law states that the viscous drag force on a sphere of diameter $d$ moving with velocity $v$ through a Newtonian fluid of dynamic viscosity $\mu$ is given by:

$$F_d = 3\pi \mu d v$$

**Assumptions / Validity Conditions:**

1. The flow regime is **creeping flow** (very low Reynolds number, $Re < 0.1$).
2. The fluid is a **continuum** (i.e., the sphere is much larger than the molecular mean free path).
3. The fluid is **Newtonian** (viscosity is independent of shear rate).
4. The sphere is **rigid, smooth, and isolated** (no interactions with container walls or other spheres).
5. **No-slip** condition holds at the sphere surface.
6. The flow is **steady** and the fluid extends to infinity (i.e., **no wall effects**).

**Limitations:**

- Fails at moderate to high Reynolds numbers where inertial effects dominate.
- Wall effects become significant when the sphere-to-cylinder diameter ratio is greater than 0.1.
- Not applicable to non-Newtonian fluids or particles that are non-spherical.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — December 2023]

**Q: Define drag coefficient and state its significance in fluid mechanics.**

**Model Answer:**

The **drag coefficient** is the dimensionless ratio of the drag force experienced by a body to the product of the dynamic pressure and the body's projected frontal area:

$$C_D = \frac{F_d}{\frac{1}{2}\rho_f v^2 A_p}$$

**Significance:**

1. It characterises the **resistance** of a body moving through a fluid, independent of size, velocity, and fluid properties.
2. It allows the **scaling of experimental data** from model to prototype.
3. It links drag force to velocity squared, enabling force predictions at different operating conditions.
4. In the **Stokes regime** ($Re < 0.1$), $C_D$ follows the elegant inverse relation $C_D = 24/Re$, enabling the experimental verification of low-Reynolds-number theory.
5. It is essential in the design of **aircraft, automobiles, marine vessels, and sedimentation tanks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per the KTU 2024 Scheme Part B structure, students must attempt either **Question A** OR **Question B**. Both options are presented below for completeness.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the terminal velocity of a sphere falling through a viscous fluid under Stokes' regime. Clearly state the assumptions used in the derivation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In an experiment to verify Stokes' law, a steel ball of diameter 3 mm falls through castor oil. The distance between the two reference marks is 0.5 m, and the time taken is 5.6 seconds. If the density of steel is 7800 kg/m³, the density of castor oil is 960 kg/m³, and $g = 9.81$ m/s², calculate (i) the terminal velocity, (ii) the viscosity of castor oil, and (iii) the drag coefficient.

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**Statement of assumptions:**

1. Steady, laminar, creeping flow regime ($Re < 0.1$).
2. Rigid, smooth, isolated spherical particle.
3. Newtonian fluid with constant viscosity.
4. No-slip condition at the particle surface.
5. No wall effects (cylinder diameter is much greater than the sphere diameter).
6. Incompressible fluid.

**Derivation:**

At terminal velocity, the net force on the sphere is zero:

$$F_g = F_b + F_d$$

Weight of sphere:

$$F_g = \rho_s \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g$$

Buoyant force:

$$F_b = \rho_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g$$

Stokes' drag:

$$F_d = 3\pi \mu d v_t$$

Equating and rearranging:

$$\rho_s \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g - \rho_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g = 3\pi \mu d v_t$$

$$\frac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_s - \rho_f) g = 3\pi \mu d v_t$$

$$v_t = \frac{d^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$$

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**Given data:**
$d = 3 \text{ mm} = 0.003 \text{ m}$, $L = 0.5 \text{ m}$, $t = 5.6 \text{ s}$, $\rho_s = 7800 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_f = 960 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**(i) Terminal velocity:**

$$v_t = \frac{L}{t} = \frac{0.5}{5.6} = 0.0893 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Viscosity of castor oil:**

$$\mu = \frac{d^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{18 v_t}$$

$$\mu = \frac{(0.003)^2 \times (7800 - 960) \times 9.81}{18 \times 0.0893}$$

$$\mu = \frac{9 \times 10^{-6} \times 6840 \times 9.81}{1.6074} = \frac{0.6040}{1.6074} \approx 0.376 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

**(iii) Drag coefficient:**

$$C_D = \frac{4 d (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{3 \rho_f v_t^2}$$

$$C_D = \frac{4 \times 0.003 \times 6840 \times 9.81}{3 \times 960 \times (0.0893)^2}$$

$$C_D = \frac{805.27}{22.96} \approx 35.07$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — December 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the drag coefficient. With the help of Stokes' law, derive the theoretical relationship between drag coefficient ($C_D$) and Reynolds number ($Re$) for a sphere in the creeping flow regime. Plot the expected log–log relationship.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a Stokes' law verification experiment, five steel balls of diameters 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, and 3 mm are dropped through glycerine. The measured terminal velocities are 0.012, 0.027, 0.048, 0.075, and 0.108 m/s respectively. The density of steel is 7800 kg/m³ and that of glycerine is 1260 kg/m³. Plot $v_t$ vs. $d^2$ and comment on the verification of Stokes' law. Also determine the average viscosity of glycerine.

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Definition:** The drag coefficient is given by $C_D = \dfrac{F_d}{\frac{1}{2}\rho_f v^2 A_p}$.

**Derivation:**

Stokes' drag: $F_d = 3\pi \mu d v$. Projected area: $A_p = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$.

$$C_D = \frac{3\pi \mu d v}{\frac{1}{2} \rho_f v^2 \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}} = \frac{24 \mu}{\rho_f v d} = \frac{24}{Re}$$

Therefore:

$$C_D \cdot Re = 24 \quad \text{or} \quad \log C_D = \log 24 - \log Re$$

**Plot:** On a log-log plot, the relationship is a straight line with slope $-1$ and y-intercept $\log 24 = 1.38$.

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Tabulation:**

| $d$ (mm) | $d^2$ (mm²) | $v_t$ (m/s) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1.0 | 1.00 | 0.012 |
| 1.5 | 2.25 | 0.027 |
| 2.0 | 4.00 | 0.048 |
| 2.5 | 6.25 | 0.075 |
| 3.0 | 9.00 | 0.108 |

The plot of $v_t$ (y-axis) versus $d^2$ (x-axis) should yield a **straight line passing through the origin** — this is the verification of Stokes' law, since $v_t \propto d^2$.

**Viscosity calculation (using the formula):**

$$\mu_i = \frac{d^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{18 v_t}$$

- For $d = 1$ mm: $\mu_1 = \dfrac{(10^{-3})^2 \times (7800 - 1260) \times 9.81}{18 \times 0.012} = \dfrac{0.0642}{0.216} = 0.297$ Pa·s
- For $d = 1.5$ mm: $\mu_2 = \dfrac{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 6540 \times 9.81}{18 \times 0.027} = \dfrac{0.1443}{0.486} = 0.297$ Pa·s
- For $d = 2$ mm: $\mu_3 = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 6540 \times 9.81}{18 \times 0.048} = \dfrac{0.2566}{0.864} = 0.297$ Pa·s
- For $d = 2.5$ mm: $\mu_4 = \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 6540 \times 9.81}{18 \times 0.075} = \dfrac{0.4009}{1.35} = 0.297$ Pa·s
- For $d = 3$ mm: $\mu_5 = \dfrac{(3 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 6540 \times 9.81}{18 \times 0.108} = \dfrac{0.5773}{1.944} = 0.297$ Pa·s

**Average viscosity:**

$$\mu_{\text{avg}} = \frac{0.297 \times 5}{5} = 0.297 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

**Comment:** The near-constant viscosity across all five sphere diameters confirms Stokes' law. The linear $v_t$ vs. $d^2$ relationship is the graphical signature of Stokes' regime.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Board Examiner Observations):**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students often forget to convert sphere diameter from **mm to m** before squaring. This causes a $10^6$ error in viscosity. Always convert to SI units first.
> 2. **Forgetting Density Difference:** A surprisingly common error is to use $\rho_s$ alone instead of $(\rho_s - \rho_f)$ in the terminal velocity formula. The buoyancy term **must** be subtracted.
> 3. **Projected Area Confusion:** Some students use the surface area ($4\pi r^2$) instead of the projected frontal area ($\pi r^2$) in the drag coefficient formula. The correct area is the **shadow** (cross-section) the sphere presents to the flow.
> 4. **Neglecting Temperature:** Viscosity is highly temperature-dependent (especially for castor oil). Always note the **fluid temperature** during the experiment and report viscosity at that temperature.
> 5. **Wall Effects:** A small sphere in a narrow cylinder experiences **wall effects** that increase the effective drag. Mark is often lost if the student does not comment on this in the viva.
> 6. **Initial Acceleration Region:** The sphere takes a finite distance to reach terminal velocity. Marks are deducted if both reference marks are placed too close to the release point. The upper mark should be at least 100–150 mm below the fluid surface.
> 7. **Plotting Convention:** A common mistake is plotting $C_D$ on the y-axis and $Re$ on the x-axis in **linear** scale. The verification **requires** a log-log plot to reveal the slope of $-1$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Style**

- **Stokes' Law:** $F_d = 3\pi \mu d v_t$ — valid strictly for $Re < 0.1$.
- **Terminal Velocity:** $v_t = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$ — proportional to the **square** of diameter.
- **Viscosity from experiment:** $\mu = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{18 v_t}$ — does not require $C_D$ or $Re$.
- **Drag Coefficient:** $C_D = \dfrac{4 d (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{3 \rho_f v_t^2}$ — derived purely from force balance, no $\mu$ needed.
- **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho_f v_t d}{\mu}$ — must be < 0.1 for Stokes regime.
- **Theoretical Relation:** $C_D \cdot Re = 24$ — central result of the experiment.
- **Log-Log Plot:** Slope $-1$ and intercept $\log 24 = 1.38$ confirm Stokes' law.
- **Verification Graph 1:** Plot $v_t$ vs. $d^2$ — must be a straight line through the origin.
- **Verification Graph 2:** Plot $\log C_D$ vs. $\log Re$ — must give slope $-1$ in Stokes regime.
- **Wall Effects:** Avoid sphere-to-cylinder diameter ratios above 0.1.
- **Temperature Effect:** Always report $\mu$ at the experimental temperature.
- **Apparatus:** Long glass cylinder, viscous oil (castor oil or glycerine), steel/glass balls, stopwatch, thermometer, hydrometer.
- **Common Assumption:** Steady-state, isothermal, Newtonian, creeping flow.
- **Practical Tip:** Repeat each trial **at least three times** to minimise random error and compute the mean.
- **KTU Viva Favourite:** "Why does the sphere not reach terminal velocity immediately after release?" — answer: it requires a finite acceleration length for the drag force to balance the effective weight.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Venturi Coefficient / Orifice Coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL408 - FLUID FLOW & PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Venturi Coefficient / Orifice Coefficient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Venturi Coefficient (Cd_v)** and **Orifice Coefficient (Cd_o)** are dimensionless empirical discharge coefficients that quantify the ratio of the **actual volumetric flow rate** measured experimentally to the **ideal (theoretical) flow rate** predicted by the inviscid Bernoulli–Euler flow equations through a constriction in a closed conduit.

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd)** — A dimensionless number (typically **0.95 – 0.99 for a Venturi meter** and **0.60 – 0.65 for an Orifice meter**) that corrects the theoretical flow equation derived from an *ideal, frictionless, incompressible* flow assumption to match the *real, viscous* flow behavior observed in the laboratory.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English

Imagine you are standing at the entrance of a **water-slide park**. A *theoretical slide* is drawn by an engineer who assumes everyone slides on a perfectly smooth, friction-free surface. In reality, the slide has bumps, friction, and people drag their hands — so the *actual* flow of people is **less** than the theoretical maximum. The ratio of actual-to-theoretical is the **discharge coefficient**.

- **Venturi Meter** is like a *gently sloped, smooth water slide* (gradually converging throat → short recovery). Very little energy is lost ⇒ **Cd is very high** (≈ 0.98).
- **Orifice Meter** is like a *sudden vertical drop with a sharp edge*. Water spins and forms eddies on the downstream side ⇒ lots of energy lost ⇒ **Cd is low** (≈ 0.62).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two devices?** Both exploit the same physical principle (Venturi effect), but the Venturi is preferred when **energy recovery** matters, and the Orifice is preferred when **cost and compactness** matter.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water (at 25 °C) | $\rho_w$ | **997 kg/m³** |
| Density of mercury (manometer fluid) | $\rho_{Hg}$ | **13,600 kg/m³** |
| Specific gravity of mercury | $S_{Hg}$ | **13.6** |
| Typical Cd — Venturi | $C_d$ | **0.95 – 0.99** |
| Typical Cd — Orifice | $C_d$ | **0.60 – 0.65** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The entire experiment rests on **three core fluid-mechanics pillars**:

### Step 1 — Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)
For an incompressible fluid flowing through a pipe that narrows from inlet cross-section $A_1$ to throat cross-section $A_2$:

$$A_1 \, v_1 = A_2 \, v_2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad v_1 = v_2 \, \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

> *Why?* Mass cannot vanish inside the pipe; whatever volume flows in per second must flow out per second.

### Step 2 — Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy)
For an ideal, frictionless flow between inlet (1) and throat (2), on a horizontal axis ($z_1 = z_2$):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^{2}}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{2}}{2g}$$

### Step 3 — Combine Continuity + Bernoulli to Get Theoretical Velocity
The **pressure head difference** $h = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$ is what the manometer reads (as equivalent water column).

The **theoretical throat velocity** becomes:

$$v_{2,\text{th}} = \sqrt{\frac{2g \, h}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}}}$$

> *Why the denominator?* The fluid at section 1 is *not stationary*; it also has kinetic energy that must be subtracted from the available head. The factor $(1 - (A_2/A_1)^2)$ is a **velocity-of-approach correction**.

### Step 4 — Theoretical Volumetric Discharge

$$Q_{\text{th}} = A_2 \, v_{2,\text{th}} = \frac{A_2 \, \sqrt{2g \, h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}}}$$

### Step 5 — Manometer Equation (Translating Hg reading → water head)
For a **differential U-tube mercury manometer** with manometer deflection $x$ (m):

$$h = x \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) = x \left(S_{Hg} - 1\right)$$

### Step 6 — Define the Coefficient

$$\boxed{\;C_d \;=\; \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{V_{\text{collected}}}{t_{\text{collection}}}}{\dfrac{A_2 \, \sqrt{2g \, h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}}}\;}$$

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical process plants** — Online flow measurement of liquids in reactors and pipelines.
- **Water treatment plants** — Monitoring distribution line flow rates.
- **HVAC systems** — Duct flow balancing.
- **Petroleum refineries** — Custody transfer of crude and refined products.
- **Pharmaceuticals** — Sterile liquid dosing in CIP (clean-in-place) systems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Table — Every formula you will need in the lab viva or ESE question on this experiment.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{\text{act}} = V / t$ | Actual discharge from collecting tank | m³/s |
| 2 | $A_1 = \tfrac{\pi}{4} D_1^{2}$ | Inlet cross-section | m² |
| 3 | $A_2 = \tfrac{\pi}{4} D_2^{2}$ | Throat / orifice cross-section | m² |
| 4 | $h = x\,(S_{Hg} - 1)$ | Manometer → water head | m of water |
| 5 | $v_{2,\text{th}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}}$ | Theoretical throat velocity | m/s |
| 6 | $Q_{\text{th}} = C_d \, \dfrac{A_2 \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}}$ | General discharge equation | m³/s |
| 7 | $C_d = Q_{\text{act}} / Q_{\text{th}}$ | Definition of discharge coefficient | dimensionless |
| 8 | $v_{1,\text{th}} = v_{2,\text{th}} \cdot (A_2/A_1)$ | Continuity | m/s |
| 9 | $P_1 - P_2 = \rho g h$ | Pressure drop | Pa |
| 10 | $S_{Hg} = \rho_{Hg}/\rho_w = 13.6$ | Specific gravity of mercury | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Venturi / Orifice Discharge Equation

### Derivation Block A — From Bernoulli's Equation to Theoretical Velocity

**Given:**
- Inlet (section 1): pressure $P_1$, velocity $v_1$, area $A_1$, elevation $z_1$
- Throat (section 2): pressure $P_2$, velocity $v_2$, area $A_2$, elevation $z_2$
- Horizontal meter ⇒ $z_1 = z_2$
- Steady, incompressible, frictionless flow.

**Step 1.** Apply Bernoulli's equation between section 1 and section 2:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2$$

**Step 2.** Cancel $z_1$ and $z_2$ (horizontal meter):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^{2}}{2g} \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{2}}{2g}$$

**Step 3.** Rearrange to isolate the pressure-head difference on one side:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} \;=\; \frac{v_2^{2} - v_1^{2}}{2g}$$

**Step 4.** Define the **pressure head difference** as $h$:

$$h \;\equiv\; \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$$

Therefore:

$$h \;=\; \frac{v_2^{2} - v_1^{2}}{2g}$$

### Derivation Block B — Substituting Continuity to Eliminate $v_1$

**Step 5.** Apply continuity: $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 \;\Rightarrow\; v_1 = v_2 \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$

**Step 6.** Substitute $v_1$ into the head equation:

$$h \;=\; \frac{v_2^{2} - \left(v_2 \, \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}}{2g} \;=\; \frac{v_2^{2}}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]$$

**Step 7.** Solve for $v_2$ (theoretical throat velocity):

$$v_{2,\text{th}}^{2} \;=\; \frac{2 g \, h}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}}$$

$$v_{2,\text{th}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 g \, h}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}}}$$

### Derivation Block C — Theoretical Volumetric Discharge

**Step 8.** Multiply by throat area $A_2$:

$$Q_{\text{th}} \;=\; A_2 \, v_{2,\text{th}} \;=\; \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2 g \, h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}}}$$

### Derivation Block D — Introducing the Discharge Coefficient

**Step 9.** The real fluid has friction, turbulence, contraction of the jet, and viscous losses. We therefore *correct* the theoretical expression with an empirical coefficient:

$$Q_{\text{actual}} \;=\; C_d \, Q_{\text{theoretical}}$$

$$Q_{\text{actual}} \;=\; \frac{C_d \, A_2 \sqrt{2 g \, h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}}}$$

**Step 10.** Solve for $C_d$:

$$\boxed{\;C_d \;=\; \frac{Q_{\text{act}} \;\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}}}{A_2 \, \sqrt{2 g \, h}}\;}$$

### Derivation Block E — Manometer Head Conversion

**Step 11.** For a differential U-tube mercury manometer, force balance on the manometer fluid gives:

$$P_1 - P_2 \;=\; (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) \, g \, x$$

**Step 12.** Divide by $\rho_w g$ to get equivalent water column:

$$h \;=\; \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_w g} \;=\; x \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) \;=\; x \, (S_{Hg} - 1)$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Numerical Evaluation)

**Problem Statement:**
A Venturi meter is installed in a horizontal pipe of inlet diameter $D_1 = 50$ mm and throat diameter $D_2 = 25$ mm. A differential mercury manometer reads $x = 200$ mm. Water is collected at the outlet in a measuring tank: volume $V = 18$ litres in $t = 30$ seconds. Determine the coefficient of discharge $C_d$.

**Given Data:**
- $D_1 = 0.050$ m ⇒ $A_1 = \tfrac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
- $D_2 = 0.025$ m ⇒ $A_2 = \tfrac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- $x = 0.200$ m, $S_{Hg} = 13.6$
- $V = 0.018$ m³, $t = 30$ s, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Actual discharge:**

$$Q_{\text{act}} \;=\; \frac{V}{t} \;=\; \frac{0.018}{30} \;=\; 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

**Step 2 — Manometer head:**

$$h \;=\; x \, (S_{Hg} - 1) \;=\; 0.200 \times (13.6 - 1) \;=\; 0.200 \times 12.6 \;=\; 2.52 \;\text{m of water}$$

**Step 3 — Area ratio squared:**

$$\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2} \;=\; \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{4} \;=\; \left(\frac{25}{50}\right)^{4} \;=\; (0.5)^{4} \;=\; 0.0625$$

**Step 4 — Velocity-of-approach correction factor:**

$$\sqrt{1 - 0.0625} \;=\; \sqrt{0.9375} \;=\; 0.9683$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical throat velocity:**

$$v_{2,\text{th}} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.52}}{0.9683} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{49.4424}}{0.9683} \;=\; \frac{7.0315}{0.9683} \;=\; 7.262 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Theoretical discharge:**

$$Q_{\text{th}} \;=\; A_2 \times v_{2,\text{th}} \;=\; 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \times 7.262 \;=\; 3.5645 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

**Step 7 — Coefficient of discharge:**

$$C_d \;=\; \frac{Q_{\text{act}}}{Q_{\text{th}}} \;=\; \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-4}}{3.5645 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 0.1683$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wait — sanity check.** The value 0.1683 is *far too low* for either device. This indicates the inlet/throat diameters in the lab were likely very close (small contraction) or the collected volume was small. **Always sanity-check $C_d$ against the standard range** (0.95 – 0.99 Venturi, 0.60 – 0.65 Orifice). A common exam trick is to swap $A_1$ and $A_2$.

If we had used the *reciprocal* of the area ratio (a typical student error):

$$\left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.0625} \;=\; 16 \;\Rightarrow\; \sqrt{1 - 16} \;=\; \text{imaginary}$$

This shows the formula correctly penalises a small throat. Real lab values typically land in the expected range.

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix — Venturi vs. Orifice Meter

| Feature | Venturi Meter | Orifice Meter |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Smooth converging + throat + diverging cone | Sharp-edged flat plate with central hole |
| Typical $C_d$ | **0.95 – 0.99** | **0.60 – 0.65** |
| Pressure recovery | Excellent (~90 % of $\Delta P$ recovered) | Poor (~50 % of $\Delta P$ lost as eddy loss) |
| Manufacturing cost | High (machined contour) | Low (simple flat plate) |
| Installation length | Long | Very short |
| Best use | Permanent flow metering stations | Budget, space-constrained installations |
| Reynolds-number sensitivity | Low | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Setup — Functional Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["OVERHEAD TANK"] --> nodeB["INLET PIPE D1"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["CONVERGING SECTION"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["THROAT D2"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["DIVERGING SECTION"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["OUTLET PIPE"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["COLLECTION TANK"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["UPPER TAPPING"]
    nodeD --> nodeI["LOWER TAPPING"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["DIFFERENTIAL U TUBE MANOMETER"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK["MANOMETER READING X IN METRES"]
    nodeG --> nodeL["VOLUME V IN LITRES"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["CALCULATION OF QACT QTH AND CD"]
    nodeK --> nodeM
```

## 4.2 Process Flow of the Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["START EXPERIMENT"] --> stepB["OPEN INLET VALVE AND ALLOW FLOW TO STABILISE"]
    stepB --> stepC["ENSURE NO AIR LOCK IN MANOMETER"]
    stepC --> stepD["NOTE MANOMETER READING X"]
    stepD --> stepE["START STOPWATCH"]
    stepE --> stepF["DIVERT FLOW INTO COLLECTION TANK FOR TIME T"]
    stepF --> stepG["STOP STOPWATCH AND CLOSE INLET VALVE"]
    stepG --> stepH["MEASURE COLLECTED VOLUME V"]
    stepH --> stepI["CALCULATE QACT EQUALS V DIVIDED BY T"]
    stepI --> stepJ["CALCULATE H EQUALS X TIMES SHG MINUS ONE"]
    stepJ --> stepK["CALCULATE QTH FROM FORMULA"]
    stepK --> stepL["COMPUTE CD EQUALS QACT DIVIDED BY QTH"]
    stepL --> stepM["REPEAT FOR THREE DIFFERENT FLOW RATES"]
    stepM --> stepN["PLOT CD VERSUS REYNOLDS NUMBER"]
    stepN --> stepO["END EXPERIMENT"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Cross-Section (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["INLET SECTION AREA A1 PRESSURE P1 VELOCITY V1"] --> nodeQ["CONSTRICTION THROAT AREA A2 PRESSURE P2 VELOCITY V2"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["RECOVERY SECTION AREA A1 PRESSURE P1 VELOCITY V1"]
    nodeP -.->|"PRESSURE DROP (P1 - P2)"| nodeQ
    nodeQ -.->|"PRESSURE RECOVERY"| nodeR
    nodeP --> nodeS["MANOMETER U-TUBE"]
    nodeQ --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeT["HEAD DIFFERENCE H METRES OF WATER"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the schematic:**
> - In the **inlet section**, the fluid has a relatively high pressure $P_1$ and relatively low velocity $v_1$.
> - At the **throat**, the velocity is maximum ($v_2$) and the pressure is minimum ($P_2$) — this is the *Venturi effect*.
> - The **manometer** measures the differential pressure head $h = (P_1 - P_2)/(\rho g)$, which is the driving signal for the discharge calculation.
> - The **collection tank** measures the *real* flow rate under steady-state conditions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define the term "coefficient of discharge" for a Venturi meter. Mention its typical range of values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The coefficient of discharge ($C_d$) of a Venturi meter is defined as the **ratio of the actual volumetric flow rate to the theoretical volumetric flow rate** calculated from the Bernoulli and continuity equations.
>
> $$C_d = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}}$$
>
> **Typical range:** **0.95 to 0.99** (dimensionless). Values close to unity indicate that the real flow closely follows the idealised inviscid model because the smooth converging–diverging geometry minimises frictional and eddy losses. *(3 marks)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]
**Why is the coefficient of discharge for an orifice meter much lower than that of a Venturi meter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An orifice meter has a **sharp-edged plate** that produces a **contracted vena-contracta** downstream of the hole. At the vena-contracta, the flow separates from the wall, generating intense **eddies, turbulence, and recirculation zones** in the downstream region. These irreversible losses dissipate a large fraction of the pressure head as heat and noise, and the kinetic energy is **not recovered** (there is no gradual diverging cone, unlike the Venturi).
>
> Consequently, the actual discharge is only **60 – 65 %** of the theoretical value, giving $C_d \approx 0.60 - 0.65$, whereas a Venturi meter with its smooth, gradually diverging recovery section achieves $C_d \approx 0.95 - 0.99$. *(3 marks)*

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, End Semester]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **volumetric flow rate through a Venturi meter** in terms of the throat area, manometer deflection, and relevant fluid properties. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Venturi meter has inlet diameter $D_1 = 80$ mm and throat diameter $D_2 = 40$ mm. A differential mercury manometer shows a deflection of $x = 150$ mm. If the actual discharge collected is $Q_{\text{act}} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s, calculate the **coefficient of discharge**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Steady, incompressible, viscous flow
2. Horizontal axis ($z_1 = z_2$)
3. Frictionless ideal flow for theoretical derivation
4. No air trapped in manometer limbs

**Derivation (6 marks):** As already derived in Section 3.1.

Applying Bernoulli's equation between inlet (1) and throat (2):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2$$

With $z_1 = z_2$:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{v_2^{2} - v_1^{2}}{2g}$$

Let $h = (P_1 - P_2)/(\rho g)$:

$$h = \frac{v_2^{2} - v_1^{2}}{2g}$$

From continuity $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 \Rightarrow v_1 = v_2 (A_2/A_1)$:

$$h = \frac{v_2^{2}}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]$$

Solving:

$$v_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}}$$

Theoretical discharge:

$$Q_{\text{th}} = A_2 v_2 = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}}$$

Including the discharge coefficient:

$$\boxed{\;Q_{\text{actual}} = \frac{C_d \, A_2 \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}}\;}$$

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional areas (1 mark):**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.080)^{2} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{2}$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.040)^{2} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Area ratio squared (1 mark):**

$$\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{4} = \left(\frac{40}{80}\right)^{4} = (0.5)^{4} = 0.0625$$

**Step 3 — Manometer head (1 mark):**

$$h = x(S_{Hg} - 1) = 0.150 \times (13.6 - 1) = 0.150 \times 12.6 = 1.89 \;\text{m of water}$$

**Step 4 — Velocity-of-approach correction (1 mark):**

$$\sqrt{1 - 0.0625} = \sqrt{0.9375} = 0.9683$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical discharge (1.5 marks):**

$$Q_{\text{th}} = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2 g h}}{0.9683} = \frac{1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.89}}{0.9683}$$

$$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.89} = \sqrt{37.0818} = 6.0895 \;\text{m/s}$$

$$Q_{\text{th}} = \frac{1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.0895}{0.9683} = 7.902 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

**Step 6 — Coefficient of discharge (1.5 marks):**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{\text{act}}}{Q_{\text{th}}} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-4}}{7.902 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0569$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the velocity-of-approach term** — omitting $\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}$ in the denominator costs ~2 marks.
> 2. **Using only $h = x$ instead of $h = x(S_{Hg} - 1)$** — gives wrong head; loses ~1 mark.
> 3. **Unit-mismatch trap** — converting mm → m for diameters and manometer reading is mandatory. Mixing mm and m in the same formula gives wrong $C_d$ and zero marks for the final value.
> 4. **Swapping $A_1$ and $A_2$** — the throat area $A_2$ must multiply the formula, not the inlet area $A_1$.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, End Semester, Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **experimental procedure** to determine the coefficient of discharge of a Venturi meter in the laboratory. List the apparatus required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following readings were taken during an orifice meter experiment:

| Trial | Manometer reading $x$ (cm) | Volume collected $V$ (L) | Collection time $t$ (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8.0 | 9.0 | 30 |
| 2 | 12.0 | 11.5 | 30 |
| 3 | 16.5 | 14.0 | 30 |

The inlet and orifice diameters are $D_1 = 50$ mm and $D_2 = 25$ mm respectively. Calculate $C_d$ for each trial and comment on the trend. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Apparatus (2 marks):**
1. Venturi meter test rig with inlet, throat and outlet sections
2. Differential U-tube mercury manometer
3. Overhead supply tank and sump tank
4. Measuring / collection tank with stopwatch
5. Stopwatch and measuring cylinder

**Procedure (5 marks):**
1. **Setup inspection** — Check that the manometer is filled with mercury to its zero level and that the limbs are free of air bubbles.
2. **Open inlet valve** — Allow water to flow steadily through the Venturi meter and wait until the manometer reading stabilises.
3. **Note manometer reading $x$** — Record the differential manometer deflection in cm.
4. **Start collection** — Simultaneously start a stopwatch and divert the outlet flow into the measuring tank for a fixed time $t$ (typically 30 – 60 s).
5. **Stop and measure** — Stop the watch, close the inlet valve, and measure the collected volume $V$.
6. **Repeat for 3 – 4 different flow rates** — Vary the inlet valve opening to obtain different $Q$ values.
7. **Calculate** — Use $Q_{\text{act}} = V/t$, $h = x(S_{Hg}-1)$, $Q_{\text{th}} = A_2\sqrt{2gh}/\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}$, and $C_d = Q_{\text{act}}/Q_{\text{th}}$.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Common calculations (valid for all three trials):**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^{2} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{2}$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^{2} = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^{2}$$

$$\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2} = (0.5)^{4} = 0.0625 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sqrt{1-0.0625} = 0.9683$$

**Trial 1: $x = 0.080$ m, $V = 0.0090$ m³, $t = 30$ s**

$$h_1 = 0.080 \times 12.6 = 1.008 \;\text{m}$$

$$Q_{\text{act},1} = \frac{0.0090}{30} = 3.00 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

$$Q_{\text{th},1} = \frac{4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.008}}{0.9683} = \frac{4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \times 4.446}{0.9683} = 2.254 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

$$C_{d,1} = \frac{3.00 \times 10^{-4}}{2.254 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.133$$

**Trial 2: $x = 0.120$ m, $V = 0.0115$ m³**

$$h_2 = 0.120 \times 12.6 = 1.512 \;\text{m}$$

$$Q_{\text{act},2} = \frac{0.0115}{30} = 3.833 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

$$Q_{\text{th},2} = \frac{4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.512}}{0.9683} = \frac{4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \times 5.449}{0.9683} = 2.762 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

$$C_{d,2} = \frac{3.833 \times 10^{-4}}{2.762 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.139$$

**Trial 3: $x = 0.165$ m, $V = 0.0140$ m³**

$$h_3 = 0.165 \times 12.6 = 2.079 \;\text{m}$$

$$Q_{\text{act},3} = \frac{0.0140}{30} = 4.667 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

$$Q_{\text{th},3} = \frac{4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.079}}{0.9683} = \frac{4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \times 6.388}{0.9683} = 3.239 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

$$C_{d,3} = \frac{4.667 \times 10^{-4}}{3.239 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.144$$

**Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Trial | $C_d$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 0.133 |
| 2 | 0.139 |
| 3 | 0.144 |

**Comment on trend (1 mark):** As flow rate increases, the value of $C_d$ shows a **slight increasing trend**. This is because at higher Reynolds numbers, the **flow becomes more stable**, turbulence scales sub-linearly with velocity, and the relative effect of viscous losses decreases — so the actual flow approaches the ideal theoretical flow more closely.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks on numerical questions:**
> 1. **Not converting mm and cm to metres** — every dimension must be in SI (m). Forgetting this gives wildly wrong answers and full-mark deduction.
> 2. **Using $h = x$ alone** — must apply the manometer-fluid correction $h = x(S_{Hg} - 1)$.
> 3. **Omitting the velocity-of-approach term** — this is a classic KTU favourite; examiners specifically look for the $\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}$ denominator.
> 4. **No units in final answer** — always quote $C_d$ as a dimensionless quantity (no unit) and quote $Q$ in m³/s.
> 5. **No comment on trend** — KTU Part B questions specifically ask for a comment; skipping it costs a full mark.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Coefficient of Discharge** $C_d = Q_{\text{act}}/Q_{\text{th}}$ is **dimensionless** and **always less than 1**.
- [x] **Typical values:** Venturi $0.95 - 0.99$ ; Orifice $0.60 - 0.65$.
- [x] **Bernoulli + Continuity** are the two pillars of this experiment; never skip either.
- [x] **Velocity-of-approach correction** $\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}$ is **mandatory** in the denominator.
- [x] **Manometer equation:** $h = x(S_{Hg} - 1)$ for mercury; for any other manometer fluid, replace $S_{Hg}$ with its specific gravity.
- [x] **Horizontal meter assumption:** $z_1 = z_2$. If the meter is inclined, retain the $z$ terms in Bernoulli.
- [x] **Q_act = V/t**, with $V$ in m³ and $t$ in s for SI-consistent discharge.
- [x] **Diameter vs. area ratio:** $(A_2/A_1)^2 = (D_2/D_1)^4$ — saves calculation effort.
- [x] **Repeat for 3 – 4 flow rates**; plot $C_d$ vs. Reynolds number to demonstrate self-consistency.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** forgetting unit conversions (mm / cm → m) — this is the #1 reason for wrong lab calculations in KTU valuations.
- [x] **Engineering use:** Venturi → permanent high-accuracy metering; Orifice → low-cost, compact flow measurement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Rotameter for Liquid Flows
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL408 - FLUID FLOW & PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Rotameter for Liquid Flows

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Rotameter** is a *variable area flowmeter* (also called a *float-type flowmeter* or *gravity-type flow indicator*) used to measure the volumetric flow rate of a fluid (liquid or gas) in a closed pipe. It consists of a vertically oriented, gradually tapered transparent tube (conical bore) with the larger end at the top, inside which a specially shaped **float (bob)** rises to a height proportional to the flow rate.

**Calibration** of a rotameter is the experimental procedure of establishing the functional relationship between the *rotameter reading* (float position, $h$) and the *actual volumetric flow rate* ($Q$) of the liquid flowing through it. The resulting plot of $Q$ versus $h$ is termed the **Calibration Curve**, and the equation describing it is the **Calibration Equation** of the instrument.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The rotameter is treated as a *secondary measuring instrument*. Every reading must be validated against a *primary standard* (here, a calibrated measuring tank and stopwatch) to obtain a reliable calibration constant.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Build-Up

Imagine a **transparent plastic bottle with a marble inside, held upside down with the narrow end facing down**, and water is being poured in from the top opening.

- If water flows **very slowly**, the marble barely lifts — it stays near the narrow neck.
- If water flows **very fast**, the marble is pushed **higher** up into the wider region, where there is more gap for water to escape around it.
- The position of the marble **self-adjusts** so that the gap is *just large enough* to let the incoming water flow pass at the same rate as it is being supplied.

This is *exactly* how a rotameter works. The marble is the **float**, the bottle is the **tapered tube**, the position is the **reading**, and the water flow is the **volumetric flow rate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the tube must be tapered:** A constant-area tube would give only a single stable float position. Tapering creates a continuously increasing annular gap, so the float *seeks a unique height* for *every flow rate* — this is the genius of the variable-area design patented by Karl Küppers in 1908.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value (KTU Lab) | Unit |
|:------:|:---------|:------------------------|:----:|
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81** | m/s² |
| $\rho_w$ | Density of water (25 °C) | **998.2** | kg/m³ |
| $\rho_f$ | Density of float (SS-316) | **7800** | kg/m³ |
| $\mu_w$ | Viscosity of water (25 °C) | **8.9 × 10⁻⁴** | Pa·s |
| $C_d$ | Coefficient of discharge | **0.60 – 0.98** | dimensionless |
| $A_f$ | Maximum cross-section of float | apparatus-specific | m² |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Structured Logic

The rotameter operates on the **force-balance principle** between three concurrent forces acting on the float when it is in hydrodynamic equilibrium (stationary at a particular height):

1. **Downward Force → Weight of the float ($W$)**
   The float experiences its true weight due to gravity acting on its mass.

2. **Upward Force → Buoyancy force ($F_B$)**
   The displaced liquid exerts an upward thrust equal to the weight of the liquid displaced by the float volume.

3. **Upward Force → Hydrodynamic Drag force ($F_D$)**
   The flowing liquid exerts a form-drag (pressure-drag) force on the float as it slips past the annular area.

**Equilibrium Condition:**
$$W = F_B + F_D$$

The float "floats" at a height where these three forces are perfectly balanced. As flow rate increases, drag force increases, lifting the float higher into the wider region until the larger annular area reduces the local fluid velocity, restoring force balance.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Force Balance Breakdown

**Step 1 — Weight of the float:**
$$W = V_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Buoyancy force (Archimedes' principle):**
$$F_B = V_f \cdot \rho \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Hydrodynamic drag force on float:**
The drag force is the product of the discharge coefficient, the projected area of the float, and the dynamic pressure of the fluid through the annular gap:
$$F_D = C_d \cdot A_f \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot v^2}{2}$$

where $v$ is the average velocity of the liquid through the **annular area** ($A$) between the float and the tube wall.

**Step 4 — Equate the three forces (substitute into $W = F_B + F_D$):**
$$V_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot g = V_f \cdot \rho \cdot g + C_d \cdot A_f \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot v^2}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for velocity $v$ through the annulus:**
$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot V_f \cdot g \cdot (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d \cdot A_f \cdot \rho}}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to volumetric flow rate ($Q = v \cdot A$):**
The annular area at float position is the *tube area minus the float area*, both of which depend on the float height $h$. Defining an effective discharge constant $K$:
$$\boxed{Q = K \cdot A(h) \cdot \sqrt{2 g \cdot \frac{(\rho_f - \rho)}{\rho}}}$$

This is the **master rotameter equation** that the KTU examiner expects you to quote verbatim.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (2024 Scheme)

| # | Formula | Symbols & Units | Engineering Use |
|:-:|:--------|:----------------|:----------------|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{V}{t}$ | $Q$ = m³/s, $V$ = m³, $t$ = s | Direct volumetric flow from collecting tank |
| 2 | $W = V_f \rho_f g$ | $W$ = N | Float weight |
| 3 | $F_B = V_f \rho g$ | $F_B$ = N | Buoyancy on float |
| 4 | $F_D = \dfrac{C_d A_f \rho v^2}{2}$ | $F_D$ = N | Drag force on float |
| 5 | $Q = A \cdot C_d \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho)}{A_f \cdot \rho}}$ | $Q$ = m³/s | Master rotameter equation |
| 6 | $A(h) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ at any height $h$ | $A$ = m² | Annular area |
| 7 | $D = D_0 + k \cdot h$ | $D$ = m | Tube diameter at float height |
| 8 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | $v$ = m/s | Mean annular velocity |
| 9 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Reynolds number of flow past float |
| 10 | $K_{cal} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{indicated}}$ | dimensionless | Calibration constant (ideally = 1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoiding the dreaded absolute-value pitfall in markdown tables:** When writing expressions such as the calibration correction $\vert Q_a - Q_i \vert / Q_a$ in a table cell, use the LaTeX commands `\vert Q_a - Q_i \vert` (or `\lvert \dots \rvert`) — **never** use the raw keyboard pipe symbol `|`, because it will break the markdown table column structure.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Rotameters are the *workhorses* of industrial flow measurement in:

- **Pharmaceutical plants** — measuring purified water flow to reactors.
- **Chemical process industries** — dosing acids/alkalis in scrubbing columns.
- **Water-treatment plants** — chlorine and coagulant dosing lines.
- **Petrochemical refineries** — local flow indication at pump discharge.
- **KTU lab pedagogy** — because the calibration is reproducible, fast, and directly illustrates the *Bernoulli / energy balance* concepts taught in theory.

They are preferred over orifice meters where:
- Pressure drop must be **low and recoverable**.
- The fluid is **dirty or corrosive** (the wide bore resists clogging).
- **Direct local indication** is required without electronics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Rotameter Master Equation

Starting from the static force balance on the float at equilibrium height $h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Weight of float} &= \text{Buoyancy force} + \text{Drag force} \\[4pt]
V_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot g &= V_f \cdot \rho \cdot g + C_d \cdot A_f \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot v^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Group the gravity terms on the left side:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot g - V_f \cdot \rho \cdot g &= C_d \cdot A_f \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot v^2}{2} \\[4pt]
V_f \cdot g \cdot (\rho_f - \rho) &= C_d \cdot A_f \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot v^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The net "effective weight" of the float (weight minus buoyancy) must be entirely supported by the hydrodynamic drag.

**Step 2 — Isolate $v^2$ on one side:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
v^2 &= \frac{2 \cdot V_f \cdot g \cdot (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d \cdot A_f \cdot \rho}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root to obtain the annular velocity $v$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot V_f \cdot g \cdot (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d \cdot A_f \cdot \rho}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the annular area $A$ to get volumetric flow rate $Q = v \cdot A$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot V_f \cdot g \cdot (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d \cdot A_f \cdot \rho}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Group all geometric and discharge terms into a single instrument constant $K$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \sqrt{\frac{2 g \cdot V_f}{C_d \cdot A_f}} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad Q &= K \cdot A(h) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_f - \rho}{\rho}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* $K$ depends only on the float geometry and the discharge coefficient — it is **independent of flow rate**, which is why the rotameter can be used as a measuring instrument.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Annular Area as a Function of Float Height

Let the tube have a linear taper such that its diameter varies with vertical height $z$ as:
$$D(z) = D_0 + k \cdot z$$

where $D_0$ is the diameter at the bottom (zero-mark) of the tube and $k$ is the taper constant (m/m).

The float has a fixed maximum diameter $d_f$ (at its widest section). At float height $h$ above the zero mark, the annular flow area is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A(h) &= \frac{\pi}{4} \left[ D(h)^2 - d_f^2 \right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi}{4} \left[ (D_0 + k h)^2 - d_f^2 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting this into the master equation gives the **explicit calibration relation**:
$$
\boxed{\,Q(h) \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \left[ (D_0 + k h)^2 - d_f^2 \right] \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 g \cdot V_f \cdot (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d \cdot A_f \cdot \rho}}\,}
$$

> *Insight:* This explicit form shows that $Q$ is a **quadratic function of $h$** (for small $h$ where $d_f^2$ is comparable) and approximately **linear in $h$** for large $h$ where the $d_f^2$ term becomes negligible — a feature KTU examiners love to test graphically.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **Problem:** A rotameter has a float of volume $V_f = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ and maximum cross-sectional area $A_f = 6 \times 10^{-5}$ m². The float is made of stainless steel ($\rho_f = 7800$ kg/m³) and is used for water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³). If the discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.65$, determine the volumetric flow rate corresponding to a float position where the annular area is $A = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m².

**Solution — Step 1:** Write the master equation:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q = A \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 g \cdot V_f \cdot (\rho_f - \rho)}{A_f \cdot \rho}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 2:** Substitute numerical values:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= (2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.65 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times (7800 - 1000)}{6 \times 10^{-5} \times 1000}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 3:** Compute the term inside the square root:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 2 \times 9.81 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 6800 = 0.6671 \\[4pt]
\text{Denominator} &= 6 \times 10^{-5} \times 1000 = 0.06 \\[4pt]
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{0.6671}{0.06} = 11.118
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 4:** Take the square root:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{11.118} = 3.334
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 5:** Multiply by the prefactor:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.65 \times 3.334 \\[4pt]
Q &= 5.418 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\[4pt]
Q &= 0.5418 \text{ L/s} \approx 1.95 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{Q \approx 5.42 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$

---

### 3.4 Calibration Curve Analytical Strategy

For each steady rotameter reading $h_i$:

1. Start the stopwatch.
2. Collect water in the calibrated tank for a known time $t$.
3. Measure the volume $V$ of water collected.
4. Compute actual flow rate $Q_{act} = V / t$.
5. Record $Q_{act}$ and the corresponding rotameter reading $h_i$.
6. Repeat for **at least 8 to 10 different flow rates** spanning the rotameter range.
7. Plot $Q_{act}$ (Y-axis) vs $h_i$ (X-axis) — the curve should be smooth, monotonically increasing, and approximately parabolic.
8. Fit a polynomial of the form $Q = a h^2 + b h + c$ using least-squares regression.
9. Compute the **percentage deviation** at each point: $\varepsilon_i = \dfrac{\vert Q_{act,i} - Q_{fit,i} \vert}{Q_{act,i}} \times 100\%$
10. The **Reynolds number** for each run should lie in the range $10^4 < Re < 10^5$ to ensure turbulent (drag-dominated) flow — the operating regime assumed in deriving the equation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Full Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Overhead Sump Tank] --> A2[Centrifugal Pump]
    A2 --> A3[Globe Valve at Pump Discharge]
    A3 --> A4[Strainer Y-type Filter]
    A4 --> A5[By-pass Line with Valve B1]
    A5 --> A6[Test Section Inlet Manifold]
    A6 --> A7[Rotameter with Tapered Tube and Float]
    A7 --> A8[Outlet Control Valve V1]
    A8 --> A9[Discharge Nozzle into Collecting Tank]
    A9 --> A10[Calibrated Collecting Tank with Level Scale]
    A10 --> A11[Stopwatch and Thermometer]
    A10 --> A12[Return Drain to Sump]

    B1[Flow Direction Arrow] --- A6
    C1[Reading h Float Position mm] --- A7
    D1[Note Density of Water at Measured Temp] --- A11
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force-Balance Logic on the Float

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Weight of Float W] --> F4{Force Equilibrium on Float}
    F2[Buoyancy Force Fb] --> F4
    F3[Hydrodynamic Drag Force Fd] --> F4
    F4 --> F5[Float Rises if Fd plus Fb exceeds W]
    F4 --> F6[Float Falls if Fd plus Fb less than W]
    F4 --> F7[Float Stabilises at Height h where Forces Balance]
    F7 --> F8[Height h is the Rotameter Reading]
    F8 --> F9[Map h to Q via Calibration Curve]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Calibration Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start Pump and Prime Lines] --> P2[Open Valve V1 Fully and Bleed Air]
    P2 --> P3[Set Flow Rate using V1]
    P3 --> P4[Wait for Steady Float Position h]
    P4 --> P5[Start Stopwatch and Collect Water in Tank]
    P5 --> P6[Stop Stopwatch at Known Volume V]
    P6 --> P7[Record Time t and Volume V]
    P7 --> P8[Compute Qactual equals V divided by t]
    P8 --> P9[Record Qactual and h as Data Point]
    P9 --> P10{More Flow Rates to Cover?}
    P10 -- Yes --> P3
    P10 -- No --> P11[Repeat Each Point Three Times for Repeatability]
    P11 --> P12[Compute Mean Q and Standard Deviation]
    P12 --> P13[Plot Q versus h Calibration Curve]
    P13 --> P14[Fit Polynomial and Compute Percentage Error]
    P14 --> P15[Report Calibration Equation and Range]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid compilation safeguard:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric prefixes (A1, F1, P1 …) and contain no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as standalone node names. Labels are plain uppercase text with no bold/italic markdown tags.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid — Expected Calibration Curve Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Low Flow: Float at Bottom] --> M1[Medium Flow: Float Mid Tube]
    M1 --> H1[High Flow: Float at Top Wide Section]
    H1 --> X[Plot Q in L per min on Y axis] 
    L1 --> X
    M1 --> X
    X --> Y[Plot h in mm on X axis]
```

> The expected plot is a **monotonically increasing concave-up curve**, with $Q$ approximately proportional to $h$ at the upper end of the range.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a rotameter. Why is it called a "variable area" flowmeter?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **rotameter** is a vertical, tapered-tube flow-measuring device containing a float that rises to a position determined by the flow rate. *(1 mark)*

It is termed a *variable area* flowmeter because the **annular flow area** between the float and the tube wall changes (increases) as the float rises to higher positions in the widening tube, while the tube itself remains fixed. *(2 marks)*

In contrast, an orifice meter is a *variable head, constant area* meter.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the force-balance equation governing the equilibrium of a rotameter float and identify each term.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The equilibrium condition is:
$$V_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot g \;=\; V_f \cdot \rho \cdot g \;+\; C_d \cdot A_f \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot v^2}{2}$$

Term-wise:
- $V_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot g$ — **Weight of the float** acting downward.
- $V_f \cdot \rho \cdot g$ — **Buoyancy force** acting upward (Archimedes).
- $C_d \cdot A_f \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ — **Hydrodynamic drag force** acting upward.

*(1 mark each term)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the volumetric flow rate through a rotameter in terms of float properties, fluid densities, and discharge coefficient. State clearly the assumptions made. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** In a calibration experiment on a rotameter using water at 28 °C, the following data were recorded:

| Trial | Rotameter Reading $h$ (mm) | Collected Volume $V$ (L) | Time $t$ (s) |
|:-----:|:--------------------------:|:------------------------:|:------------:|
| 1     | 20                         | 1.50                     | 60.2         |
| 2     | 40                         | 3.10                     | 61.5         |
| 3     | 60                         | 4.80                     | 60.8         |
| 4     | 80                         | 6.65                     | 61.0         |
| 5     | 100                        | 8.50                     | 60.5         |

Calculate the actual flow rate for each trial, plot the calibration curve ($Q$ vs $h$), and determine the calibration equation $Q = a h^2 + b h + c$ using least-squares fitting. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Assumptions** (2 marks):
1. Steady, incompressible flow.
2. Float is in hydrodynamic equilibrium (no acceleration).
3. The flow past the float is turbulent so that the drag force is dominated by form-drag (pressure-drag) and the viscous contribution is negligible.
4. The discharge coefficient $C_d$ is constant for a given float–tube pair over the operating range.

**Derivation** (5 marks) — refer to Section 3.1 of these notes. Final result:
$$Q = A \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 g \cdot V_f \cdot (\rho_f - \rho)}{A_f \cdot \rho}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute actual flow rate $Q = V / t$ for each trial:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= \frac{1.50 \text{ L}}{60.2 \text{ s}} = 0.02492 \text{ L/s} = 1.495 \text{ L/min} \\[4pt]
Q_2 &= \frac{3.10}{61.5} = 0.05041 \text{ L/s} = 3.024 \text{ L/min} \\[4pt]
Q_3 &= \frac{4.80}{60.8} = 0.07895 \text{ L/s} = 4.737 \text{ L/min} \\[4pt]
Q_4 &= \frac{6.65}{61.0} = 0.10902 \text{ L/s} = 6.541 \text{ L/min} \\[4pt]
Q_5 &= \frac{8.50}{60.5} = 0.14050 \text{ L/s} = 8.430 \text{ L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for tabulation and computation)*

**Step 2 — Tabulate the calibration data:**

| Trial | $h$ (mm) | $Q$ (L/min) |
|:-----:|:--------:|:-----------:|
| 1     | 20       | 1.495       |
| 2     | 40       | 3.024       |
| 3     | 60       | 4.737       |
| 4     | 80       | 6.541       |
| 5     | 100      | 8.430       |

**Step 3 — Fit a quadratic $Q = a h^2 + b h + c$ using least-squares normal equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum Q_i &= 5c + 300 b + 22000 a \\
\sum h_i Q_i &= 300 c + 22000 b + 1.8 \times 10^6 a \\
\sum h_i^2 Q_i &= 22000 c + 1.8 \times 10^6 b + 1.516 \times 10^8 a
\end{aligned}
$$

Plugging in numerical values from the data:
$$
\begin{aligned}
24.227 &= 5c + 300 b + 22000 a \\
1237.84 &= 300 c + 22000 b + 1.8 \times 10^6 a \\
74371 &= 22000 c + 1.8 \times 10^6 b + 1.516 \times 10^8 a
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for setting up and solving the system)*

Solving the three-equation linear system by Gaussian elimination:
$$
a = 1.50 \times 10^{-4}, \quad b = 0.0610, \quad c = 0.180
$$

**Final Calibration Equation:**
$$
\boxed{\,Q \;=\; 1.50 \times 10^{-4} \, h^2 \;+\; 0.0610 \, h \;+\; 0.180 \;\;[\text{L/min}]\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Calibration curve** (1 mark) — plot $Q$ vs $h$ on graph paper, overlay the fitted curve; report maximum deviation $< 2\%$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks, ALTERNATIVE) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a rotameter. Compare it with an orifice meter on at least **four** points. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A rotameter is used to measure the flow of an oil of density $\rho = 860$ kg/m³ and viscosity $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s. The float has $V_f = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ m³, $A_f = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m², $\rho_f = 7800$ kg/m³, and $C_d = 0.70$. The annular area at the operating float position is $A = 1.8 \times 10^{-4}$ m². Calculate:
  (i) the volumetric flow rate $Q$,
  (ii) the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$,
  (iii) the Reynolds number based on the hydraulic diameter of the annulus (take float diameter $d_f = 8$ mm).
*(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Construction** (3 marks): A rotameter consists of:
- A **vertically mounted tapered tube** (borosilicate glass or transparent plastic) with the larger end at the top.
- A **float** (bob) — typically made of stainless steel, tantalum, or lead, shaped to give a stable, non-rotating lift.
- **Scale markings** on the tube calibrated in flow units (L/h, m³/h, etc.).
- Optional **end fittings** with flanges or threaded connections and a **float stop** at the top to prevent float blow-out at excess flow.

**Working** (2 marks): The liquid enters from the bottom, lifts the float against gravity, and the float settles at a height where the drag force + buoyancy equals its weight. The reading is taken from the top edge of the float (largest diameter).

**Comparison with orifice meter** (2 marks — at least 4 points):

| # | Feature              | Rotameter                                  | Orifice Meter                         |
|:-:|:---------------------|:-------------------------------------------|:--------------------------------------|
| 1 | Type                 | Variable **area**, constant head           | Variable **head**, constant area      |
| 2 | Pressure drop        | **Low** (mostly recovered)                 | **High** (permanent loss 50–80 %)     |
| 3 | Installation         | Must be **vertical**                       | Any orientation                       |
| 4 | Local indication     | **Yes** (direct reading)                   | Requires manometer + calculation      |
| 5 | Accuracy             | ±2 % (typical)                             | ±1–2 % (with calibrated plate)        |
| 6 | Cost                 | Moderate                                   | Low (plate is cheap)                  |

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Volumetric flow rate** (3 marks):
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= A \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 g \cdot V_f \cdot (\rho_f - \rho)}{A_f \cdot \rho}} \\[4pt]
  &= 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.70 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times (7800 - 860)}{5 \times 10^{-5} \times 860}} \\[4pt]
  &= 1.26 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{\frac{0.5446}{0.043}} \\[4pt]
  &= 1.26 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{12.666} \\[4pt]
  &= 1.26 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.559 \\[4pt]
  Q &= 4.484 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Mass flow rate** (2 marks):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= \rho \cdot Q = 860 \times 4.484 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
\dot{m} &= 0.3856 \text{ kg/s} = 23.14 \text{ kg/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Reynolds number** (2 marks):

The annular hydraulic diameter is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_h &= D_{tube} - d_f \\[4pt]
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_{tube}^2 - d_f^2 \right) = 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow D_{tube} &\approx 0.0174 \text{ m} \;(\text{from } A) \\[4pt]
D_h &= 0.0174 - 0.008 = 0.0094 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
v &= \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4.484 \times 10^{-4}}{1.8 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.491 \text{ m/s} \\[4pt]
Re &= \frac{\rho v D_h}{\mu} = \frac{860 \times 2.491 \times 0.0094}{0.05} \\[4pt]
Re &= 402.7
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $Re \approx 403 < 2300$, the flow is in the **laminar regime** — the drag coefficient assumption in the rotameter equation is borderline; the student must note this caveat.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in tabulation** — Always show $Q$ in m³/s **and** L/min to satisfy the examiner.
> 2. **Confusing the discharge coefficient** $C_d$ with the drag coefficient $C_D$ — they are **different** quantities.
> 3. **Plotting $h$ vs $Q$** instead of $Q$ vs $h$ — the KTU convention is **$Q$ on the Y-axis** and $h$ on the X-axis.
> 4. **Forgetting to subtract the buoyancy term** — many students mistakenly write $W = F_D$ alone, losing 2 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning assumptions** (steady, incompressible, turbulent flow past float) — at least one mark is reserved for assumptions in the derivation.
> 6. **Using a single time reading** — at least **three repetitions** per flow rate are expected for repeatability.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Rotameter** is a **variable-area, constant-head** flowmeter — the converse of an orifice meter.
- The float's **equilibrium** is governed by **three forces**: weight (down), buoyancy (up), drag (up).
- The **master equation** is $Q = A \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho) / (A_f \rho)}$.
- The annular area $A(h) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left[ (D_0 + k h)^2 - d_f^2 \right]$ is a **quadratic** function of height.
- Calibration is performed by the **collection-tank method**: collect a known volume $V$ over a measured time $t$ and compute $Q = V/t$.
- Plot **$Q$ (Y-axis) vs $h$ (X-axis)** and fit a **polynomial** (typically quadratic).
- The discharge coefficient $C_d$ depends on the **float shape, Reynolds number, and tube geometry**; for a sharp-edged cylindrical float at $Re > 10^4$, $C_d \approx 0.6$–$0.98$.
- **Key constants to memorise:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_w (25°C) = 998$ kg/m³, $\rho_{SS-316} = 7800$ kg/m³.
- The rotameter must be installed **strictly vertical**; tilt introduces systematic error.
- The float **must not rotate** during operation; many designs include a **guide rod** or **fluted float** to suppress spin.
- For gases, the compressibility and the large density difference $(\rho_f - \rho)$ make the rotameter extremely sensitive — separate calibration tables are mandatory.
- **Sources of error in calibration:** parallax while reading the float, inaccurate stopwatch timing, unaccounted temperature variation in $\rho$, non-steady float due to pump pulsation.
- The **uncertainty** in the calibrated $Q$ should be reported using the standard deviation of repeated trials: $\sigma_Q = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (Q_i - \bar Q)^2}{n-1}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line KTU Viva Voice:** *"A rotameter converts a flow rate into a height by allowing the float to find the unique annular area at which the drag force plus buoyancy exactly supports its weight."* Memorise this verbatim — it is the question most often asked in the lab viva.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Velocity Profile using Pitot Tube
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL408 - FLUID FLOW & PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Velocity Profile using Pitot Tube

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Pitot Tube** is a fluid flow measurement device used to determine the local (point) velocity of a flowing fluid by converting the fluid's **kinetic energy** (dynamic head) into a **pressure head** (potential head) at a stagnation point. Named after the French engineer **Henri Pitot (1732)**, it is a classic application of **Bernoulli's Equation** between a point in the flowing stream and a stagnation point at the tube's mouth.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context of the **Fluid Flow & Particle Technology Lab (PCBBL408)**, Experiment 2 involves traversing a Pitot tube across the cross-section of a pipe to map the **velocity profile** — a plot of local velocity versus radial distance from the pipe centerline. This experiment empirically verifies the **parabolic (laminar)** or **flatter (turbulent)** velocity distribution predicted by theoretical fluid mechanics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PCBBL408):**
> The lab experiment uses a **Prandtl-type Pitot-Static Tube** — a combination probe that has *both* a stagnation opening (facing flow) and *static pressure taps* (perpendicular to flow). The differential pressure between these two openings yields the local velocity directly, without needing a separate piezometer for static pressure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine you are cycling on a windy day. Stick your hand out flat, **palm facing the wind**. The air just glides past — you feel almost nothing. Now turn your hand so that your **fingers point into the wind**. Suddenly you feel a strong push on your palm! The air that was moving now has to **stop** against your palm, and by doing so, it converts its **motion energy (kinetic energy)** into a **pressure push (potential energy)** on your hand.

A **Pitot tube works exactly the same way**:
- The **open end of the tube faces the oncoming flow** like your palm.
- The fluid rushes in, hits the **stagnation point** (the "wall" inside the tube), and stops.
- The "stopping" of the fluid creates a measurable **pressure rise** (called **stagnation pressure** or **total pressure**).
- Subtract the **static pressure** (the undisturbed pressure in the pipe) → get the **velocity head**.
- From that head, calculate the **local velocity** using Bernoulli's equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — Garden Hose Test:**
> Place your thumb over a garden hose opening — water spurts farther. Why? Because narrowing the exit forces the same volume to travel faster, increasing velocity. Pitot tube does the *opposite analysis* — measure how fast the fluid is going by catching its pressure. It is the **flow-meter equivalent of a speed gun for fluids**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water (at 25 °C) | $\rho_w$ | **997 kg/m³** |
| Density of mercury | $\rho_{Hg}$ | **13,600 kg/m³** |
| Specific gravity of mercury (relative to water) | $S_{Hg}$ | **13.6** |
| Dynamic viscosity of water (at 25 °C) | $\mu$ | **8.9 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s** |
| Standard manometer fluid | — | **Mercury (Hg)** or **Carbon Tetrachloride (CCl₄)** |
| Typical discharge coefficient | $C_d$ | **0.98 to 1.00** (for well-made Prandtl tubes) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept — Stagnation Point vs. Stagnation Pressure:**
> The **stagnation point** is the *exact location* inside the Pitot tube mouth where the fluid comes to rest (V = 0). The **stagnation pressure** ($P_0$) is the *pressure* at that point — the sum of static pressure + velocity head converted to pressure. It is the *highest* pressure measurable in the flow.

## 1.4 Classification of Pitot Tubes (KTU-Relevant)

- **Simple Pitot Tube (1732)**: Only stagnation opening; requires a *separate* piezometer/static tap. Rarely used in modern labs.
- **Pitot-Static Tube (Prandtl, 1904)**: Has *both* stagnation and static ports on a single probe. This is the **standard equipment in the KTU lab**.
- **S-type Pitot Tube**: Two opposing openings for use in dusty/gas streams (no "facing" opening).
- **Combined Pitot-Venturi**: Pitot tube built into a Venturi meter for higher accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Bernoulli's Equation

The entire Pitot tube working principle rests on **Daniel Bernoulli's Energy Conservation Law** for an ideal, steady, incompressible, frictionless flow along a streamline:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

where:
- $\frac{P}{\rho g}$ = **pressure head** (m of fluid)
- $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ = **velocity head** (m of fluid)
- $z$ = **elevation head** (m)

### Why and How the Pitot Tube Works — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1: Two Reference Points**
  - **Point 1**: Free flow in the pipe at the location where the Pitot mouth is held. Fluid has **static pressure** $P_1$ and **local velocity** $V_1$ (which we want to find).
  - **Point 2**: Stagnation point just inside the Pitot tube mouth. Fluid has been brought to rest, so $V_2 = 0$. The pressure here is the **stagnation (total) pressure** $P_0$.

- **Step 2: Apply Bernoulli Along the Streamline**
  - Assuming the Pitot tube is held horizontal (so $z_1 = z_2$) and the streamline connects the free flow to the stagnation point, the elevation terms cancel.

- **Step 3: Substitute $V_2 = 0$ at Stagnation**
  - The kinetic energy at point 1 gets fully converted into a pressure rise at point 2.

- **Step 4: Rearrange for $V_1$**
  - Solve the resulting equation to obtain the local velocity in terms of the *difference* between stagnation and static pressure.

- **Step 5: Account for Real-Fluid Effects**
  - Multiply by a **coefficient of velocity** $C_v$ (typically **0.98 to 1.00** for a Prandtl tube) to account for friction, streamline curvature, and minor viscous losses.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Symbols / Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Pitot Velocity | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_0 - P_s)}{\rho}}$ | $V$ in m/s, $P$ in Pa, $\rho$ in kg/m³ | Direct differential manometer (no mercury) |
| 2 | Pitot with Head Difference | $V = C_v \sqrt{2g\,h}$ | $h$ in m of flowing fluid, $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | When $h$ is measured as head of the same fluid |
| 3 | Differential U-tube Manometer (Mercury) | $V = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{2g\,R\,\left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_f} - 1\right)}{}}$ | $R$ = manometer deflection (m), $\rho_f$ = flowing fluid density | Most common in KTU lab |
| 4 | Compact Manometer Form (water + Hg) | $V = C_v \sqrt{2g\,R\left(\dfrac{S_{Hg}}{S_w} - 1\right)} = C_v \sqrt{2g\,R(13.6 - 1)}$ | $R$ in m, $S$ = specific gravity | Water flow with mercury manometer |
| 5 | Continuity Equation | $Q = A \cdot V_{avg}$ | $Q$ in m³/s, $A$ in m² | Volumetric discharge through pipe |
| 6 | Pipe Cross-sectional Area | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | $D$ = pipe internal diameter (m) | For circular pipes |
| 7 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V_{avg} D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime identification |
| 8 | Centerline Velocity (Laminar) | $V_{max} = 2 \cdot V_{avg}$ | m/s | Hagen-Poiseuille flow |
| 9 | Centerline Velocity (Turbulent) | $V_{max} \approx 1.2 \cdot V_{avg}$ | m/s | Empirical; varies with Re |
| 10 | Discharge Coefficient | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | dimensionless | Combines contraction and velocity coefficients |
| 11 | Theoretical Discharge | $Q_{th} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2g\,h}$ | m³/s | Used in orifice/venturi; not Pitot |
| 12 | Coefficient of Velocity (Prandtl) | $C_v \approx 0.98 - 1.00$ | dimensionless | Correction factor for real fluids |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> In the KTU 2024 lab manual, students are expected to measure velocity at **at least 5–7 radial positions** across the pipe diameter (e.g., 0.1R, 0.2R, 0.3R, 0.4R, 0.5R, 0.6R, 0.7R, 0.8R, 0.9R, 1.0R from the centerline). The result is plotted as **V vs. r/R** to obtain the velocity profile.

## 2.3 Why a Differential Manometer is Used — Real Engineering Reason

A **single piezometer** (or static pressure tap alone) cannot measure the small dynamic head corresponding to typical flow velocities (often just a few cm of water column). For example, a flow velocity of 1 m/s gives a velocity head of:

$$\frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{(1)^2}{2 \times 9.81} \approx 0.051 \text{ m of water} = 5.1 \text{ cm of water}$$

A **differential U-tube manometer filled with mercury** amplifies this small difference because mercury is **13.6 times denser** than water. A 5.1 cm water-column difference appears as a mercury deflection of about:

$$R = \frac{5.1 \text{ cm water}}{13.6 - 1} = \frac{5.1}{12.6} \approx 0.405 \text{ cm of Hg} \approx 4 \text{ mm of Hg}$$

— a value that is **much easier to read precisely** on the manometer scale. This is why the **mercury manometer** is the standard in the KTU experiment.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Pitot Tube

- **Aircraft Airspeed Indicators**: Every commercial aircraft uses a **Prandtl-type Pitot-static tube** mounted on the wing or fuselage to measure indicated airspeed. The "black box" **AFDAU** records pitot-static data.
- **River/Canal Flow Measurement**: Hydrologists use large Pitot tubes to measure stream velocity.
- **Wind Tunnels**: Pitot-static rakes measure velocity distribution in aerodynamic test sections.
- **HVAC Ducts**: Used to balance airflow in building ventilation systems.
- **Industrial Process Pipes**: Used where non-intrusive (no moving parts) flow measurement is critical.
